claims.claim: As the state of charge (SOC) of the battery increases, the lower the temperature at which thermal runaway occurs, and the more badly the structural damage of the electrode material after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: it was found that as the state of charge (SOC) of the battery increases, the lower the temperature at which thermal runaway occurs, and the more badly the structural damage of the electrode material after thermal runaway.

claims.claim: For NMC and LCO batteries, the types of toxic substances in thermal runaway emissions increase gradually with the increase in SOC, while for LFP batteries, most types of toxic substances were detected from 30% SOC.
claims.quote: the types of toxic substances increase gradually with the increase in the SOC, while as for batteries using LiFePO4 as the cathode material, most types of toxic substances were detected from 30% SOC.

claims.claim: LIB thermal runaway is divided into three stages: self-heating stage (50–140°C), thermal runaway stage (140–850°C), and thermal runaway termination stage (above 850°C).
claims.quote: the thermal runaway of LIB is divided into three stages [44], namely, self-heating stage (50~140 °C), thermal runaway stage (140~850 °C) and thermal runaway termination stage (above 850 °C).

claims.claim: Among the three battery types (NMC, LCO, and LFP), LFP is the most thermally stable, and 30% SOC is considered by most researchers to be a safer SOC.
claims.quote: among the three types of batteries (NMC, LCO, and LFP), LFP is the most stable, while 30% SOC is considered by most researchers to be a safer SOC.

claims.claim: In the thermal runaway latency zone (before pressure relief valve rupture), the low internal temperature and hypoxic conditions favor generation of small-molecule incomplete reaction products such as CO.
claims.quote: In the thermal runaway latency zone, the internal temperature of LIB is low, the chemical reaction rate is slow, and it is easy to generate small-molecule incomplete reaction products such as CO due to the internal hypoxia.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of LFP batteries at 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC, the vast majority of carbon elements existed in the form of CO and CO2, causing a sharp increase in internal pressure that led to explosion because the 18650 packaging had no time to release pressure.
claims.quote: LFP with 0%, 50% and 100% SOC generated fewer large-molecule gaseous products after thermal runaway. The vast majority of carbon elements existed in the form of CO and CO2, resulting in a sharp increase in internal pressure of the battery. In addition, the 18650 battery packaging was relatively tight, and there was no time to release pressure. As a result, an explosion occurred.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway of NMC at 0% SOC, the cathode material retained a compact layered morphology and the electrode structure was not damaged, whereas at 30% SOC the SEI was damaged, the layered structure was torn, and metal particles were observed outside the broken cathode material.
claims.quote: After the NMC thermal runaway reaction at 0% SOC (Figure 8b), the cathode material still had a compact layered morphology. The morphology of 0% SOC NMC batteries was maintained during the thermal runaway process. In the sample after the NMC thermal runaway reaction at 30% SOC (Figure 8c), the SEI was damaged, the layered structure was damaged and torn, and the existence of metal particles could be observed outside the broken cathode material.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC NMC after thermal runaway, only fragments could be observed in the cathode sample and the carbon characteristic peak only was detectable by XRD, indicating the cathode was completely destroyed by the violent explosion.
claims.quote: After the thermal runaway reaction of NMC at 100% SOC (Figure 8e), only fragments could be observed in the cathode sample. The metal particles could not be found, and the external impurities were doped into the structure... 100% SOC NMC could observe the carbon characteristic peak only, which indicated that with the violent explosion, the cathode was completely destroyed and cannot be detected.

claims.claim: In the thermal runaway products of LCO at 30% SOC, characteristic peaks of Al2O3 were found at 2θ = 37.73°, 43.30°, 45.78°, and 66.76°, indicating that the cathode aluminum foil was oxidized at high temperature.
claims.quote: In the 30% SOC LCO (Figure 9c), the cathode aluminum foil was oxidized at high temperature, as the characteristic peak of Al2O3 (110), (113), (400), (440) appeared at 2θ = 37.73°, 43.30°, 45.78° and 66.76°.

claims.claim: Six very toxic substances were detected in LIB thermal runaway products: 2-propenal, methyl vinyl ketone, propanedinitrile, propanenitrile, 1,2-dimethyl-hydrazine, and thiocyanic acid ethyl ester.
claims.quote: six very toxic substances such as 2-propenal, methyl vinyl ketone, propanedinitrile, propanenitrile, 1,2-dimethyl-hydrazine and thiocyanic acid ethyl ester could be detected and analyzed.

claims.claim: Toxic substances were not detected in the thermal runaway reaction products of 0% SOC NMC, and the maximum battery surface temperature did not exceed 100°C during this reaction.
claims.quote: toxic substances were not detected in the thermal runaway reaction products of 0% SOC NMC... only a small amount of electrolyte leakage occurred during the reaction of 0% SOC NMC, and the maximum temperature of the battery surface did not exceed 100 °C.

claims.claim: Compared with other LIB types, LFP produced the fewest types of thermal runaway products, but two very toxic nitrogen-containing substances (1,2-dimethyl-hydrazine and thiocyanic acid ethyl ester) were detected, with most toxic products found at 30% SOC.
claims.quote: Compared with other types of LIBs, the least amount of types of thermal runaway products were detected by LFP (as shown in Table 5), but two types of very toxic substances containing nitrogen, such as 1,2-dimethyl-hydrazine and thiocyanic acid ethyl ester, were detected, and most of the toxic products were detected in LFP with 30% SOC.

claims.claim: Electrolyte solvents (EC, PC, DMC) reacting with lithium generate small-molecule hydrocarbon compounds, and propylene generated from the PC–lithium reaction is continuously oxidized to form acrolein (2-propenal) at high temperature.
claims.quote: Electrolyte solvents (such as EC, PC, DMC) entered the anode and reacted with the lithium, generating small-molecule hydrocarbon compounds... The propylene generated by the reaction between PC and lithium was continuously oxidized to form acrolein at high temperature.

claims.claim: The oxygen content in the test box decreases sharply to below 19% during the thermal runaway behavior zone, creating a risk of explosion.
claims.quote: the oxygen content in the test box decreases sharply to below 19%, and there is a risk of explosion, which is very dangerous.

claims.claim: Using different numbers of heating plates, heating at different locations, and using different heating rates can significantly alter the triggering time of thermal runaway, the highest temperature after thermal runaway, and the severity of battery thermal runaway, but these changes do not affect the generation and emission of toxic species.
claims.quote: we found that using different numbers of heating plates, heating at different locations, and using different heating rates can significantly alter the triggering time of thermal runaway, the highest temperature after thermal runaway, and the severity of the battery thermal runaway... we also found that this change does not affect the generation and emission of toxic species after thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) in lithium-ion batteries is defined as the phenomenon of uncontrollable rising battery temperature caused by exothermic chain reactions.
claims.quote: TR is the phenomenon of uncontrollable rising battery temperature caused by the exothermic chain reaction of LIBs

claims.claim: Mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuse are the three main factors leading to thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: the sources of such harm can be categorized into three main types, namely mechanical, electrical, and thermal factors. Manufacturing defects and battery aging can also result in TR

claims.claim: During overcharge, the heat produced by the exothermic reaction between deposited lithium and electrolyte was found to be greater than 43% of the total heat generated.
claims.quote: The heat produced by the exothermic reaction between the deposited lithium and electrolyte during the overcharge was found to be greater than 43%.

claims.claim: LFP (lithium iron phosphate) emerged as the safest cathode material due to its comparatively lower heat production rate compared to LCO, LMO, and other cathode materials.
claims.quote: lithium iron phosphate (LFP) emerged as the safest cathode material because of its comparatively lower heat production rate compared to other materials

claims.claim: LMO (lithium manganate) exhibits the highest initial decomposition temperature and most excellent stability among cathode materials studied.
claims.quote: the cathode decomposition reaction played a significant role in the generation of heat within the batteries, with lithium manganate (LMO) exhibiting the highest initial decomposition temperature and most excellent stability.

claims.claim: After low-temperature cyclic aging at 0°C, the initial temperature of self-generated heat in ternary LIBs was reduced from 90°C to 30°C, with a new heat generation peak appearing independent of SOC.
claims.quote: It was found that the initial temperature of the self-generated heat of the decaying battery was reduced from 90 °C to 30 °C. The rate of self-generated heat increased significantly in the temperature range of 30~100 °C and a new heat generation peak appeared, which was independent of SOC.

claims.claim: After 300 charge-discharge cycles, battery capacity decreases by approximately 10% (90% SOH) and series resistance increases by approximately 10%.
claims.quote: When the battery is cycled up to 300 times, the battery capacity decreases by about 10% (90% SOH) and the impedance (series resistance) increases by about 10%

claims.claim: Calendar aging at 55°C and 100% SOC causes the self-heating temperature and TR temperature of the battery to increase while the exothermal rate during TR decreases, indicating improved thermal safety after calendar aging.
claims.quote: Research shows that the self-heating temperature and TR temperature of the battery increase, while the exothermal rate during TR decreases. This demonstrates that the thermal safety of the battery improved after calendar aging.

claims.claim: Rapid charge and discharge during cycling leads to rupture of the SEI film, which promotes reaction between graphite and electrolyte and decreases the thermal stability of the battery.
claims.quote: Rapid charge and discharge of the battery during the cycle lead to rupture of the SEI film, which promotes the reaction between graphite and electrolyte, and then the thermal stability of the battery decreases

claims.claim: TR gas from LIBs mainly contains CO, CO2, H2, CH4, C2H4, C3H6, C3H8, C4H10, C4H8, and other olefins and alkanes, of which CO, CO2, and H2 generally account for more than 70%.
claims.quote: the production of TR gas of LIBs mainly contains CO, CO2, H2, CH4, C2H4, C3H6, C3H8, C4H10, C4H8, and other olefins and alkanes, of which CO, CO2, and H2 generally account for more than 70%

claims.claim: In a LiNixMnyCozO2 (NCM) power LIB with 20–80 Ah capacity, CO2 accounts for 36.56%, CO for 28.38%, H2 for 22.27%, C2H4 for 5.61%, CH4 for 5.26%, C2H6 for 0.99%, and C3H6 for 0.52% of the total exhaust fraction.
claims.quote: six gases, CO, CO2, H2, CH4, C2H4, and C3H6, account for about 99% of the total exhaust fraction, with CO2 accounting for 36.56%, CO for 28.38%, H2 for 22.27%, C2H4 for 5.61%, CH4 for 5.26%, C2H6 for 0.99%, and C3H6 for 0.52%

claims.claim: O2 reacts with the electrolyte to produce CO2 when O2 is sufficient; otherwise the reaction produces CO.
claims.quote: O2 reacts with the electrolyte to produce CO2 when O2 is sufficient. Otherwise, the reaction produces CO.

claims.claim: At temperatures of 260°C or more, a reaction between PVdF binder and lithium occurs, releasing H2.
claims.quote: Elevated temperatures of 260 °C or more may lead to a reaction between PVdF and Li, releasing H2 in the process

claims.claim: A comparable reaction between CMC binder and lithium that releases H2 may occur beyond 250°C.
claims.quote: Similarly, a comparable reaction between CMC and Li may occur beyond 250 °C.

claims.claim: The gas released by the NCA battery was up to 317 mmol during TR, while the gas released by the LFP battery was 61 mmol, and the NCA battery produced more CO and H2 than the LFP battery.
claims.quote: The gas released by the NCA battery was up to 317 mmol, while the gas released by the LFP battery was 61 mmol, and the NCA battery produced more CO and H2 than the LFP battery.

claims.claim: The total combustible gases generated during TR from NCM and LFP battery modules were 21.09 g and 4.17 g, respectively, with NCM module much higher than LFP.
claims.quote: the total amount of flammable gas released from the TR of NCM and LFP batteries was very different and the NCM module was much higher than that of the LFP module. The total combustible gases generated by the two were 21.09 g and 4.17 g, respectively.

claims.claim: The LFP battery released a significantly lower amount of oxygen during cathode decomposition compared to NCM, limiting electrolyte oxidation during TR and reducing heat generated.
claims.quote: Compared to the NCM battery, the LFP battery released a significantly lower amount of oxygen during cathode decomposition. This low amount of available oxygen limited the extent of electrolyte oxidation during TR, ultimately reducing the heat generated.

claims.claim: TR gas volume released by a battery at 50%, 100%, and 150% SOC was 0.8 L, 2.5 L, and 6.0 L, respectively, showing that the amount of TR gas released is proportional to SOC.
claims.quote: With the TR gas of the battery at 50%, 100%, and 150% SOC, the volume of the released gas was 0.8 L, 2.5 L, and 6.0 L, respectively.

claims.claim: The total amount of TR gas produced per battery capacity averaged 1.96 L per Ah of capacity.
claims.quote: the total amount of TR gas produced in the battery was closely related to the capacity of the battery, with an average of 1.96 L of gas occurring per Ah of capacity.

claims.claim: For NCA batteries with SOC greater than 50%, the volume fraction of CO accounted for 40–50% of the total TR gas volume.
claims.quote: when the SOC value of the nickel-cobalt aluminate (NCA) battery was greater than 50%, the volume fraction of CO accounted for 40–50% of the total volume.

claims.claim: For LFP batteries with SOC greater than 50%, the volume fraction of H2 was about 20–30% of the total TR gas volume.
claims.quote: For LFP batteries, H2 was the most important combustible component and the volume fraction of H2 also increased with the increase of the SOC value. When the SOC value was greater than 50%, the volume fraction of H2 was about 20–30% of the total volume.

claims.claim: For NCA and LFP batteries, the explosion range of NCA battery is about twice as large as that of LFP battery at 25% SOC, but both achieve their maximum explosion range at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: when the battery SOC is at 25%, the explosion range of an NCA battery is about twice as large as that of an LFP battery. But both achieve the maximum at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: CH4 and C2H4 show no apparent correlation with battery SOC, and their combined sum is less than 10% of the total TR gas volume.
claims.quote: There was no apparent correlation between other small molecules, including CH4 and C2H4, and the battery SOC value. The sum of the two was less than 10% of the total gas volume

claims.claim: After cyclic aging, the interval between gas release and ignition was reduced from 21 seconds at 0 cycles to only 1 second at 60 cycles for 18650-type LiNi0.5Co0.2Mn0.3O2 batteries.
claims.quote: the interval between gas release and ignition is 21 s for a cycle number of 0, and that time is reduced to only 1 s when the cycle number is increased to 60.

claims.claim: After cyclic aging, the reaction between the anode and electrolyte is advanced, resulting in increased gas production during TR tests.
claims.quote: According to their experimental results, the reaction between the anode and the electrolyte of the battery after aging was advanced, resulting in increased gas production during the TR test.

claims.claim: HF gas was detected in all fire tests of LIBs regardless of battery form factor (soft pack, cylindrical, or laptop battery pack), but the amount differed: 16 mg, 15 mg, and 7.3 mg of HF per gram of battery weight reduction, respectively.
claims.quote: HF gas was detected in all tests, whether a soft pack battery, a cylindrical battery, or a laptop battery pack, but the amount of gas produced differed. For every 1 g of weight reduction in the battery, 16 mg, 15 mg, and 7.3 mg of HF gas were produced by the soft pack, cylindrical, and Lenovo laptop battery packs, respectively.

claims.claim: The production of CO in LIB TR increases as SOC increases, because higher stored energy is more conducive to incomplete combustion.
claims.quote: For CO, the production of CO increases as the SOC increases. That is because, the more energy stored in the cell, the more conducive to incomplete combustion

claims.claim: Total HF gas production shows an opposite trend to CO versus SOC: HF increases with decreased SOC.
claims.quote: Interestingly, the trend observed in total HF gas differs from the expected trend observed in CO versus SOC and previous researchers have followed a similar opposite trend (increased HF with decreased SOC).

claims.claim: Using water as a fire-fighting agent resulted in higher maximum concentrations of toxic gas emissions including HF, CO, and H2, because water triggers reaction with PF5 which initiates carbonate electrolyte decomposition.
claims.quote: using water as an agent resulted in higher maximum concentrations of toxic gas emissions, including HF, CO, and H2. The primary source of HF is the thermal decomposition of LiPF6 and electrolyte solvents, which occurs at higher temperatures. The addition of water triggers a reaction with PF5, subsequently initiating carbonate electrolyte decomposition based on LiPF6.

claims.claim: Water application increases the rate of HF production but does not alter the overall total production of HF.
claims.quote: Despite increasing the rate of HF production, it is worth noting that the overall production of HF is not altered

claims.claim: Water mist with surfactants suppresses TR gas explosions by two mechanisms: cooling flames to prevent heat transfer to unburned gas, and diluting concentrations of combustible gases such as CO.
claims.quote: water mists with surfactants positively reduce the potential for explosions of TR gas. There are two distinct mechanisms by which water mist effectively suppresses explosions. Firstly, it provides a cooling effect on flames, effectively preventing heat transfer to unburned gas. Second, the mist dilutes the concentrations of combustible gases, such as CO, rendering them less reactive.

claims.claim: A FED or FEC value equal to one means that 50% of the people in a fire scenario will lose the ability to escape.
claims.quote: In this evaluation method, a FED or FEC value equal to one means that 50% of the people in this fire scenario will lose the ability to escape

claims.claim: HF contributes the most prominently to total gas toxicity level among TR gases, dominating over asphyxiating gases.
claims.quote: When comparing the hazards of asphyxiating and irritant gases, the irritant gas dominates the total gas toxicity level and HF contributes the most prominently.

claims.claim: Aging battery TR releases less heat resulting in low combustion efficiency, thereby generating more toxic gases, and the cumulative effect of asphyxiating gases in aging batteries reaches catastrophic concentration more quickly than in fresh batteries.
claims.quote: Aging battery TR is stronger and releases less heat, resulting in low combustion efficiency, and, then, generates more toxic gases. Based on the FED model, analyzing the final TR gas toxicity with different aging degrees, it was found that the cumulative effect of asphyxiating gases in aging batteries was higher than that in fresh batteries and more quickly reached the catastrophic concentration.

claims.claim: The lower explosion limit (LEL) of LIB TR gas at 100% SOC was calculated to be 6.22% and the upper explosion limit (UEL) was 38.4%.
claims.quote: The lower explosion limit (LEL) was 6.22% and the upper explosion limit (UEL) was 38.4% of LIB at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: The upper explosion limit (UEL) of LIB TR gas is more affected by SOC than the LEL and usually becomes larger as SOC increases, expanding the explosion-limit range.
claims.quote: The LEL values are less sensitive to changes in the battery SOC. On the contrary, the UEL of the gas is more affected by the SOC and it usually becomes larger as the SOC increases. It can be concluded that the explosion-limit range of the gas expands with the increase of SOC.

claims.claim: The LEL of TR gas follows the same trend as alkane content, while the UEL shares a similar trend with unsaturated hydrocarbon content; when unsaturated hydrocarbon content increases significantly, the gas explosion-limit range increases.
claims.quote: the LEL follows the same trend as the alkane content, while the UEL shares a similar trend with the unsaturated hydrocarbon content. Compared to saturated hydrocarbons, the stability of the double bond structure of unsaturated hydrocarbons is worse and its reaction activation energy is higher. When the content of unsaturated hydrocarbons in TR gas increases significantly, the gas explosion-limit range will increase.

claims.claim: The minimum ignition energy of a specific LIB TR gas formulation was measured to be 0.3 MJ, which is close to the minimum ignition energy of acetylene and propane.
claims.quote: measured the minimum ignition energy of the gas to be 0.3 MJ, which is closer to the minimum ignition energy of acetylene, propane, and other gases.

claims.claim: In a full-scale battery electric vehicle test, white smoke from TR batteries took only 7 seconds to induce a severe explosion that damaged the hood structure.
claims.quote: The smoke was released intermittently and it took only 7 s to induce a severe explosion. The fixed structure of the hood of the automobile was damaged by the shock wave.

claims.claim: TR propagation in a battery module can occur discretely when the first cell experiences overheating TR, potentially propagating to the whole battery pack with catastrophic consequences.
claims.quote: when the first cell in a LIB module experienced an overheating TR, it caused the TR of the cells in the module to propagate discretely. The consequences of a single cell's TR could be catastrophic when the TR is completely propagated to the whole battery pack.

claims.claim: A 6-mm-thick aerogel thermal insulation sandwich effectively blocked TR propagation in battery packs.
claims.quote: The TR propagation was effectively blocked when a 6-mm-thick aerogel thermal insulation sandwich was used.

claims.claim: Organic PCM (especially paraffin-based) is flammable and its latent heat of phase transition is limited, making it insufficient to fully absorb the heat released during TR; a battery module with PCM can enhance heat transfer and increase ignition risk of the failing cell.
claims.quote: PCM has a critical drawback; it is flammable, especially paraffin-based organic PCM. The latent heat of the phase transition is limited. Although it can do well to inhibit the cyclic heat generation of the battery, it is not easy to fully absorb the heat released during the TR. Zhi et al. investigated the effect of PCM on the propagation of TR. They found that the battery module with PCM rather enhances the heat transfer and the risk of ignition of the failing cell.

claims.claim: Adding a larger mass fraction of flame-retardant additives to composite PCM reduces its cooling performance but positively delays cell fire time and reduces heat release rate.
claims.quote: The cooling performance of CPCM was reduced when a larger mass fraction of flame-retardant additives was added but it positively delayed the cell fire time and reduced the heat release rate.

claims.claim: Inorganic PCM features non-flammability and high latent heat of phase change compared to organic PCM, but has defects including corrosiveness, supercooling, dehydration, leakage, and phase separation.
claims.quote: compared with organic PCM, inorganic PCM features nonflammability and high latent heat of phase change... Inorganic PCM (IPCM) is corrosive and has defects such as supercooling, dehydration, leakage, and phase separation during the phase-change process, which restricts its application in batteries.

claims.claim: Numerical simulation using the TCHS model showed that SAT/EG (sodium acetate trihydrate doped into expanded graphite) could completely suppress TR propagation in battery modules.
claims.quote: Numerical TR-simulation experiments using the numerical thermochemical heat-storage (TCHS) model were performed and the results showed that SAT/EG could completely suppress TR propagation

claims.claim: The IET and FLET evaluation methods for LIB TR gas toxicity have the disadvantage of not considering the time factor, unlike the FED and FEC models.
claims.quote: The disadvantage of using IET and FLET values to evaluate LIB TR gas compared to the FED and FEC models is that the time factor is not considered.

claims.claim: Both FED and FEC models have limitations including the assumption of no reaction between toxic gases, which restricts their application.
claims.quote: Both models have some assumptions, such as no reaction between toxic gases, which limits the application of these models.

claims.claim: Most experimental studies on LIB TR gas production use new batteries, and there is a significant lack of research on TR gas production from aged batteries.
claims.quote: In the study of the TR gas production of LIBs, most of the batteries used in the experiments are new batteries and there is a certain lack of research related to the TR gas production of aging batteries.

claims.claim: GC-MS analysis detected more than 100 distinct species of organic compounds during LIB combustion, which are potent irritants to human skin, eyes, and nasal passages.
claims.quote: The results obtained from GC-MS analysis demonstrate that more than 100 distinct species of organic compounds were detected during combustion. These organic products from combustion (COPs) have been identified as potent irritants to the human skin, eyes, and nasal passages.

claims.claim: Gas release signals provide an early warning of thermal runaway approximately 1585 seconds before fire ejection in NCM cells and approximately 987 seconds before fire ejection in LFP cells.
claims.quote: the most intense combustion event was observed after the safety venting of the NCM cell. Before this, only a minimal amount of gas was released, paired with a slight electrolyte leak after the rupture of the relief vent, roughly 1585 s before the fire ejection (blue line). This serves as an early indicator during TR. In the case of the LFP cell, this was about 987 s before the fire

claims.claim: For NCM cells, gas release signals precede voltage drop by approximately 1119 seconds (around 19 minutes), which is a longer lead time than the pressure rise signal.
claims.quote: the NCM cell exhibits a gas release signal approximately 1119 s (around 19 min) before the voltage drop. This is a notably longer lead time than the pressure rise signal.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway degree (TRD) is defined as the product of the molar quantity of gas produced during the TR process and the square root of the peak chamber temperature (TRD = ngas × T_max^0.5), and serves as a relative metric for gauging TR severity.
claims.quote: TRD is defined as: TRD = ngas T^0.5_max. Wherein ngas represents the molar quantity of gas produced during the TR process, calculated using the Ideal Gas Law. Tmax is the peak temperature of the chamber throughout the test. It is important to mention that TRD serves as a metric to gauge the severity of TR in various batteries under differing conditions; it is a relative value, not an absolute one.

claims.claim: Under 300 W heating, the NCM cell reached a peak chamber pressure of 0.98 MPa while the LFP cell reached only 0.46 MPa, with NCM cells also exhibiting higher peak surface temperatures (431 °C) compared to LFP cells (335.8 °C).
claims.quote: The chamber's peak pressure is 0.46 MPa for the LFP, significantly lower than the 0.98 MPa recorded for the NCM cell... After TR, the central battery surface temperature elevated to 335.8 °C for the LFP cell and 431 °C for the NCM cell.

claims.claim: The H2 content in NCM cell vent gas (~10%) is lower than in LFP cells (~20%), and because H2 has a significantly lower explosion threshold than CO, post-TR vent gas explosion risks are considerably higher for LFP cells compared to NCM cells.
claims.quote: the H2 content in the NCM cell (~10%) is lower than in the LFP cell (~20%). Given that H2 has a significantly lower explosion threshold than CO, post-TR vent gas explosion risks are considerably higher for LFP cells compared to NCM cells.

claims.claim: TRD values for NCM cells (~191–205) are higher than for LFP cells (~96–193) under equivalent trigger methods, indicating a heightened intensity of the TR process for NCM cells compared to LFP cells of the same capacity.
claims.quote: When subjected to identical trigger methods, the TRD for NCM cells surpasses that of LFP cells, given their equivalent capacities. This indicates a heightened intensity in the TR process for NCM cells compared to LFP cells.

claims.claim: TRD demonstrates good repeatability across parallel tests with a maximum relative deviation below 5%, supporting its use as a characteristic parameter for risk estimation in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Despite the variability observed during TR, the experimental TRD outcomes remain relatively consistent, suggesting that the repeatability can capture distinctions associated with different trigger methods, especially since the maximum RD is below 5%. Hence, TRD can be utilized as a defining metric for risk assessment in LIBs.

claims.claim: Heating at 5 °C min⁻¹ produces more severe TR consequences than 300 W plate heating because the distributed heating causes a widespread internal short circuit, whereas the 300 W plate warms only one side and produces a localized short circuit.
claims.quote: The heating at 5 °C min−1 provides more distributed heat to the entire cell, potentially causing a widespread internal short circuit almost immediately. In contrast, with the 300 W method, the heating plate warms only one side of the cell, potentially leading to a localized internal short circuit near the plate... Thus, the consequences of TR when heating at 5 °C min−1 are more severe than those induced by the 300 W approach.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is defined in this study as the point when the rate of cell surface temperature rise surpasses 1 °C s⁻¹.
claims.quote: In this study, TR is identified when the rate of cell surface temperature rise surpasses 1 °C s−1.

claims.claim: The combined CO + CO2 volume in NCM cells is approximately 45%, while in LFP cells it is approximately 25%, implying that the NCM cell has a heightened oxidizing capability compared to the LFP cell.
claims.quote: The combined CO + CO2 volume in NCM cells hovers around 45%, while in LFP cells it is about 25%. This implies that the NCM cell has a heightened oxidizing capability compared to the LFP cell.

claims.claim: Global shipment of LIB binders reached 82,000 tons in 2022, with an annual increase of 60.8%, and is estimated to exceed 500,000 tons by 2030.
claims.quote: Global shipment of LIB binders reached 82,000 tons in 2022, with an annual increase of 60.8 %. By 2030, the shipment is estimated to exceed 500,000 tons.

claims.claim: PVDF binder content in cathode materials is low (1–3 wt%), making its direct thermal change difficult to detect during pyrolysis studies.
claims.quote: Because the PVDF content in waste cathode was as low as 1–3 %, its direct thermal change was difficult to detect.

claims.claim: TG-FTIR-MS analysis identified HF, CH3F (fluoromethane), C2H2F2 (vinyl fluoride), and C6H3F3 (trifluorobenzene) as toxic gaseous fluoride products from PVDF pyrolysis.
claims.quote: the gaseous products could be identified as hydrogen fluoride (HF, m/z = 20), fluoromethane (CH2F+/CHF2+, m/z = 33, 51), acetaldehyde (CHOCH3, m/z = 44), vinyl fluoride (C2H2F2, m/z = 64), fluoroethane (C2H3F3⋅H+/C2H2F4, m/z = 85, 102), and trifluorobenzene (C6H3F3, m/z = 132).

claims.claim: PVDF pyrolysis kinetics follow the Avrami-Erofeev equation (random nucleation followed by random growth model) with an apparent activation energy of 287.12 kJ/mol and pre-exponential factor of 10^20.08.
claims.quote: the kinetic mechanism function of PVDF pyrolysis could be described as: G(α) = −ln(1 − α). This indicated that the PVDF pyrolysis followed the model of random nucleation followed by random growth (Avrami-Erofeev Equation)... the apparent activation energy (EF) was computed as 287.12 kJ/mol... the pre-exponential factor (AK) was computed as 10^20.08

claims.claim: DFT calculations show C-F bonds in PVDF require least energy to cleave (4.84–4.92 eV), followed by C-H bonds (5.00–5.47 eV), with C-C bonds being most stable (8.83–9.86 eV), establishing the cleavage priority order C-F ≪ C-H < C-C.
claims.quote: Bond dissociation energies (BDEs) further prioritized cleavage sites as presented in Fig. 5c. The results showed that C-F bonds (4.84–4.92 eV) required least energy, C-H bonds (5.00–5.47 eV) were intermediate, and C-C bonds (8.83–9.86 eV) were most stable. This energy hierarchy (C-F ≪ C-H < C-C) supported initial C-F scission.

claims.claim: Approximately 68 wt% of PVDF is pyrolyzed and transferred into the air as gaseous products while 32 wt% remains as solid carbon residue.
claims.quote: about 68 wt% of PVDF was pyrolyze and transferred into the air while 32 wt% of PVDF retained as the carbon residue.

claims.claim: Projected global gaseous fluoride emissions from PVDF pyrolysis in LIB recycling will increase from 3.10 kt in 2025 to 19.74 kt in 2040 under current recycling paradigms.
claims.quote: the emission of gaseous fluoride waste will increase from 3.10 kt in 2025–19.74 kt in 2040.

claims.claim: LCO cathode particles did not significantly alter PVDF pyrolysis pathways across PVDF:LCO ratios of 10:90, 20:80, and 95:5, as confirmed by identical decomposition temperatures of 473.7 ± 3.4°C across all ratios.
claims.quote: TG analysis results (Fig. S5a-c) showed identical decomposition temperatures (473.7 ± 3.4°C) and mass loss trends across ratios... cathode particles did not significantly alter PVDF pyrolysis pathways, though trace HF may react with LCO forming LiF.

claims.claim: At 500°C and 60 min pyrolysis, the cathode particle separation rate reaches 99.50%, while at 300°C the separation rate is only 6.78%, indicating a strong temperature dependence of PVDF pyrolysis.
claims.quote: When temperature ranged from 300°C to 450°C, the separating rate of cathode particle increased from 6.78 % to 98.25 %. The optimal separating rate finally achieved 99.50 % under 500°C.

claims.claim: Conventional combustion tail gas treatment methods used in LIB recycling enterprises operate below 400°C and cannot effectively remove gaseous organic fluorides, generating HF and unknown fluorides that cause equipment corrosion.
claims.quote: conventional combustion methods (<400°C) are employed to eliminate gaseous organics. Nevertheless, in this process, gaseous fluorine would generate HF and some unknown fluorides, resulting in equipment corrosion or other secondary hazards.

claims.claim: For LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 batteries (211 Ah and 256 Ah), the critical temperature for thermal runaway onset at the electrode tabs was approximately 120°C for both, defined as a temperature change rate of 1°C/s.
claims.quote: The temperature of the battery tabs was measured, revealing that both batteries were in a critical state of thermal runaway near 120 degrees Celsius.

claims.claim: The 211 Ah LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 battery (Sample A, energy density 342.06 Wh/kg) exhibited a mass loss rate of 73% during thermal runaway, while the 256 Ah battery (Sample B, energy density 325.82 Wh/kg) exhibited a mass loss rate of 64.87%.
claims.quote: The mass loss of the two samples is 73% and 64.87%.

claims.claim: The primary gas components vented during thermal runaway of the 211 Ah LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 battery were CO (41.3%), CO2 (24.8%), H2 (21%), C2H4 (7.4%), and CH4 (3.9%).
claims.quote: For the 211 Ah battery, the vented gases were mainly composed of CO (41.3%), CO2 (24.8%), H2 (21%), C2H4 (7.4%) and CH4 (3.9%)

claims.claim: The 211 Ah battery (Sample A) had a maximum chamber pressure of 380.5 kPa and a maximum KLIB of 99.5 kPa·m·s−1, while the 256 Ah battery (Sample B) had a maximum chamber pressure of 330.3 kPa and a maximum KLIB of 68.2 kPa·m·s−1, indicating higher explosion risk for Sample A.
claims.quote: the pressure fluctuation ranges of the two samples were 99.5 and 68.2 kPa·m·s−1... the 211 Ah sample exhibited larger chamber pressure fluctuations and reached higher peak pressures, indicating a higher risk of explosion.

claims.claim: Gas production normalized to standard conditions (0°C, 101 kPa) was 1.958 L/Ah for the 211 Ah battery and 2.182 L/Ah for the 256 Ah battery, indicating that gas production increases with battery capacity.
claims.quote: the gas production is converted to standard conditions, resulting in 1.958 L/Ah for Sample A and 2.182 L/Ah for Sample B. It can be observed that gas production is related to the capacity of the battery.

claims.claim: When thermal runaway is accompanied by more severe combustion, the electrolyte components detected in the vented gas decrease because ejected electrolyte is more completely ignited.
claims.quote: When TR is accompanied by combustion, the electrolyte components in the collected gas sample will decrease. The severity of combustion in Sample A is higher than that in Sample B. The electrolyte components ejected will also be more fully ignited.

claims.claim: The mixed vented gases from the 211 Ah LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 battery had a lower flammable limit (LFL) of 8.49% and upper flammable limit (UFL) of 58.3%, while the 256 Ah battery had an LFL of 7.43% and UFL of 51.88%.
claims.quote: The calculated results are shown in Table 6 below. Sample UFL LFL Flammable Range A 58.3 8.49 49.81 B 51.88 7.43 44.45

claims.claim: High-nickel NCM90.5.5 batteries demonstrated shorter total eruption durations (32 s) compared to NCM622 batteries (36 s), attributed to higher Ni content intensifying redox reactions and increasing gas generation rate during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Compared to Zhang's analysis of NCM6 2 2, these two batteries demonstrate shorter eruption times. This could be attributed to the higher Ni content in the samples examined in this research, intensifying the redox reactions during TR and significantly increasing the gas generation rate. Consequently, the eruption duration decreases.

claims.claim: It took only 1 second from safety valve opening to the experimental chamber being filled with smoke during thermal runaway of LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 batteries.
claims.quote: It took only 1 s for the sample to transition from the opening of the safety valve to filling the experimental chamber with smoke.

claims.claim: This study only compared batteries with the same positive electrode material (NCM90.5.5) at different capacities and energy densities, and did not compare thermal runaway temperatures and gas production across different positive electrode materials, limiting generalizability.
claims.quote: This study only conducted comparisons with the same positive electrode materials, focusing solely on the differences in capacity and energy density. Subsequent additions should include comparisons of TR temperatures and gas production characteristics for different positive electrode materials, capacities, and application types.

claims.claim: The linearized gas flow rate during thermal runaway for the high-power 18650 cell (SonyVTC5A, NCA, 2.5 Ah) is 22.15 ± 14.70 gs−1.
claims.quote: the linearized gas flow rate for the high-power and high-energy cell is 22.15 gs−1 and 27.92 gs−1, respectively

claims.claim: The linearized gas flow rate during thermal runaway for the high-energy 18650 cell (LGMJ1, NMC811, 3.5 Ah) is 27.92 ± 9.500 gs−1.
claims.quote: the linearized gas flow rate for the high-power and high-energy cell is 22.15 gs−1 and 27.92 gs−1, respectively

claims.claim: The high-energy 18650 cell (LGMJ1, NMC811) exhibits a single peak asymmetrical gas velocity pattern during thermal runaway with a peak velocity of 398.5 ± 207.1 ms−1.
claims.quote: in case of the high-energy cell, it follows a single peak asymmetrical pattern with a peak of 398.5 ms−1

claims.claim: The high-power 18650 cell (SonyVTC5A, NCA) exhibits a bumpy gas velocity pattern with two to three bumps during thermal runaway, with a maximum gas velocity of 260.9 ± 103.5 ms−1.
claims.quote: the high-power cell shows a bumpy pattern with a maximum gas velocity of 260.9 ms−1

claims.claim: The transition time from safety valve opening to thermal runaway for the high-energy LGMJ1 cells (189.54 ± 87.56 s) is at least twice as long as for the high-power SonyVTC5A cells (69.46 ± 12.67 s).
claims.quote: It took 69.46 ± 12.67 s for the SonyVTC5A cells to go from opening of the safety valve into TR, whereas the non-reheated LGMJ1 cells needed 189.54 ± 87.56 s

claims.claim: The mass loss during the thermal runaway stage as a percentage of cell mass averaged 57.50% for SonyVTC5A and 63.45% for LGMJ1 cells, as measured by oscilloscope.
claims.quote: The boxplots (e) and (f) underneath show averaged mass losses with the Osci for SonyVTC5A and LGMJ1 of 57.50% and 63.45%, respectively.

claims.claim: When the safety valve opened during overheating tests, the average cell holder temperature was 182.01 °C for the high-power SonyVTC5A and 171.57 °C for the high-energy LGMJ1 cells.
claims.quote: when the safety valve opens, the cell holder temperature was in average 182.01 °C for the high-power battery and 171.57 °C for the high-energy battery

claims.claim: The gas temperature during thermal runaway exceeded the upper measuring limit of 1000 °C for both cell types tested, while the cell holder reached only approximately 262 °C and 272 °C for SonyVTC5A and LGMJ1, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas temperature exceeded the upper measuring limit of the data logger of 1000 °C. Obviously higher temperature were present. Whereas, the temperature of cell holder achieved approximately 262 °C and 272 °C for SonyVTC5A and LGMJ1, respectively.

claims.claim: Simulated gas flow rates from venting and TRP models (2.5 gs−1 to 3.5 gs−1) are approximately one order of magnitude lower than the experimentally measured gas flow rates for 18650 cells (22.15 gs−1 and 27.92 gs−1).
claims.quote: the simulated gas flow rates are given with 2.5 gs−1 [31] to 3.5 gs−1 [35] and one order of magnitude lower than our measured gas flow distribution with 22.15 ± 14.70 gs−1 for SonyVTC5A and 27.92 ± 9.500 gs−1 for LGMJ1 cells.

claims.claim: The momentum and mass conservation method determines gas flow rate and gas velocity at the vent area without requiring knowledge of vent pressure, vent temperature, vent area size or stability, or ejecta composition.
claims.quote: With this approach, the size and stability of venting area, venting temperature and cell temperature, venting pressure and cell pressure, as well as the composition of the ejecta are irrelevant for deriving the gas velocity and gas flow rate.

claims.claim: Optical methods measure gas velocity beyond the vent area and are limited to a few milliseconds of observation before wall interactions occur, yielding maximum jet velocities of 30–50 ms−1 for 18650 cells—approximately ten times lower than velocities measured at the vent area by the momentum and mass conservation method.
claims.quote: Garcia et al. [49–51] used optical methods and monitored maximum jet velocities between 30–50 ms−1, which are almost ten times lower than ours... Optical methods measure the velocity beyond the vent area, whereas the momentum and mass conservation method obtains information of the venting at the vent area. Additionally, optical methods can investigate the venting as long as no wall interactions appears, which is mostly limited to a few milliseconds

claims.claim: A mechanical nail penetration trigger would interfere with the force measurement in the momentum and mass conservation method, making it difficult to extract venting parameters.
claims.quote: mechanical trigger, e.g., nail penetration, would affect the force measurement with an overlapped force response and would make it difficult to extract the venting parameters.

claims.claim: The first venting event at safety valve opening for cylindrical 21700 NCA cells ejects approximately 0.536 g of mass in 0.064 s at a mass flow rate of 8.375 gs−1, which is below the ±2.45 g detection accuracy of the test bench force sensor.
claims.quote: Huang et al. [28] reported that the first venting takes 0.064 s for a 21700 format battery with a mass flow rate of 8.375 gs−1. Consequently, 0.536 g mass was ejected in their study and is around five times lower than the accuracy of our force sensor.

claims.claim: A symmetrical velocity pattern occurs at oval and round safety valves mostly used in prismatic LIBs, whereas an asymmetrical velocity pattern with the highest velocity peak is associated with cavity safety valves common in cylindrical LIBs.
claims.quote: A symmetrical velocity pattern occurs at oval and round safety valves which are mostly used in prismatic LIBs, whereas an asymmetrical velocity progress with the highest velocity peak developed with a cavity safety valve and is common in cylindrical LIBs

claims.claim: The safety valve of the tested 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 prismatic battery ruptures and vents when the pressure differential between the internal and external regions reaches 5 MPa.
claims.quote: According to the manufacturer's recommendations, the safety valve ruptures and vents when the pressure differential between the internal and external regions of the battery reaches 5 MPa.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of the 58 Ah NMC battery, the expansion force rose from baseline to a peak value of 1980 N within 2.5 seconds, representing a 12-fold increase, starting at the −15 s mark.
claims.quote: At the −15 s mark: Expansion force initiates a sharp ascent, reaching a peak value of 1980 N at −13.5 s, representing a 12-fold increase within a duration of 2.5 s.

claims.claim: The first appearance of sparks during thermal runaway coincided with a 0.01 V decrease in voltage and occurred at time point −(11.5 ± 1.5) s, concurrent with the expansion force reaching its maximum value and sharply declining.
claims.quote: The point at which the expansion force reached its maximum value and sharply declined, corresponding to the occurrence of the first jetting of sparks (as depicted in behavior shown in Figure 4a), was at the time point −(11.5 ± 1.5) s, concurrent with a 0.01 V decrease in voltage.

claims.claim: In the first eruption mode during thermal runaway, incomplete combustion of solid particles resulted in jetting to heights of up to 3.4 m, with a momentary eruption velocity of 90 m/s at the 1.5-s mark upon safety valve opening.
claims.quote: In the first mode, due to incomplete combustion, the generated solid particles can jet to heights of up to 3.4 m. The most intense occurrence of the first mode transpires at the 1.5-s mark, with a momentary eruption velocity of 90 m/s upon the opening of the safety valve.

claims.claim: The maximum flame propagation velocity during the gas explosion mode (second eruption mode) was 17.24 m/s, occurring at the 1.4-s mark, with the explosion center located 0.1 to 0.4 m from the safety valve.
claims.quote: The most intense event in the second mode transpires at the 1.4-s mark, with a maximum flame propagation velocity of 17.24 m/s... with a rough estimation indicating the explosion center to be within a range of 0.1 to 0.4 m from the safety valve.

claims.claim: Explosive phenomena during thermal runaway are initiated by ejected sparks igniting the combustible gases expelled during jetting, not by direct ignition of the gas cloud.
claims.quote: Observations from video footage indicate that explosive phenomena are initiated by the ignition of the ejected sparks, which ignite the combustible gases expelled during the jetting process.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of the 58 Ah NMC battery, the mass loss rate was 32.4 ± 2%, and the maximum expansion force ranged from 1900 to 2000 N across three repeated experiments.
claims.quote: EFmax (N): 1900~2000 ... The mass loss rate (%): 32.4 ± 2

claims.claim: The ideal gas law is inapplicable during the non-equilibrium stage of thermal runaway eruption due to a highly uneven temperature field, causing discrepancies between calculated and actual gas quantities.
claims.quote: However, due to the highly uneven temperature field induced by the vigorous eruption, a non-equilibrium stage emerges... During this stage, the ideal gas law is inapplicable due to the complex variations in eruption.

claims.claim: When calculating flammability limits of battery ejecta, the presence of solid particulates cannot be disregarded because ejected sparks representing combustion of solid particulates persist throughout the entire ejection process.
claims.quote: The ejected sparks, which represent the combustion of solid particulates, persisted throughout the entire ejection process. Hence, when calculating the flammability limits of battery ejecta, the presence of solid particulates cannot be disregarded.

claims.claim: The temperature above the safety valve (TE) reached a maximum of 941.2 °C at 7 s after voltage reached zero, while the battery surface temperature (TS) reached a maximum of 810 °C at 100 s, during thermal runaway of the 58 Ah NMC battery.
claims.quote: At the 7 s mark: TE attains its maximum value of 941.2 °C... At the 100 s mark: TS reaches its maximum value of 810 °C.

claims.claim: At 20 kPa ambient pressure, there are no combustion flames during the entire thermal runaway process of a single 18650 cylindrical cell, because oxygen mass concentration per volume is less than the stoichiometric ratio.
claims.quote: at a pressure of 20 kPa, there are no combustion flames in the whole TR process. This is probably because large amounts of combustible gases and ejections, released rapidly after the sparks (safety valve opened completely), are not ignited under 20 kPa for the reason that oxygen mass concentration per volume is less than the stoichiometric ratio.

claims.claim: As ambient pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average safety valve open time (first venting) decreases from 144 s to 133 s for 18650 cylindrical cells, from 475 s to 461 s for 10 Ah pouch cells, and from 559 s to 533 s for 15 Ah pouch cells.
claims.quote: As the pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average values of t1 (the average open time of the safety valve or the rupture time of the bag, which was counted by the video recorded in the tests) decrease from 144 s to 133 s, 475 s to 461 s, and 559 s to 533 s, for 18650 cylindrical cells, 10 Ah pouch cells, and 15 Ah pouch cells, respectively.

claims.claim: When ambient pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average trigger time of thermal runaway spark ejections is shortened for cylindrical cells (from 145 s to 133 s) but delayed for 10 Ah and 15 Ah pouch cells (from 625 s to 643 s and from 730 s to 761 s, respectively).
claims.quote: The average trigger time (t2) of spark ejections for cylindrical cells is 145 s in 95 kPa and 133 s in 20 kPa. However, t2 increases from 625 s to 643 s, and from 730 s to 761 s for 10 Ah and 15 Ah pouch cells, when the pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: Peak HRR decreases from 1.2 kW to 0.65 kW, 14.2 kW to 1.2 kW, 26.3 kW to 6.1 kW, and 41.1 kW to 8.7 kW for 1 cylindrical cell, 5 cylindrical cells, a 10 Ah pouch cell, and a 15 Ah pouch cell, respectively, when ambient pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.
claims.quote: The peak HRR decreases from 1.2 kW to 0.65 kW, 14.2 kW to 1.2 kW, 26.3 kW to 6.1 kW, and 41.1 kW to 8.7 kW for 1 cylindrical cell, 5 cylindrical cells, a 10 Ah pouch cell, and a 15 Ah pouch cell, respectively.

claims.claim: Average peak THC values at 20 kPa (1275 ppm, 3474 ppm, 819 ppm, and 1641 ppm for the four test groups) are higher than at 95 kPa (1019 ppm, 256 ppm, 127 ppm, and 559 ppm), and average peak CO values increase from 0.19, 0.21, 0.16, and 0.24 vol.% at 95 kPa to 0.28, 0.91, 0.59, and 0.72 vol.% at 20 kPa.
claims.quote: The average peak THC values in 20 kPa are about 1275 ppm, 3474 ppm, 819 ppm, and 1641 ppm for four groups of tests, which can be seen in Figure 13b, which are higher than 1019 ppm, 256 ppm, 127 ppm, and 559 ppm under 95 kPa, respectively. From the CO curves in Figure 12b, the average peak values of CO increase from 0.19 vol.% to 0.28 vol.%, 0.21 vol.% to 0.91 vol.%, 0.16 vol.% to 0.59 vol.%, and 0.24 vol.% to 0.72 vol.%, with the pressure drop from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: Pouch cells have higher peak HRR and THR (higher thermal fire hazard) but lower concentrations of THC and CO per unit capacity than cylindrical 18650 cells, both at 95 kPa and 20 kPa.
claims.quote: the values of peak HRR, THR, and peak CO per unit capacity for the pouch cell are higher than the values of the cylindrical cell, but the peak values of CO/CO2, THC and CO per capacity are lower than in cylindrical cells. It indicates that the pouch cell with a soft aluminum–polymer bag has higher thermal hazards of TR fire, but a lower concentration of toxic/combustible gases than cylindrical cells with hard cans.

claims.claim: Mutual interference between cells in a multiple-cell pack still exists under 20 kPa through direct contact heat transfer, which is not affected by pressure variation, but the process is less vigorous than under 95 kPa without violent explosion and jet flames.
claims.quote: Those phenomena indicate that mutual interference still exists under 20 kPa, but this process is not as vigorous as the multiple cells under 95 kPa, without the violent fire behaviors of explosion and jet flames. This mutual interference may be ascribed to the heat transfer through the pathway of direct contact among the cells, which is not affected by the variation in pressure.

claims.claim: The CO/CO2 ratios under 95 kPa (1.55, 0.96, 0.45, and 0.21 for the four test groups) are lower than the ratios under 20 kPa (2.05, 1.99, 1.87, and 2.55), indicating that combustion efficiency of combustible ejections decreases with reduction in external pressure from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.
claims.quote: The ratios of CO/CO2 are 1.55, 0.96, 0.45, and 0.21, for the four groups of tests under 95 kPa, respectively, and are lower than the ratios of 2.05, 1.99, 1.87, and 2.55 under 20 kPa... This indicates that the combustion efficiency of combustible ejections decreases with the reduction in the external pressure from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: For pouch cells at 20 kPa, the smoking duration after first venting (Δt1) is greatly extended from 150 s to 182 s and from 171 s to 228 s for 10 Ah and 15 Ah cells respectively, delaying the average thermal runaway trigger time compared to 95 kPa.
claims.quote: this average smoking duration is greatly extended, from 150 s to 182 s and from 171 s to 228 s for 10 Ah and 15 Ah pouch cells, as the pressure varies from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, leading to a delay of the average trigger time of the TR at 20 kPa.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway intensity of individual cells decreases under lower ambient pressure, but the burning intensity increases dramatically when thermal runaway occurs in a battery pack.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway intensity of individual cells decreases under lower ambient pressure, but the burning intensity increases dramatically when thermal runaway occurs in a battery pack.

claims.claim: LFP cells did not flame at 0% and 30% SOCs, while LCO cells flamed at all SOCs tested, consistent with LFP cells having higher thermal stability.
claims.quote: LFP tests did not flame at 0% and 30% SOCs, while LCO tests flamed at all SOCs (Figure 5b). This is consistent with LFP cells having higher thermal stability compared to LCO cells.

claims.claim: OC was more abundant during cell venting, while EC and phosphate (PO4 3−) were more abundant when flaming combustion occurred.
claims.quote: While OC was more abundant during cell venting, EC and PO4 3− were more abundant when flaming combustion occurred.

claims.claim: The most abundant PM2.5 constituents from LIB thermal runaway combustion were OC, EC, phosphate (PO4 3−), and fluoride (F−), contributing 7–91%, 0.2–40%, 1–44%, and 0.7–3% to PM2.5 mass, respectively.
claims.quote: The most abundant PM2.5 constituents were OC, EC, phosphate (PO4 3−), and fluoride (F−), contributing 7–91%, 0.2–40%, 1–44%, and 0.7–3% to the PM2.5 mass, respectively.

claims.claim: Particles from LFP tests had higher organic matter (OM) but lower elemental carbon (EC) compared to LCO tests, consistent with the higher thermal stability of LFP cells.
claims.quote: Overall, particles from LFP tests had higher OM but lower EC compared to LCO tests, consistent with the higher thermal stability of LFP cells.

claims.claim: Ignition of the emissions stream, rather than cell state of charge (SOC), is mostly responsible for the general composition of emitted PM2.5 particles from LFP cells.
claims.quote: This indicates that ignition of the emissions stream, rather than cell SOC, is mostly responsible for the general composition of emitted PM2.5 particles.

claims.claim: For LFP tests at 50% and 75% SOC dominated by flaming combustion, PO4 3− fraction dominated PM2.5 mass at 34–44%, while OM fractions dropped to 1–20% compared to high OM at 0% and 30% SOC.
claims.quote: Compared to the 0% and 30% SOC cases, the OM fractions were much lower (1–20%), while the PO4 3− fraction, practically absent in the 0% and 30% SOC cases, dominated with 34–44% of PM2.5 mass.

claims.claim: All LIB fire PM2.5 particles measured in this study had anion/cation ratios greater than 1.0, indicating that the freshly emitted particles were acidic.
claims.quote: Anion/cation ratios > 1.0 indicate that the particles were acidic.

claims.claim: Metal abundances (Mn, Co, Ni, Cu) in PM2.5 (<2.5 µm) were 0.1–3% of the values reported for larger settleable particles in the literature, because lighter carbonaceous particles remain airborne longer while heavier metallic particles settle out.
claims.quote: Measured values for Mn, Co, Ni, and Cu were much lower at 0.1–3% of the literature values... the larger, settleable particles that were analyzed in previous studies were likely to have metallic compositions instead of the majority of carbonaceous particles collected in this study, which were lighter and suspended in air for a longer time.

claims.claim: Different heating rates applied to LIB cells during testing did not affect emissions, because thermal runaway will occur independently of heating rate once it has been triggered.
claims.quote: Different heating rates did not affect emissions, as thermal runaway will occur independently of heating once it has been triggered.

claims.claim: For LCO tests, LCO anode and cathode underwent more decomposition (evidenced by increasing Li and Co abundance in PM2.5) at higher SOCs despite constant flame temperatures of approximately 400°C.
claims.quote: That Li and Co increased with SOC indicates that the LIB anode and cathode underwent more decomposition at higher SOCs despite constant flame temperatures of ~400 °C (Figure 5), contrasting with the abrupt temperature changes for LFP tests.

claims.claim: Soot particles collected near the LIB flame by Xu et al. had very high total carbon content (90–94%) and lower phosphate abundances than particles sampled further from the flame, because volatile compounds such as phosphate had not yet cooled and condensed into the particle phase.
claims.quote: The soot-dominated composition from Xu et al. could be caused by the particles being collected close to the flame before volatile compounds (e.g., phosphate) cooled down and condensed in the particle phase, which is evident from much lower P abundances than in this study.

claims.claim: NH4+ abundance in PM2.5 was highest at low SOCs for both LFP and LCO cell types and decreased for mid-range SOCs, likely because NH4+ decomposes at high flaming temperatures.
claims.quote: NH4+ abundance was highest at low SOCs for both cell types and decreased for mid-range SOCs, especially in LCO tests, likely because NH4+ decomposes at high flaming temperatures.

claims.claim: The LCO cell's thin wrapper likely results in multiple venting locations only some of which are ignited, which can cause misallocation of emission source between venting and flaming phases, unlike LFP cells whose steel case and relief vents concentrate venting into a single stream.
claims.quote: The LCO cell's thin wrapper likely results in multiple venting locations, only some of which are ignited. Since all emissions are attributed to flaming once any flames are seen, this could result in a misallocation of the emission source. The LFP cell's steel case and relief vents, on the other hand, ensure venting is concentrated into a single stream, which was either ignited or not.

claims.claim: Elemental phosphorus dominated elemental emissions for both LIB cell types, constituting 6.4% and 8.5% of PM2.5 mass on average for LFP and LCO tests, respectively.
claims.quote: Elemental emissions for both LIB types were dominated by P, consisting of 6.4% and 8.5% of PM2.5 mass on average for LFP and LCO tests, respectively.

claims.claim: EC2 (refractory carbon with graphite-like structure evolving at 740°C) was the dominant elemental carbon fraction, accounting for 76% of total EC on average across both LIB cell types.
claims.quote: EC2 was the dominant EC fraction, accounting for 76% of total EC on average.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in LIBs is characterized by rapid self-heating starting at around 150–220°C and increasing to about 1000°C.
claims.quote: thermal runaway is characterised by rapid self-heating starting at around 150–220 ◦C and increasing to about 1000 ◦C

claims.claim: The first step towards thermal runaway upon overheating is the breakdown of the SEI layer on the graphite anode, which occurs at about 90–120°C.
claims.quote: the first step towards TR is commonly the breakdown of the SEI layer on the graphite anode, which occurs at about 90–120 ◦C

claims.claim: When the separator melts at temperatures in the range of 135–165°C, electrical contact between the anode and cathode can result in an internal short circuit and self-discharge.
claims.quote: When the separator melts at temperatures in the range of 135–165 ◦C, the electrical contact between the anode and cathode can result in an ISC and, thereby, self-discharge.

claims.claim: At temperatures of 150–310°C, the cathode begins to decompose and release oxygen.
claims.quote: As the temperature increases further in the range of 150–310 ◦C, the cathode begins to decompose and release oxygen.

claims.claim: At 200°C and higher, all components of the electrolyte start to decompose exothermically, producing CO2, HF, hydrocarbons, and various fluorinated hydrocarbon gases.
claims.quote: As the temperature reaches 200 ◦C and higher, all components of the electrolyte start to decompose exothermically, upon which CO2, hydrogen fluoride (HF), hydrocarbons, and various fluorinated hydrocarbon gases are formed.

claims.claim: Reactive gases O2 and H2 are dissolved in the electrolyte to a lesser extent than the more stable CO2 and hydrocarbons, making them more available to react during a thermal event.
claims.quote: the reactive gases O2 and H2 are dissolved in the electrolyte to a lesser extent than the more stable CO2 and hydrocarbons. This means that the reactive gases are, to a larger extent, available to react with additional gases formed during a thermal event

claims.claim: LFP cathodes likely produce very little or no oxygen during thermal runaway, giving them the lowest risk among cathode chemistries for oxygen-driven reactions.
claims.quote: Lithium–iron phosphate (LFP) cathodes likely produce very little or no oxygen at all, which is represented by the half-cell (cathode) chemistry with the lowest risk going into TR.

claims.claim: The reactivity ranking of oxygen-producing cathode materials is LCO > NCA > NCM > LMO.
claims.quote: The reactivity of the oxygen-producing cathodes is ranked as follows: LCO > NCA > NCM > LMO

claims.claim: No significant fraction of O2 has been reported in vented gases from LIB cells, with two plausible explanations: O2 is consumed internally before venting, or O2 was not targeted for measurement.
claims.quote: No significant fraction of O2 has been reported from studies of vented gases. There are at least two plausible explanations for the low presence of the vented gases: either O2 is essentially consumed in the chemical reactions inside the cell before the venting occurs, or simply O2 has not been a target compound for investigation in most studies

claims.claim: The gas volume of the first vent comprises approximately 1–7% of the total gas volume when followed by a main vent.
claims.quote: the gas volume is in the range of about 1–7% of the total gassing when the first vent was followed by a main vent

claims.claim: For NCA cells at low SOC, CO2 dominates vent gas composition, while CO increases and dominates at SOC greater than 60%, with the sum of CO and CO2 remaining consistent at about 70% across all SOCs.
claims.quote: An important trend was that at a low SOC, CO2 dominated, while CO increased and dominated at a SOC greater than 60%. However, the sum of the CO and CO2 was consistent at about 70% for all cells.

claims.claim: Slow thermal runaway initiation by homogeneous heating favors higher relative amounts of CO2, whereas fast triggers like nail penetration or high-power localized heaters typically lead to higher relative concentrations of CO.
claims.quote: Slow TR initiation, such as the homogeneous heating of the entire cell, favours higher relative amounts of CO2, whereas fast triggers, like nail penetration or high-power localised heaters, typically lead to incomplete oxidation and higher relative concentrations of CO.

claims.claim: Single-cell results cannot be extrapolated linearly to multi-cell configurations because higher temperatures are realized in multi-cell setups, directly impacting chemical processing and reaction rates.
claims.quote: the test level influences the TR dynamics, and single-cell results cannot be extrapolated linearly to multi-cell configurations due to the higher temperatures realised in the latter case, which directly impacts the chemical processing and rates of reaction.

claims.claim: For highly reactive species such as HF and POF3, the presence of additional surfaces in multi-cell assemblies reduces actual gas concentrations due to rapid adsorption and reaction.
claims.quote: For highly reactive species, such as HF and POF3, the presence of additional surfaces in multi-cell assemblies and batteries reduces the actual gas concentrations due to rapid adsorption and reaction.

claims.claim: EMC shows signs of thermal decomposition at 175°C in the presence of 1.2 M LiPF6, while DMC is not affected by the salt at temperatures up to 450°C.
claims.quote: With 1.2 M of LiPF6, EMC shows signs of thermal decomposition already at 175 ◦C, while DMC is not affected.

claims.claim: For aged NCA cells, calendar-aged cells had significant amounts of C2H2 in the first vent, attributed to gases formed during ageing rather than direct response to the experimental trigger.
claims.quote: Calendar-aged cells, in particular, had significant amounts of C2H2, which was attributed to gases formed during the ageing processes and not in direct response to the experimental trigger.

claims.claim: CO2 concentrations measured only in an inert atmosphere can be considered representative of vent gas composition because CO oxidizes to CO2 in the presence of O2 in non-inert environments.
claims.quote: only the CO/CO2 ratio measured in an inert atmosphere can be considered representative of the vent gas composition.

claims.claim: A 10°C temperature difference between 150°C and 160°C in NCA cells led to more than a doubling of gas volume, indicating significant internal processing in that temperature range.
claims.quote: there was quite a large difference between cells quenched at 150 ◦C and 160 ◦C; the additional 10 ◦C led to more than a doubling of the gas volume, indicating that significant internal processing occurred in the cell at this temperature.

claims.claim: The critical surface temperatures at thermal runaway onset (Tf-TR) for 50 Ah batteries under lateral heating are: LFP 346°C, NIBs 292°C, NCM523 290°C, NCM622 281°C, ranking thermal stability as LFP > NIBs > NCM523 > NCM622.
claims.quote: The critical temperature for thermal runaway (Tf-TR) of the batteries near the heating plate are as follows: LiFePO4 (LFP) battery: 346 °C, Sodium-ion battery (NIBs): 292 °C, NCM523 battery: 290 °C, NCM622 battery: 281 °C.

claims.claim: The heat input required to trigger thermal runaway in 50 Ah batteries ranks as: LFP (592.8 kJ) > NCM622 (262 kJ) > NCM523 (274 kJ) > NIBs (158 kJ), with NIBs requiring the least energy.
claims.quote: The heat injected into the batteries is as follows: 592.8 kJ for the LFP battery, 274 kJ for the NCM523 battery, and 262 kJ for the NCM622 battery... NIBs battery has the lowest triggering energy for thermal runaway, while the LFP battery has the highest triggering energy, making it the safest.

claims.claim: For NCM622 and NCM523 batteries, the safety valve opening and onset of violent thermal runaway occur almost simultaneously, making it nearly impossible to use safety valve opening as an early warning indicator.
claims.quote: for the two types of NCM lithium batteries, due to their higher energy density, the opening of the safety valve and the intense thermal runaway (sharp temperature rise) occur simultaneously. Therefore, it is almost impossible to use the opening of the safety valve as an early warning indicator for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: NIBs thermal runaway gas mixture has the lowest lower flammability limit (LFL = 3.39%) among the four battery types, giving it the highest explosion risk, primarily due to significant propylene (C3H6) content.
claims.quote: the sodium-ion battery had the lower flammability limit, with an LFL value of 3.98. This is primarily because the gases produced during the thermal runaway of the sodium-ion battery contain a significant amount of propylene (C3H6), which has the lower flammability limit.

claims.claim: The dominant combustible gas differs by battery chemistry: CO dominates for NCM622 (35%) and NCM523 (29%), while H2 dominates for LFP (44%) and NIBs (30%) during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: For NCM622 and NCM523, carbon monoxide is the dominant combustible gas, with volume fractions of 35% and 29%, respectively. In contrast, hydrogen is the main flammable gas for LFP and NIBs, with volume fractions of 44% and 30%, respectively.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway propagation rates through the battery thickness rank as NCM523 (3.375 mm/s) > NCM622 (2.7 mm/s) > NIBs (1.726 mm/s) > LFP (1.241 mm/s), with LFP exhibiting the lowest rate due to lower thermal reactivity of LiFePO4.
claims.quote: The propagation rate for the sodium-ion battery is 1.726 mm/s, for the LFP battery is 1.241 mm/s, for the NCM523 battery is 3.375 mm/s, and for the NCM622 battery is 2.7 mm/s. The LFP battery exhibits the lowest thermal runaway propagation rate, attributed to the lower thermal reactivity of the LiFePO4 material.

claims.claim: NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate thermal runaway more readily than LFP cells.
claims.quote: comparative experiments reveal that NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate TR more readily than LFP cells, indicating chemistry-dependent risk profiles

claims.claim: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180°C and 250°C, with peak heating occurring between 210°C and 360°C.
claims.quote: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180°C and 250°C, with peak heating occurring between 210°C and 360°C

claims.claim: In NP tests on NMC prismatic cells, with increasing SOC, CO concentrations became dominant over CO2, whereas H2 concentrations showed a more gradual increase with SOC.
claims.quote: SOC was observed to influence the relative concentrations of CO and CO2 in prismatic cell configurations. With increasing SOC, CO concentrations became dominant over CO2, whereas H2 concentrations showed a more gradual increase with SOC, almost evening out with the CO2 concentration at 100% SOC

claims.claim: In NP tests conducted in air atmosphere versus N2 atmosphere, air environments resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend.
claims.quote: the concentrations of H2 and CO2 can differ in tests conducted in air or inert environments: air environments resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend

claims.claim: LFP cells of different capacities were found not to undergo failure during NP tests, while NMC pouch cells at full charge experienced TR conditions during NP tests regardless of the test environments.
claims.quote: LFP cells of different capacities were found not to undergo failure during NP tests, let alone a TR... only NMC pouch cells at full charge experienced TR conditions during NP tests regardless of the test environments

claims.claim: LFP batteries emit significantly higher amounts of CO during thermal runaway—up to an order of magnitude greater than LCO and NMC batteries.
claims.quote: both LCO and NMC batteries release similar levels of CO, while lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries emit significantly higher amounts–up to an order of magnitude greater than LCO and NMC batteries

claims.claim: Total gas generation per unit capacity for NMC prismatic cells across NP studies ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah, suggesting a correlation between cell capacity and gas evolution.
claims.quote: the total gas generation per unit capacity ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah, suggesting a correlation between cell capacity and gas evolution

claims.claim: Using a penetration speed of 1 mm/s in NP tests, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles and allow more stable and consistent triggering of TR.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. examined the influence of penetration speed (e.g., 1 mm/s, 5 mm/s, 10 mm/s, 40 mm/s, and 80 mm/s) and found that using a penetration speed of 1 mm/s, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles. This slow penetration rate was found to be able to allow for more stable and consistent triggering of TR

claims.claim: Moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products (e.g., PF5 and POF3) to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume during thermal abuse tests.
claims.quote: moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products (e.g., PF5 and POF3) to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), further increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume

claims.claim: The separator melts at 135–165°C during thermal runaway propagation, allowing electrode contact and resulting in an internal short circuit.
claims.quote: The rising temperature leads to the melting of the separator (135–165°C), allowing the electrodes to make contact, resulting in an internal short circuit

claims.claim: Smoke particulate from failing lithium-ion batteries contains nickel, cobalt, and manganese in ratios broadly similar to those expected from the cell cathodes.
claims.quote: nickel, cobalt and manganese are present in the smoke as well as the near-field residues, and are in similar ratios to those expected from the cathodes within these batteries.

claims.claim: Aluminium is present in higher proportions in solid near-field residues than in smoke particulate for NMC cells, attributed to molten aluminium globules that condense or react with air to form alumina compounds rather than being carried in the smoke.
claims.quote: the proportion of aluminium observed in the solid residue is greater than that in the smoke particulate. This is most likely due to the larger sized globules of molten aluminium ejected which will either condense or rapidly react in air to form alumina compounds.

claims.claim: Copper is less likely than aluminium to be released into smoke during single-cell failure because copper's melting point (1083°C) is much higher than aluminium's (660°C), making it less mobile in the exiting gas stream.
claims.quote: Copper, with its much higher melting point (1083 1C as compared to 660 1C for aluminium) is less likely to have been released from the cell in this manner.

claims.claim: In multi-cell failure events generating higher temperatures, more significant levels of copper may be seen in smoke and residues compared to single-cell events.
claims.quote: It may be that in failure events involving multiple cells, where which generate higher temperatures, that more significant levels of copper are seen in the smoke and in the residues.

claims.claim: For LCO-cathode cells (F and G), aluminium proportion in residue was less than in smoke particulate, attributed to higher temperatures and more violent failure events causing wider aluminium dispersal.
claims.quote: for cells F and G there is less aluminium in the residue than the smoke; these cells have an LCO cathode-the higher temperatures generated by this cathode upon failure, and the more violent events observed may suggest that the aluminium is more likely to be widely dispersed when these cells fail.

claims.claim: The proportion of nickel in smoke particulate appears slightly higher than in near-field residues across the full range of cells tested.
claims.quote: the proportion of nickel in the smoke particulate seems slightly higher than in the residues.

claims.claim: The analytical methods used in this study do not allow comprehensive determination of the metal-containing compounds or species present in smoke and residues.
