claims.quote: The methods of analysis used here do not allow the comprehensive determination of the metal containing compounds/species; this is the subject of further work.

claims.claim: NMC module subjected to nail penetration resulted in the highest PM2.5 emission rate of 551 g/h and total particle number emission rate of 2.1E+17 particles/h (or 1.9E07 part./cm3).
claims.quote: This module resulted in the highest emission rates of 551 g/h of PM2.5 and 2.1E þ 17 particles/h (or 1.9E07 part./cm3) of total PN.

claims.claim: LFP modules subjected to nail penetration did not exhibit thermal runaway propagation to adjacent cells, whereas the NMC module subjected to nail penetration exhibited cell-to-cell propagation of thermal runaway through all 14 cells.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway did not propagate from the penetration region to other cells in the module... When nail penetration was initiated, the nail penetrated the first cell. The first cell entered thermal runaway immediately, and this led to the propagation of thermal runaway from cell-to-cell unlike the case for LFP modules. All 14 cells entered thermal runaway eventually, one at a time.

claims.claim: During LFP overcharge tests, HF peak concentration reached approximately 170–177 ppm, which exceeded the NIOSH IDLH level of 30 ppm for several minutes even under well-ventilated conditions.
claims.quote: From a health effects standpoint, the HF peak of 177 ppm is of the greatest concern. National Institute for Occupational Safety & Health (NIOSH) defines the level of HF that is immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH) as 30 ppm, and the data show the measured concentration remaining above this level for several minutes even under these well-ventilated conditions.

claims.claim: No HF was observed during the NMC nail penetration test, attributed to the use of an anion salt not containing fluoride in that battery chemistry.
claims.quote: Despite the high rate of electrolyte combustion, there was no HF observed in the NMC test at all, likely because of the use of an anion salt not containing fluoride.

claims.claim: Battery fire PM2.5 emission rates from LFP overcharge and NMC nail penetration tests (exceeding 375 g/h) are 5 to 6 orders of magnitude higher than those typically emitted from the exhaust of a modern heavy-duty diesel engine.
claims.quote: Both overcharge tests of LFP modules, and the nail penetration test of the NMC module resulted in PM2.5 emissions exceeding 375 g/h and total PN emissions of the order of 2E þ 17 part./h. These emission rates are 5 to 6 orders of magnitude higher than those typically emitted from the exhaust of a modern heavy-duty diesel engine.

claims.claim: For LFP overcharge tests, particle emissions showed a continuous increase for approximately 400 seconds after thermal runaway inception, followed by a gradual return to baseline levels after approximately 1000 seconds.
claims.quote: After thermal runaway inception, a continuous increase in emissions were observed for (cid:2)400 s after which emissions gradually returned to baseline levels after (cid:2)1000 s.

claims.claim: The NMC nail penetration test produced a peak CO2 value more than 20 times that observed in LFP overcharge tests, indicating a much larger mass of electrolyte combusted per second.
claims.quote: From a gaseous emissions standpoint, NMC nail penetration test resulted in a peak CO2 value that was more than 20 times that observed in LFP overcharge tests, indicating a much larger mass of electrolyte combusted per second.

claims.claim: During the NMC nail penetration test, formaldehyde was measured above its 20 ppm IDLH level for less than 5 seconds.
claims.quote: The most hazardous compound observed during NMC nail penetration test was formaldehyde, which was measured above its 20 ppm IDLH level for less than 5 s.

claims.claim: Volatile weight fraction of PM2.5 was approximately 20% for both LFP overcharge tests and approximately 8% for the NMC nail penetration test.
claims.quote: Vacuum oven sublimation based volatile weight fraction determination yielded similar fractions of the order of 20% for both tests... Test 5 [NMC nail penetration]: Volatile weight fraction of PM2.5: 8%

claims.claim: Mechanical abuse factors such as nail penetration may directly result in battery failure stages 3 and 4, bypassing the earlier thermal and electrical abuse stages.
claims.quote: While the above stages are more applicable for thermal and electrical abuse factors that lead to cell heating, mechanical abuse factors may directly result in failure stages 3 and 4.

claims.claim: The vent gas volume increased linearly with SOC for LiCoO2 pouch cells (3.7 V, 4.8 Ah), ranging from 1.3 L at 30% SOC heated at 5°C/min to 9.9 L at 100% SOC heated at 20°C/min.
claims.quote: The vent gas volume increased linearly with SOC, and ranged from 1.3 L, for a pouch cell charged to 30% SOC and heated at 5°C/min, to 9.9 L, for a pouch cell charged to 100% SOC and heated at 20°C/min.

claims.claim: The combustion energy increased linearly with SOC for LiCoO2 pouch cells, ranging from 5.2 kJ at 30% SOC and 5°C/min to 114 kJ at 100% SOC and 20°C/min.
claims.quote: The combustion energy increased linearly with SOC, and ranged from 5.2 kJ, for a pouch cell charged to 30% SOC and heated at 5°C/min, to 114 kJ, for a pouch cell charged to 100% SOC and heated at 20°C/min.

claims.claim: For LiCoO2 pouch cells, heating at 20°C/min compared to 5°C/min resulted in an average of 14.1% more vent gas and an average of 34.4% greater combustion energy across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: When comparing cells heated at 20°C/min to cells heated at 5°C/min, across the tested SOCs, the pouch cells released an average of 14.1% more vent gas and had an average of 34.4% greater combustion energy.

claims.claim: For LiCoO2 cylindrical cells, heating at 20°C/min compared to 5°C/min resulted in an average of 7.2% more vent gas and an average of 13.0% greater combustion energy across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: the cylindrical released an average of 7.2% more vent gas and had an average of 13.0% greater combustion energy.

claims.claim: Slower heating rates cause more electrolyte to boil and vent before thermal runaway occurs, leaving less electrolyte in the cell at the time of thermal runaway, which reduces vent gas volume and combustion energy compared to faster heating rates.
claims.quote: This is because slower heating rates allow more time for the electrolyte inside of the cells to boil and vent than with cells heated at faster heating rates. Therefore, cells heated at slower rates have less electrolyte remaining in the cells once thermal runaway actually occurs which reduces the amount of vent gas and combustion energy over the same cells tested at faster heating rates.

claims.claim: The top three vent gas constituents by volume across all SOCs for both LiCoO2 pouch and cylindrical cells were carbon dioxide, hydrogen, and carbon monoxide, contributing 81±2%vol and 54±12%vol of the total gas mixture for pouch and cylindrical cells, respectively.
claims.quote: The top three constituents as a percentage of the vent gas by volume across all SOCs for the pouch and cylindrical cells were carbon dioxide, hydrogen, and carbon monoxide. On average, the three gases contribute to 81±2%vol and 54±12%vol of the total gas mixture for pouch cells and cylindrical cells, respectively.

claims.claim: The average concentration of hydrogen by volume in vent gas across all SOCs was 24±4%vol for LiCoO2 pouch cells and 12±4%vol for LiCoO2 cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: The average concentration of hydrogen by volume in vent gas across all SOCs for the pouch and cylindrical cells were 24±4%vol and 12±4%vol, respectively.

claims.claim: At 30% SOC, the percent change in combustion energy between heating rates of 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 129% for pouch cells and 64% for cylindrical cells, representing the maximum percent change across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: The maximum percent change in combustion energy between a pouch cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 129% and occurred in cells charged to 30% SOC... The maximum percent change in combustion energy between a cylindrical cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 64% and occurred in cells charged to 30% SOC.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the total difference in combustion energy between heating rates of 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 21.5 kJ for pouch cells and 5.9 kJ for cylindrical cells, representing the maximum absolute difference across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: The maximum total difference in combustion energy between a pouch cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 21.5 kJ and occurred in cells charged to 100% SOC... The maximum total difference in combustion energy between a cylindrical cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 5.9 kJ and occurred in cells charged to 100% SOC.

claims.claim: At 5%vol Halon 1301, the flammability limits of premixed lithium-ion battery vent gas in air range from 13.80%vol to 26.07%vol, and 8.59%vol Halon 1301 is required to render all ratios of Li-Ion pBVG in air inert.
claims.quote: At 5%vol Halon 1301 the flammability limits range from 13.80%vol to 26.07%vol of a premixed lithium-ion battery vent gas in air (Li-Ion pBVG). Testing suggests that 8.59 %vol Halon 1301 is required to render all ratios of the Li-Ion pBVG in air inert.

claims.claim: A previous FAA study statistically determined that cylindrical cells heated at or above 15°C/min were more likely to produce a violent thermal runaway reaction than cells heated at less than 15°C/min, with critical thresholds defined as a maximum cell case temperature above 250°C and release of over 0.5 L of vent gas.
claims.quote: heating rates at or above 15°C/min were more likely to produce a violent thermal runaway reaction than cells heated at less than 15°C/min. The critical thresholds for a violent thermal runaway reaction were determined to be a maximum cell case temperature above 250°C and the release of over 0.5 L of vent gas.

claims.claim: The current 5%vol Halon 1301 knockdown concentration and 3%vol sustained concentration used in Class C cargo compartments may not be sufficient to inert lithium-ion battery vent gas and air mixtures.
claims.quote: the 5%vol Halon 1301 knockdown concentration and the sustained 3%vol Halon 1301 in a Class C cargo compartment may not be sufficient at inerting lithium-ion battery vent gas and air mixtures.

claims.claim: For LiCoO2 pouch cells, the percentage of carbon dioxide by volume in vent gas decreased while carbon monoxide by volume increased with increasing SOC, while hydrogen remained largely unchanged.
claims.quote: Measurements show that the percentage of carbon dioxide by volume decreased while carbon monoxide by volume increased with increasing SOCs. Many of the other gas constituents including hydrogen remain largely unchanged.

claims.claim: 27.7%vol Halon 1301 is the minimum inerting concentration for hydrogen and air mixtures.
claims.quote: Testing suggests that 27.7%vol Halon 1301 is the minimum inerting concentration for hydrogen and air mixtures.

claims.claim: The vent gas volume for LiCoO2 cylindrical cells (3.7 V, 2.6 Ah) increased linearly with SOC, ranging from 1.1 L at 30% SOC heated at 5°C/min to 3.75 L at 100% SOC heated at 20°C/min.
claims.quote: The vent gas volume increased linearly with SOC, and ranged from 1.1 L, for a cylindrical cell charged to 30% SOC and heated at 5°C/min, to 3.75 L, for a cylindrical cell charged to 100% SOC and heated at 20°C/min.

claims.claim: The failure rate of lithium-ion batteries under safe and proper operation conditions is estimated to be 1 in 40 million.
claims.quote: The failure rate of LIBs under safe and proper operation conditions is extremely low, estimated to be 1 in 40 million [2].

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is an uncontrolled and self-propagating exothermic reaction within the battery that leads to rapid increase in temperature, venting of combustible gases and potential thermal events.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway is an uncontrolled and self-propagating exothermic reaction within the battery that leads to rapid increase in temperature, venting of combustible gases and potential thermal events [7].

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is generally caused by internal short circuits, manufacturing defects, mechanical abuse, or exposure to high temperatures.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway is generally caused by internal short circuits, manufacturing defects, mechanical abuse, or exposure to high temperatures.

claims.claim: The most common gases observed during thermal runaway of a lithium-ion battery include carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), methane (CH4), hydrogen (H2), and ethane (C2H6).
claims.quote: The most common gases observed during thermal runaway of a LIB include carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), methane (CH4), hydrogen (H2), and ethane (C2H6) [3].

claims.claim: Battery vent gas mixtures from NCA chemistry are on the highly explosive end of the spectrum, while LFP gas mixtures are on the less explosive end.
claims.quote: these gases have also been shown to be on the highly explosive end (NCA) and less explosive end (LFP) of the spectrum as far as common battery vent gas mixtures are concerned [3].

claims.claim: The NCA gas mixture composition is 10% CO2, 37.1% CO, 42.8% H2, 7.1% CH4, 3% C2H4, and 0% C2H6.
claims.quote: Table 2.1: Gas compositions of analyzed battery vent gases ... NCA 10 37.1 42.8 7.1 3 0

claims.claim: The LFP gas mixture composition is 48.4% CO2, 29.5% CO, 5.6% H2, 7% CH4, 0% C2H4, and 0.5% C2H6.
claims.quote: Table 2.1: Gas compositions of analyzed battery vent gases ... LFP 48.4 29.5 5.6 7 0 0.5

claims.claim: Battery vent gas mixtures representative of NCA and LFP chemistries are unlikely to auto-ignite at atmospheric pressure and require an external ignition source to pose a safety threat.
claims.quote: As these gas mixtures are unlikely to auto-ignite at atmospheric pressure, there must be an external ignition source for these gases to pose a safety threat.

claims.claim: The power supply used in the experiment was capable of delivering up to 16 kV at 500 A using a capacitor charger connected to a capacitor bank.
claims.quote: The power supply connected to the electrodes was capable of delivering up to 16 kV at 500A. It consisted of a capacitor charger connected to a capacitor bank.

claims.claim: Increasing the electrode separation distance increases the minimum breakdown voltage regardless of gas medium, because the increased distance results in an increased volume of gas which must be ionized, representing increased resistance.
claims.quote: Regardless of the gas media, this increased volume represents an increased resistance. Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that a higher voltage is required to produce a current between the electrodes.

claims.claim: For LFP gas mixture at a flow velocity of 5 m/s, the breakdown voltage at room temperature (19 °C) is notably lower than at 200 and 300 °C until approximately 1.5 mm separation distance, after which the values converge.
claims.quote: For the LFP gas mixture, the breakdown voltage at room temperature (19 °C) is notably lower than it is at 200 and 300 °C until approximately 1.5mm separation distance. Around this point, the values converge and follow a similar trend.

claims.claim: For pure N2 at a flow velocity of 5 m/s, the breakdown voltage is lower at 200 and 300 °C than at room temperature throughout all separation distances tested, with no obvious convergence.
claims.quote: For pure N2, the opposite appears to be true. The breakdown voltage is lower throughout all of the separation distances for 200 and 300 °C, with no obvious convergence with the room temperature values at any point.

claims.claim: Individual elemental gases N2 and O2 have a noticeably lower breakdown voltage than a mixture of N2 and O2, suggesting synergistic effects increase breakdown strength in the mixture.
claims.quote: it can be seen from the general trend that the individual gases, N2 and O2 have a noticeably lower breakdown voltage value than that of the N2 and O2 mixture. One likely cause of this is the effect of gas mixture synergism.

claims.claim: At room temperature, the breakdown voltage of NCA gas (mixed with 80% N2) is higher than that of LFP gas (mixed with 80% N2) for the majority of electrode separation distances, but the opposite is true at 300 °C.
claims.quote: it can be seen that the breakdown voltage of NCA is higher than that of LFP for the majority of the separation distances at room temperature. Interestingly, the opposite is true for the two gas mixtures at 300 °C.

claims.claim: No distinguishable difference in breakdown voltage was observed between flow velocities of 2.5 m/s and 5 m/s for pure N2 at room temperature.
claims.quote: Flow velocities of 2.5 and 5 m/s were analyzed, with no distinguishable differences between the two velocities.

claims.claim: A flowing gas mixture representative of air (79% N2 and 21% O2) at 5 m/s exhibits a higher breakdown voltage at all electrode separation distances compared to still-standing air.
claims.quote: The flowing gas mixture exhibits a higher breakdown voltage at all distances, likely due to the previously mentioned effects.

claims.claim: Higher gas flow velocities are expected to promote movement of charged particles, enhance electron diffusion, and decrease the mean free path of electrons, thereby reducing ionization probability and requiring higher breakdown voltage.
claims.quote: Increasing the flow velocity is expected to promote the movement of charged particles and enhance electron diffusion within the gas medium... higher flow velocities tend to decrease the mean free path of electrons, meaning a shorter average distance between collision events with gas molecules [9]. This will reduce the amount of energy they are able to gain, reducing the probability of ionization and the formation of an ionized channel.

claims.claim: Electrode conditioning caused by high current warming the electrodes during continuous testing leads to a decrease in required breakdown voltage over time, resulting in inconsistent replicated measurements.
claims.quote: The most likely and most significant cause of this inconsistency is expected to be electrode conditioning. As the system is running and high levels of current pass through the electrodes, they begin to warm up. As the temperature of the conductive material increases, the required voltage to produce electrical breakdown decreases.

claims.claim: The first expulsion of vent gases in a Li-ion battery undergoing a thermal event usually occurs around 150 °C, and a second expulsion commonly occurs around 300 °C.
claims.quote: the first expulsion of vent gases in a Li-ion battery undergoing a thermal event usually occurs around 150 °C. A second expulsion of vent gases is common, usually occurring around 300 °C.

claims.claim: At stoichiometric conditions (Φ = 1), the flame kernel intensity from NCA gas ignition by electrical discharge at 300 °C and 5 m/s is higher and sustained longer compared to fuel-rich conditions (Φ = 3.5) where intensity dissipates very quickly.
claims.quote: Image 1 represents the stoichiometric condition, with an equivalence ratio of 1, and has the highest intensity for the longest. At Φ = 3.5, shown in image 3, the intensity of any luminosity dissipates very quickly.

claims.claim: When gas temperature is higher (300 °C vs. 150 °C), the intensity of both the flame kernel and the discharge are sustained longer, and the flame kernel propagates upward away from the discharge at higher speed.
claims.quote: When the gas temperature is higher, as seen in image 2, the intensity of both the flame kernel and the discharge are sustained longer... it can also be seen that the flame kernel at the higher temperature propagates upwards away from the discharge at higher speed.

claims.claim: Increasing gas flow rate decreases residence time in the quartz mixing tube, reducing the degree of premixing and shortening the duration of flame kernel intensity after ignition.
claims.quote: Increasing the flow rate will decrease the residence time, meaning they have less time in the quartz tube to mix... the duration of the intensity is the longest for this case, and shortest in the high velocity (15 m/s) case.

claims.claim: NCA gas mixture exhibits significantly higher and more sustained flame intensity after ignition compared to LFP gas mixture under identical flow conditions (300 °C, 5 m/s, Φ = 1), attributed to NCA's higher H2 content (42.8%) and lower CO2 content (10%) versus LFP's lower H2 (29.5%) and higher CO2 (48.4%).
claims.quote: the overall intensity shown in the frames is much higher and much more sustained in the NCA gas mixture (image 1) than in the LFP gas mixture (image 2)... the NCA gas mixture is comprised of 42.8 % H2 and 10% CO2, whereas the LFP mixture has 29.5% H2 and 48.4% CO2. Because CO2 is chemically inert, it does not readily react with other substances.

claims.claim: For NCA gas at 300 °C and 5 m/s, when the equivalence ratio is increased to Φ = 1.7, the flame propagates downstream for approximately 3 ms then propagates upstream back into the mixing tube (flashback), driven by a laminar flame speed sufficient to overcome the opposing gas flow.
claims.quote: When the equivalence ratio is increased to 1.7, the flame propagates downstream for a duration of about 3 ms, then begins to propagate upstream, back into the mixing tube. This behavior is known as flashback.

claims.claim: Under fuel-rich conditions (Φ = 3.5) with NCA gas, the flame is sustained in a diffusion-like mode with an elongated shape, because entrained air requires more time to dilute the fuel-enriched mixture below the upper flammability limit.
claims.quote: under these conditions, the flame is sustained in a diffusion-like mode. The entrainment of air from the surroundings needs more time to blend with the heavy fuel-enriched mixture to dilute the gas mixture to an equivalence ratio below the upper flammability limit. Consequently, the flame demonstrates this elongated shape.

claims.claim: Under fuel-rich conditions with NCA gas mixture, the sustainability of the flame is driven by H radicals rather than OH radicals, indicating combustion can be sustained even where there is a low amount of oxygen present.
claims.quote: The sustainability of a flame resulting from ignition of the NCA gas mixture is not driven by OH, but rather by H. This indicates the combustion process can occur and be sustained even where there is a low amount of oxygen present.

claims.claim: Current battery venting system designs are typically optimized for managing OH-driven combustion, and extinguishing methods typically depend on suffocating the flame by depriving it of oxygen, which may be hazardous against H-radical-driven NCA vent gas flames.
claims.quote: The current battery venting system designs are typically optimized for managing OH-driven combustion. Additionally, extinguishing methods of these battery fires typically depend on suffocating the flame, and depriving it of oxygen. Depending on the current battery venting system design, this can prove to be hazardous.

claims.claim: The high-speed camera operating at 150 kHz (6.42 microsecond exposure, 256×144 pixel resolution) was not fast enough to temporally resolve the initiation and evolution of the ignition process, which requires capture in the nanosecond time scale.
claims.quote: This implies the sampling rate of the imaging system was not fast enough to temporally resolve the initiation and evolution of the ignition process... It is recommended for further investigation that a camera capable of capturing images at higher framerates, likely in the nanosecond scale, be implemented.

claims.claim: There is no agreed-upon method of calculating the ionization coefficient α for gas mixtures, making it difficult to predict breakdown voltages of gases with different compositions.
claims.quote: there is no agreed-upon method of calculating α for gas mixtures, making it difficult to predict breakdown voltages of gases with different compositions.

claims.claim: The Paschen curve is applicable to gases in the low pressure range of approximately 10 to 1000 Torr, and plots minimum breakdown voltage as a function of the product of pressure and electrode separation distance.
claims.quote: A Paschen curve... plots the minimum breakdown voltage as a function of the product of pressure and distance between electrodes. This curve is applicable to gases in low pressure, in the range of approximately 10 to 1000 Torr [15].

claims.claim: For NCA gas at 300 °C and 5 m/s, increasing temperature from 150 °C to 300 °C changes the flame anchoring location from the electrodes downstream of the mixing tube to the outlet of the mixing tube, and the flame becomes more compact and centralized.
claims.quote: With an increase in temperature, the flame intensity and laminar flame speed increases. This can be seen immediately in Figure 4.12 when comparing the NCA gas mixture at 150 °C (image 1) and 300 °C (image 2), as the flame changes from being anchored at the electrodes to being anchored at the mixing tube. The flame also becomes more compact and centralized at higher temperatures.

claims.claim: The TR trigger temperature of LFP batteries is approximately 184°C, which is higher than that of NCM ternary batteries, which ranges from approximately 130–143°C across NCM523, NCM622, NCM811, and NCM905 variants.
claims.quote: The TR trigger temperature of LFP battery is about 180 °C, which is higher than that of the NCM ternary battery, which is around 130–140 °C.

claims.claim: Under standard conditions (25°C, 101 kPa), the normalized gas production of NCM ternary batteries ranges from 1.814 to 2.752 L/Ah, while LFP battery gas production is significantly lower at 0.569 L/Ah.
claims.quote: The gas production of the NCM battery is between 1.814–2.752 L/Ah after normalization, while the gas production of the LFP battery is significantly lower at 0.569 L/Ah.

claims.claim: In LFP battery TR gas production, H2 accounts for 50.82% of the gas composition, whereas CO accounts for only 9.3%, resulting in a lower lower flammability limit (LFL) compared to NCM battery TR gas.
claims.quote: In the gas production of LFP batteries, H2 accounts for 50.82%, while CO only accounts for 9.3%... The TR formation gas of the LFP battery contains a higher amount of H2 compared to the NCM battery, resulting in a lower LFL due to the relatively low LFL of H2.

claims.claim: Based on gas production volume, the degree of harm caused by thermal runaway is ranked as NCM905 > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > LFP, but based on gas composition flammability, the hazard ranking reverses to LFP > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM905.
claims.quote: Based on battery gas production, the degree of harm caused by TR can be ranked as follows: NCM9 0.5 0.5 > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > LFP... from the perspective of thermal runaway gas composition, the hazard ranking is LFP > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM9 0.5 0.5.

claims.claim: In NCM batteries, higher nickel content leads to a lower initial temperature of oxygen release and worse thermal stability, while the presence of Mn improves thermal stability.
claims.quote: In NCM batteries, Ni is the most unstable element, with higher nickel content leading to a lower initial temperature of oxygen release and worse thermal stability. The presence of Mn can improve thermal stability.

claims.claim: The main product of LFP battery TR is electrolyte with only a small amount of particulate matter, whereas the main product of NCM battery TR is particulate matter; the gas–solid ratio (RG−S) of LFP is 50.619 compared to 0.518–1.614 for NCM variants.
claims.quote: the main product of the LFP battery's TR is electrolyte, and only a small amount of particulate matter is produced... the main product of the NCM battery's TR is particulate matter... Table 6: LFP RG−S = 50.619; NCM523 = 1.614; NCM622 = 0.518; NCM811 = 1.034; NCM9 0.5 0.5 = 0.910.

claims.claim: The NCM ternary battery experiences a structural transition from a layered phase (space group R3m) to a disordered LiMn2O4 spinel and M3O4 spinel phase (space group Fd3m) within the temperature range of 350°C to 441°C during thermal decomposition.
claims.quote: the NCM ternary battery experiences a transition from its internal layered structure (with space group R3m) to a disordered LiMn2O4 spinel and M3O4 spinel phase (with space group Fd3m) within the temperature range of 350 °C to 441 °C.

claims.claim: There is no direct evidence that lithium iron phosphate (LFP) will decompose at high temperatures to release oxygen, in contrast to NCM cathode materials.
claims.quote: However, there is no direct evidence that lithium iron phosphate will decompose at high temperatures to release oxygen.

claims.claim: Above 260°C, the binder PVDF reacts with intercalated lithium to directly produce hydrogen, and this reaction occurs during TR of both NCM and LFP batteries.
claims.quote: Above 260 °C, the binder PVDF further reacts with intercalated lithium to directly produce hydrogen. This chemical reaction occurs during the TR of both NCM and LFP batteries.

claims.claim: For LFP batteries under lateral heating, the vent valve opened first causing a pressure surge without triggering TR, and TR only occurred approximately 300 seconds later, whereas for NCM batteries the vent valve opening and TR occurred simultaneously.
claims.quote: the vent value of the LFP battery opened first, resulting in a surge in pressure inside the AEC cabin, but TR did not occur, and the voltage remained stable at the operating voltage. It was only after approximately 300 s that TR occurred.

claims.claim: The heat release rate during thermal runaway of a single 18650 lithium-ion cell may be as high as 8.3 kW.
claims.quote: The heat release rate might be as high as 8.3 kW for one 18650 lithium-ion cell

claims.claim: Over 80% of venting gas species from lithium-ion batteries are H2, CO, CO2, CH4, and C2H4.
claims.quote: Sometimes, over 80% of them are H2, CO, CO2, CH4, and C2H4

claims.claim: The content of CO in venting gas increases at 100% SOC compared with 0% SOC.
claims.quote: Essl et al. found that the content of CO was increased at 100% SOC compared with 0% SOC

claims.claim: The maximum laminar flame speed of DMC is 300 mm/s at an equivalence ratio of 1.04.
claims.quote: A maximum flame speed of 300 mm·s−1 was recorded when the equivalence ratio was 1.04

claims.claim: The heat release rate of a pool fire of electrolyte EC/PC/EMC (25/25/50) decreases as the pressure decreases.
claims.quote: The heat release rate of this pool fire decreased as the pressure decreased

claims.claim: For a stoichiometric venting gas mixture in a 2D channel, the peak overpressure from the ignition pressure wave is 22.9 kPa, which is greater than the peak overpressure produced by the combustion wave at 15.6 kPa.
claims.quote: the peak overpressure from the ignition pressure wave is 22.9 kPa, which is greater than the peak overpressure produced by the combustion wave at 15.6 kPa at the initial stage of ignition

claims.claim: Increasing initial pressure from 81 kPa to 174 kPa increases maximum average flame velocity from 93.5 m/s to 151.5 m/s for a stoichiometric venting gas mixture at 329 K, without changing the combustion mode.
claims.quote: The maximum average flame velocities are 93.5 m·s−1, 100 m·s−1, 138.2 m·s−1, and 151.5 m·s−1, corresponding to an initial pressure of 81 kPa, 101 kPa, 154 kPa, and 174 kPa. A similar trend is displayed with the propagation time. However, no deflagration appears, and the combustion mode is not changed.

claims.claim: When the initial temperature is 400 K, the maximum flame velocity of venting gas combustion reaches 402.5 m/s, exceeding the local sonic speed, and the combustion mode changes from slow deflagration to fast deflagration.
claims.quote: When the initial temperature is 400 K, the flame propagation shows a different variation compared with the other three initial temperatures. The flame velocity exceeds the local sonic speed, and the deflagration mode occurs for the combustion of the venting gas mixture.

claims.claim: At an initial temperature of 400 K, the maximum overpressure from the ignition pressure wave is 48 kPa and the maximum overpressure from the combustion wave is 42 kPa, which is approximately twice the overpressure at 360 K.
claims.quote: The maximum overpressure of the ignition pressure wave at the initial temperature of 400 K is 48 kPa, and the maximum overpressure of the combustion wave is 42 kPa, which is twice the overpressure at the initial temperature of 360 K.

claims.claim: If the venting gas of a lithium-ion battery mixes with air at a temperature higher than 400 K, there is a high risk of fast deflagration and even explosion of the battery pack.
claims.quote: Therefore, if the venting gas of a lithium-ion battery mixes with the air and the temperature is higher than 400 K, there is a high risk of fast deflagration and even the explosion of the battery pack.

claims.claim: When the excess air coefficient decreases to approximately 0.9, the combustion mode of venting gas changes from slow deflagration to fast deflagration.
claims.quote: When the excess air coefficient is around 0.9, the combustion mode changes. When the excess air coefficient is decreased to 0.8, the average flame velocity has exceeded the local sonic speed, and the combustion mode has changed from slow deflagration to fast deflagration

claims.claim: The maximum flame velocity for venting gas combustion is approximately 445 m/s and is achieved at an excess air coefficient of 0.8 (slightly fuel-rich mixture).
claims.quote: The maximum velocity arrives at 445 m·s−1 when the excess air coefficient is 0.8.

claims.claim: At an excess air coefficient of 0.8, the peak overpressure caused by the ignition pressure wave is 61.7 kPa, which is less than the peak overpressure caused by the combustion wave of 91.3 kPa.
claims.quote: When the excess air coefficient is 0.8, the peak overpressure caused by the ignition pressure wave is 61.7 kPa, which is less than that caused by the combustion wave of 91.3 kPa.

claims.claim: Increasing initial pressure reduces flame front stability, causing wrinkles to appear earlier and more frequently, which increases the reaction surface area and accelerates flame front propagation.
claims.quote: The increase in the initial pressure will reduce the stability of the flame front. Under a high initial pressure, the wrinkles will occur earlier and more frequently on the flame front surface, and the roughness of the flame surface will increase, leading to an increase in the reaction surface area that accelerates the propagation of the flame front

claims.claim: The laminar flame velocity of venting gas combustion has an Arrhenius relationship with initial temperature, causing the reaction rate to increase exponentially with increasing initial temperature.
claims.quote: The reaction rate increases exponentially with the increase in the initial temperature because the activation energy normally is not influenced by the temperature.

claims.claim: This study only considers flame propagation in a 2D channel, and a more precise 3D simulation is needed for a more accurate evaluation.
claims.quote: This study only considers the propagation process in a 2D channel. In the future, a more precise 3D simulation needs to be performed to give a more precise evaluation.

claims.claim: The safe working temperature of lithium batteries should not exceed 80°C; if the temperature surpasses this, thermal runaway will occur.
claims.quote: the safe working temperature of lithium batteries should not exceed 80 ◦C. If the temperature surpasses this, thermal runaway will occur, which is a key issue that affects the safety of the batteries

claims.claim: For 50 Ah LFP batteries at 100% SOC, when the surface temperature reaches between 190–210°C, the temperature increase rate accelerates to 37°C/min and voltage decreases to zero instantly.
claims.quote: When the surface temperature of the battery reaches between 190–210 ◦C, the thermal regulation of the battery is activated, the temperature increase rate accelerates to 37 ◦C/min, and the voltage decreases to zero instantly.

claims.claim: For 50 Ah LFP batteries at 100% SOC, the maximum surface temperature reached during thermal runaway is approximately 320°C.
claims.quote: Eventually, the battery temperature reaches a maximum of around 320 ◦C.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway reaction in LFP batteries begins at approximately 160°C, which is the temperature at which the safety valve opens.
claims.quote: A tangent with multiple stages of temperature rise rate can be found in the vicinity of 160 ◦C at the junctures... the lithium iron phosphate battery valve opens before thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the LFP battery valve opened but no major secondary eruption occurred, whereas at 50% and 100% SOC, a second eruption occurred.
claims.quote: At 0% SOC, the battery valve was opened and there was no major secondary eruption. A second eruption occurred at 50% and 100% SOC, and the manifested traits are largely constant.

claims.claim: The severity of thermal runaway in LFP batteries increased with increasing SOC, with maximum temperatures ranked as 100% TMAX > 50% TMAX > 0% TMAX.
claims.quote: The severity of the thermal runaway increased with increasing SOC, as shown by the graphic in Figure 4, 100% TMAX > 50% TMAX > 0% TMAX.

claims.claim: NCA cells can reach maximum temperatures of 1075°C during thermal runaway, whereas LFP cells exhibited a less pronounced thermal runaway with maximum cell temperatures as high as 305°C.
claims.quote: charged NCA cells showed a drastic thermal runaway behavior. NCA cells can reach maximum temperatures of 1075 ◦C. In this study, LFP cells exhibited a less pronounced thermal runaway with maximum cell temperatures as high as 305 ◦C.

claims.claim: H2 accounts for approximately 50% of the normalized gas volume generated by LFP batteries during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: From the perspective of gas production, H2 accounts for a relatively high proportion of the gas generated by lithium iron phosphate batteries, approaching about 50%.

claims.claim: The 50 Ah LFP battery emits approximately 4.5 mol of gaseous pollutants during thermal runaway, with CO2, CH4, H2, and CO having the highest volume percentages.
claims.quote: The battery will emit around 4.5 mol of gaseous pollutants. CO2, CH4, H2, and CO are the gases with the highest volume %, as determined by the GC-MS apparatus, with CH4, H2, and CO being combustible and CO containing a specific level of toxicity.

claims.claim: The mass loss rate of 50 Ah LFP batteries after thermal runaway was 16% at 0% SOC, 17% at 50% SOC, and 18% at 100% SOC.
During the first valve opening of an LFP battery (160–200°C), the vent temperature drops sharply because the temperature of the ejected material is lower than the surrounding air temperature.
claims.quote: The temperature of the nozzle (Figure 4) exhibits a sharp decline at this point because the temperature of the material being ejected is lower than the surrounding air temperature.

claims.claim: The aluminum-shell battery safety valve shrapnel can typically resist pressures between 0.5 and 0.8 bar; when internal pressure exceeds this, the valve opens and releases flammable gas and electrolyte.
claims.quote: Typically, shrapnel can resist pressures between 0.5 and 0.8 bar. When the pressure produced by the reaction within the battery exceeds the pressure carried by the shrapnel, the safety valve will open and release flammable gas and a portion of the electrolyte

claims.claim: The secondary eruption of a 50 Ah LFP battery occurs approximately 20 minutes after the opening of the safety valve.
claims.quote: The secondary eruption of a lithium iron phosphate battery will occur around 20 min after the opening of the valve.

claims.claim: During the second eruption zone (200–300°C), a considerable quantity of gas is expelled from the LFP battery, the battery temperature reaches its maximum, and the gas quantity also reaches its maximum.
claims.quote: Second eruption zone: The temperature of the battery ranges from 200 to 300 ◦C. At this point, a considerable quantity of gas is expelled from the battery as a result of a strong reaction. At this point, the battery temperature reaches its maximum, and the quantity of gas also reaches its maximum and becomes steady.

claims.claim: During the first valve opening stage of LFP battery thermal runaway, the chamber pressure underwent its first abrupt increase, rising to 110 kPa/s.
claims.quote: the battery's surface temperature remains mostly unchanged, but the experimental chamber's interior pressure undergoes its first abrupt increase, rising to 110 kPa/s.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the normalized gas amount for NCM batteries ranges within 0.075–0.105 mol/Ah (1.68–2.35 L/Ah), while for LFP batteries it is only 0.025–0.035 mol/Ah (0.56–0.78 L/Ah), implying NCM batteries generate more than around 1–3 times the gases in moles compared to LFP batteries.
claims.quote: In regard to NCM batteries, the normalized gas amount varies within 0.075–0.105 mol/Ah (1.68–2.35 L/Ah), consistent with 0.09 mol/Ah [18] and 0.08 mol/Ah [17] to a certain degree, while that for LFP batteries is only 0.025–0.035 mol/L (0.56–0.78 L/Ah), close to 0.03 mol/Ah, found in previous work [17], implying that NCM batteries generate more than around 1–3 times the gases in moles compared to LFP batteries.

claims.claim: The maximum gas releasing rate shows a strongly positive and linear correlation with battery capacity for NCM batteries (0.04–0.31 mol/s), rather than with specific energy density, with a linear fit slope of 0.0023 and intercept of −0.0482.
claims.quote: the predicted values demonstrate a strongly positive correlation with battery capacity instead of specific energy density for either NCM or LFP batteries. This could be explained well by the sufficient reactants for larger capacities in terms of NCM or LFP cathodes. A linear fit to predict the maximum gas releasing rate is provided for NCM batteries as a function of capacity in Figure 4b. The slope of the linear fit is 0.0023 and the intercept is −0.0482.

claims.claim: For a 117 Ah prismatic NCM811 battery, a venting event occurring at elevated ambient temperatures (160–250 °C) produces approximately double the maximum gas releasing rate compared to room temperature, for both 50% SOC (0.374 mol/s vs. 0.185 mol/s) and 100% SOC (0.481 mol/s vs. 0.219 mol/s).
claims.quote: A venting event occurring at elevated temperatures tends to erupt more violently and increase by double, compared to either 0.374 mol/s under 160–250 degrees Celsius with 0.185 mol/s at room temperature for 50% SOC or 0.481 mol/s under 160–250 degrees Celsius with 0.219 mol/s at room temperature for 100% SOC.

claims.claim: At elevated ambient temperatures (160–250 °C), total gas production for NCM811 prismatic batteries grew around 10% and characteristic venting durations (t50, t90, t95, t99) reduced to approximately half the values measured at normal temperatures.
claims.quote: The gas production of both the 50% SOC case and the 100% SOC case grew around 10% at elevated temperatures, and the characteristic durations reduced to half the results at normal temperatures.

claims.claim: The venting process of NCM batteries terminates in only 2 or 3 minutes, while several or tens of minutes are necessary for LFP batteries to eject all gases.
claims.quote: Generally, the venting event disappears in only 2 or 3 min for NCM batteries, while several or tens of minutes are necessary for all gases to be ejected out of LFP batteries, suggesting a longer period to deal with the exhaust.

claims.claim: For batteries at 50% SOC, thermal runaway is not triggered when the safety valve first opens, but thermal runaway subsequently occurs after a few minutes causing a second gas injection; this two-injection behavior is common to both NCM and LFP batteries.
claims.quote: For 0% SOC and 50% SOC, thermal runaway is not triggered when the safety valve opens, leading to a long period to release generated gases and vaporize electrolytes. However, batteries with 50% SOC will enter into thermal runaway after a few minutes, and the second injection appears. This common phenomenon for both NCM and LFP batteries should be noted for hazard suppression regarding fire accidents.

claims.claim: The burst pressure of safety valves is approximately 1.2 MPa for cylindrical batteries and roughly 0.5 MPa for prismatic batteries; a venting event occurs when the battery internal pressure minus external pressure exceeds the burst pressure.
claims.quote: The burst pressure of the safety valve installed on battery shells is around 1.2 MPa for cylindrical batteries and roughly 0.5 MPa for prismatic batteries [22,32]. If the internal pressure of the battery minus the external pressure is over the burst pressure, a venting event could happen.

claims.claim: The ideal gas law is not applicable during the initial violent ejection phase of a venting event due to an extremely uneven temperature field, causing the calculated gas amount to deviate from the actual curve; the quasi-equilibrium assumption is only valid when the derivative of gas amount is below 0.1 mol/s and declining monotonously.
claims.quote: However, there was a non-equilibrium stage due to extremely uneven temperature fields caused by the violent ejections, where the ideal gas law was not applicable... the hypothesis was approximately held in this study if the derivative of the gas amount was lower than 0.1 mol/s and declined in a continuously monotonous trend.

claims.claim: For NCM523 batteries at 75% SOC, the maximum gas releasing rate was highest at the lowest tested ambient pressure (0.02 MPa, 0.062 mol/s) and decreased at higher pressures (0.049 mol/s at 0.1 MPa, 0.048 mol/s at 0.26 MPa), while total gas amount increased with pressure.
claims.quote: Venting events occurring under a relatively high pressure give off more gases, except with the NCM622 battery at 0.1 MPa, which could be mainly influenced by internal reactions. Both NCM523 and NCM622 batteries ejected more violently at the lowest pressure level in this study.

claims.claim: A positive correlation of total gas amount and maximum gas releasing rate with SOC was confirmed for both LFP and NCM811 batteries, with the difference between 0% SOC and 50% SOC being more prominent for NCM811 than LFP due to NCM811's higher energy density.
claims.quote: A positive correlation of total gas amount and maximum gas releasing rate with SOC was satisfied for both LFP and NCM811 batteries. The above parameters had a prominent difference between 0% SOC and 50% SOC for NCM811 batteries compared with LFP batteries as a result of the extremely high energy density of NCM811 over LFP.

claims.claim: For NCA 18650 cells subjected to thermal abuse at 5°C/min with 100% SoC, the maximum temperature and pressure reached at the TR peak were 511 ± 96°C and 5.071 barg, compared to 381 ± 89°C and 1.802 barg for LTO, and 406 ± 62°C and 2.202 barg for LFP.
claims.quote: The maximum temperatures and pressures reached at the peak were 511 ± 96 ◦C and 5.071 barg for NCA, 381 ± 89 ◦C and 1.802 barg for LTO and 406 ± 62 ◦C and 2.202 barg for LFP.

claims.claim: For LFP cells subjected to thermal abuse at 5°C/min with 100% SoC in air, CO is entirely converted to CO2, with CO concentration being negligible (nil ppm) and CO2 concentration at 1.65 × 10^5 ppmv, attributed to the higher stability of the LFP cathode material which remains stable up to 500°C.
claims.quote: For LFP cells, CO is entirely converted to CO2, with the CO concentration being negligible (nil ppm) and a CO2 concentration at 1.65 × 105 ppmv. This behavior is attributed to the higher stability of the LFP cathode material, which remains stable up to 500 ◦C [24], a temperature not reached even during TR (maximum cell surface temperature around 310 ◦C).

claims.claim: HF concentrations measured over a normalized 30-minute period during thermal abuse tests at 100% SoC exceeded the IDLH limit of 30 ppm for all three cell chemistries tested (NCA: 49 ± 1 ppm, LTO: 101 ± 12 ppm, LFP: 110 ± 23 ppm), with LFP exceeding the limit by an order of magnitude.
claims.quote: The data in Table 5 indicate that, regardless of the chemical composition, the concentration values exceed the IDLH values of 30 ppm for HF [31] and 1200 ppm for CO [30] over a 30 min period as set by NIOSH. In the worst case, the HF emissions from the LFP cell exceed the limit by an order of magnitude.

claims.claim: CO concentrations measured over a normalized 30-minute period during thermal abuse tests at 100% SoC exceeded the IDLH limit of 1200 ppm for all three cell chemistries (NCA: 7564 ± 705 ppm, LTO: 9018 ± 131 ppm, LFP: 6060 ± 826 ppm).
claims.quote: The data in Table 5 indicate that, regardless of the chemical composition, the concentration values exceed the IDLH values of 30 ppm for HF [31] and 1200 ppm for CO [30] over a 30 min period as set by NIOSH.

claims.claim: The liquid ejected from LFP cells during thermal abuse was identified as ethylene carbonate (EC), and its presence as a liquid is attributable to the cell surface temperature not exceeding 350°C during TR, which prevented complete evaporation of EC (boiling point approximately 244°C).
claims.quote: The liquid ejected from the LFP was identified as ethylene carbonate (EC), the solvent with the highest boiling temperature among the carbonates in the electrolytic solution, at approximately 244 ◦C. The presence of this liquid is attributable to the fact that the surface temperature of the cell in question did not exceed 350 ◦C during the TR event. This prevented the evaporation of the entirety of the EC present in the solution.

claims.claim: NCA cells subjected to thermal abuse at 5°C/min with 100% SoC exhibited a mass loss of 17.39 ± 1.38 g, significantly greater than LTO (8.80 ± 0.00 g) and LFP (8.88 ± 4.94 g), accompanied by ejection of 4.010 g of solid metallic powder versus no solid ejection from LTO.
claims.quote: a greater loss of material, calculated as the different between the initial weight and the final weight of the Li-ion cell (indicated with ∆cell), was observed for NCA cell, with a value of 17.39, compared to 8.80 g and 8.88 g for and LTO and LFP cells... the quantity of residues ejected is also higher for NCA compared to LTO (4.010 g vs. 0.131 g)

claims.claim: The amount of HF quantified from 18650 cylindrical cells in this study is lower than reported in the literature for pouch and prismatic cells, even after normalization to kWh, attributed to the lower mass of internal components in 18650 cells (approximately 45 g) compared to pouch cells (600–900 g).
claims.quote: the amount of HF quantified in this work is lower than reported in the literature. The discrepancy in the quantity of HF can be attributed to the different geometries and dimensions of the cells employed in this work, which were 18650 cylindrical, in comparison to those documented in the literature, such as pouch and prismatic... an 18650 cell weighs approximately 45 g, whereas a pouch cell weighs between 600 and 900 g.

claims.claim: In thermal abuse tests conducted in air versus N2, the TR onset and maximum temperature values were more influenced by the feed gas than by the heating rate, because emitted organic compounds from the electrolyte undergo combustion in air, resulting in higher temperature values than in N2 where combustion is constrained.
claims.quote: the TR onset and maximum temperature values were found to be more influenced by the feed gas employed in the tests than by the heating rate. Indeed, following the venting process, the emitted gases may undergo a chemical reaction with the surrounding atmosphere. In the case of air, the elevated temperature values observed are influenced by the combustion of organic compounds from electrolyte, emitted by the cell. In N2, combustion reactions are significantly constrained by the oxygen production resulting from the degradation of cathode active materials.

claims.claim: The LEL of battery venting gas for LFP batteries is approximately 6%–7.5%, while that for NCM batteries is in the range of 8% to 11.5%, demonstrating higher deflagration risk for LFP batteries.
claims.quote: The LEL of BVG for LFP batteries is around 6%–7.5%, while that for NCM batteries is in the range of 8% to 11.5%. The result demonstrates that the explosion hazards of BVG for LFP batteries are higher than that for NCM batteries.

claims.claim: Among NCM batteries, higher nickel content corresponds to a higher LEL value of venting gas, meaning increased energy density reduces deflagration risk from the perspective of gas explosibility.
claims.quote: it is found that NCM batteries with higher nickel content have a higher LEL value of BVG, which means that the increased energy density reduces the deflagration risk from the perspective of gas explosibility.

claims.claim: A single micro-overcharge of 50 mV above the upper cut-off voltage has little effect on the LEL value of battery venting gas for NCM cells.
claims.quote: In addition, a single micro-overcharge has little effect on the LEL value of BVG.

claims.claim: The volume proportion of H2 in LFP battery venting gas ranges from 27% to 52%, which is much larger than that for NCM batteries at 12%–20%.
claims.quote: The amount of H2 for LFP batteries varies from 27% to 52%, much larger than that for NCM batteries (12%–20%).

claims.claim: The total proportion of H2 and hydrocarbon gases in battery venting gas is negatively correlated with energy density (slope coefficient of −0.21), while the total proportion of CO2 and CO is positively correlated with energy density (slope coefficient of 0.21).
claims.quote: The fitting line shows that the total proportion of H2 and hydrocarbon gases is negatively related to the energy density with a slope coefficient of −0.21. While for the total proportion of CO2 and CO, it demonstrates a positive correlation with battery energy density, and the slope of the fitting line is 0.21.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature (Tmax) during thermal runaway of NCM cells ranges from 555 to 1066°C, while that of LFP cells is in the range of 307–525°C under adiabatic conditions at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the Tmax of NCM cells ranges from 555 to 1066°C, while that of LFP cells is in the range of 307–525°C.

claims.claim: A single micro-overcharge leads to lower TR tolerance and lower TR hazards in NCM cells, attributed to consumption of internal active materials including active lithium and electrolyte.
claims.quote: a single micro-overcharge reduces the value of Tmax, as reported by Ref. [39]. This is due to the consumption of internal active materials, including active lithium and electrolyte.

claims.claim: The TR triggering temperature T2 for LFP cells is between 190 and 220°C, while T2 for NCM cells is in the range of 140–175°C, indicating that cathode material type significantly affects T2.
claims.quote: the T2 for LFP cells is between 190 and 220°C, while the T2 for NCM cells is in the range of 140–175°C... the T2 of NCM batteries is lower than that of LFP batteries, indicating that the type of cathode material significantly affects T2.

claims.claim: H2 detection during LFP battery overcharging occurred 271 seconds earlier than smoke detection and 579 seconds before thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: The detection of H2 occurred 271 s earlier than smoke and 579 s before thermal runaway onset. Therefore, capturing characteristic gases like H2 enables early warning of battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: For a 50 Ah LFP battery, the thermal runaway process releases about 4.5 mol of gas, primarily consisting of CO2, CH4, H2, and CO.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway process releases about 4.5 mol of gas, primarily consisting of CO2, CH4, H2, and CO as shown in Figure 1.

claims.claim: Fully charged NCA batteries undergoing thermal runaway when heated reach a maximum temperature of 1075 °C and release up to 317 mmol of gas, whereas fully charged LFP batteries have a peak temperature of only 448 °C with significantly less gas released.
claims.quote: Golubkov et al. found that fully charged NCA batteries undergo particularly violent thermal runaway when heated, reaching a maximum temperature of 1075 °C and releasing up to 317 mmol of gas. In contrast, fully charged LFP batteries experience milder thermal runaway, with a peak temperature of only 448 °C and significantly less gas released.

claims.claim: When SOC is reduced to 0%, thermal runaway does not occur in NCA or LFP batteries even when heated to around 250 °C, demonstrating that both battery types require charging to trigger thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the SOC is reduced to 0%, thermal runaway does not occur even when heated to around 250 °C, demonstrating that both NCA and LFP batteries require charging to trigger thermal runaway.

claims.claim: 50% SOC LFP batteries do not produce H2 during thermal runaway, while the generation of highly toxic CO and HF increases with SOC.
claims.quote: Liu et al. found that 50% SOC LFP batteries do not produce H2 during thermal runaway, while the generation of highly toxic CO and HF increases with SOC.

claims.claim: LiPF6 begins decomposing at approximately 80 °C, releasing toxic gases such as HF and flammable gases like CO, CH2, and C2H4.
claims.quote: Kerr et al. determined that LiPF6 begins decomposing at approximately 80 °C. Once decomposition occurs, it releases toxic gases such as HF and flammable gases like CO, CH2, and C2H4.

claims.claim: For a 280 Ah LiFePO4 prismatic cell, thermal runaway intensity markedly escalated when SOC surpassed 50%, with peak battery temperatures of 92.3 °C, 251.1 °C, and 330.7 °C and gas emission volumes of 95.8 L, 275.7 L, and 713.1 L at 25%, 50%, and 75% SOC respectively.
claims.quote: The findings revealed a marked escalation in TR intensity when SOC surpassed 50%. Comparative analysis of 25%, 50%, and 75% SOC conditions revealed that the peak battery temperatures were 92.3 °C, 251.1 °C and 330.7 °C, accompanied by respective gas emission increments of 95.8 L, 275.7 L and 713.1 L.

claims.claim: The lower flammability limit (LFL) of battery venting gas decreases with higher SOC, primarily due to the increasing proportion of H2 in BVG at elevated SOC, since H2's high combustibility reduces the overall LFL.
claims.quote: Notably, the lower flammability limit (LFL) decreases with higher SOC, consistent with observations in the literature. This trend primarily stems from the increasing proportion of H2 in BVG at elevated SOC (its high combustibility reduces the overall LFL).

claims.claim: NCA batteries have a lower lower flammability limit (LFL) and wider flammability range (RF) for post-thermal runaway gases compared to LFP batteries, resulting in a higher flammability hazard index.
claims.quote: Wang et al. discovered that compared to LFP batteries, NCA batteries have a lower lower flammability limit (LFL) and wider flammability range (RF) for post-thermal runaway gases, resulting in a higher flammability hazard index.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway hazard ranking by cathode material is NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM111 > LiFePO4, with NCM811 being the most hazardous.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway hazard ranking: NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM111 > LiFePO4, with NCM811 being the most hazardous among these lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: The transition from 1 MPa to 3 MPa safety valve rupture pressure triggers a dramatic surge in H, O, and OH radical formation rates during battery venting gas combustion, confirming that elevated rupture pressures intensify chain branching reactions and hasten combustion.
claims.quote: The transition from 1 MPa to 3 MPa pressure triggers a dramatic surge in radical formation rates across all species, characterized by more pronounced peak intensities. At 3 MPa, the ROP maxima for H, O, and OH radicals attain their highest values, signifying the most active chain reaction pathways. This confirms that elevated rupture pressures intensify the BVG chain branching reactions, thereby hastening combustion.

claims.claim: At environmental pressure of 30 kPa, the explosion limit range of thermal runaway gases from lithium-ion batteries at 100% SOC is 8.01% to 53.35%, wider than the range of 8.89% to 46.81% at 101 kPa, demonstrating that lower environmental pressure widens the explosion limit range.
claims.quote: At an environmental pressure of 30 kPa, the explosion limit ranges for thermal runaway gases of lithium-ion batteries at 25% SOC and 100% SOC were 6.58% to 24.38% and 8.01% to 53.35%, respectively. At 101 kPa, the ranges were 6.08% to 22.58% for 25% SOC and 8.89% to 46.81% for 100% SOC.

claims.claim: The CAFT method's standard critical temperature of 1573 K for hydrocarbon mixtures is unsuitable for hydrogen-containing mixtures, limiting the CAFT method's applicability in calculating explosion limits for thermal runaway gases.
claims.quote: In standard CAFT applications, the critical temperature for hydrocarbon mixtures is set at 1573 K. However, this temperature is unsuitable for hydrogen-containing mixtures, limiting the CAFT method's applicability in calculating explosion limits for thermal runaway gases.

claims.claim: A chemical predictive model for lithium-ion battery thermal runaway predicted gas release of 2.79 L/Ah compared to experimentally measured 2.39 L/Ah, and could predict peak temperatures with an error of ±15 °C and onset temperatures with ±10 °C for batteries with capacities ≥15 Ah.
claims.quote: The study found that the model could predict peak temperatures (with an error of ±15 °C) and onset temperatures (±10 °C) for all batteries with capacities ≥15 Ah. Experimentally measured gas release during thermal runaway was 2.39 L/Ah, whereas the model predicted 2.79 L/Ah.

claims.claim: At a DMC concentration of 0.3%, DMC exerts a net promotion effect on BVG ignition, but when DMC content surpasses this threshold, continued augmentation triggers a systematic increase in Minimum Ignition Energy and reduction in Adiabatic Flame Temperature, indicating a transition from promotion to inhibition.
claims.quote: At low dimethoxy methane (DMC) concentrations (0.3%), incremental DMC addition results in diminished Minimum Ignition Energy (Emin) and elevated Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Tad). Conversely, when DMC content surpasses this threshold, continued augmentation triggers a systematic Emin increase and Tad reduction. This demonstrates that DMC exerts a mild promotion effect on ignition below 0.3% concentration, transitioning to inhibition at higher levels.

claims.claim: Overcharging at 2 C produced the highest total heat generation among charging rates tested (0.5 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C), and H2 and CO content increased significantly with charging rate while CO2 decreased, with lower explosion limit decreasing at higher charging rates.
claims.quote: Overcharging at 2 C posed the highest thermal runaway risk, with the total heat generation being the highest... Additionally, H2 and CO content increased significantly with charging rate, while CO2 decreased. The lower explosion limit (LEL) of mixed gases also decreased with higher charging rates, indicating elevated explosion risks at high rates.

claims.claim: In every instance where abusive conditions were terminated upon initial hydrogen detection, thermal runaway was effectively mitigated, validating trace hydrogen sensing as an actionable early-warning mechanism.
claims.quote: In every instance where abusive conditions were terminated upon initial hydrogen detection, thermal runaway was effectively mitigated. These conclusions validate trace hydrogen sensing as an actionable early-warning mechanism for thermal runaway prevention.

claims.claim: For nail-penetration-triggered thermal runaway in Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2 batteries, a critical SOC threshold at 70% triggered flame jetting phenomena, with flame propagation rates exceeding 50% SOC values by tenfold.
claims.quote: A critical threshold at 70% SOC triggered flame jetting phenomena, with flame propagation rates exceeding 50% SOC values by tenfold.

claims.claim: For LFP batteries, H2 content is higher than CO during thermal runaway, whereas for NCM batteries CO and CO2 account for a larger proportion, and higher Ni content leads to greater total CO and CO2 content and lower H2 content.
claims.quote: In terms of gas production, for LFP batteries, H2 content is higher than CO, whereas for NCM batteries, CO and CO2 account for a larger proportion, and the higher the Ni content, the greater the total content of CO and CO2, and the lower the H2 content.

claims.claim: Internal pressure in 18650-type NCA batteries rises with SOC during thermal runaway, reaching up to 4.25 bar at 100% SOC, and at higher SOC levels heat from internal gas-phase reactions dominates.
claims.quote: The study indicated that internal pressure rises with SOC (reaching up to 4.25 bar at 100% SOC), and at higher SOC levels, heat from internal gas-phase reactions dominates.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the SIB pressure relief valve opens at a positive electrode tab temperature of 51.09°C, the NCM valve opens at 75.3°C, and the LFP valve opens at 107.51°C, with LFP subsequently undergoing thermal runaway at 150.47°C.
claims.quote: when the positive electrode tab temperature reaches 51.09°C (t = 549 s), the battery pressure relief valve opens... When the positive electrode tab temperature reaches 75.3°C (t = 800 s), the NCM pressure relief valve opens... For LFP, the pressure relief valve opens at a positive electrode tab temperature of 107.51°C (t = 2310 s), and TR occurs as the temperature continues to rise to 150.47°C

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the instantaneous chamber pressure during thermal runaway is 535.82 kPa for SIB, 637.0 kPa for NCM, and 168.3 kPa for LFP, making SIB and NCM significantly higher than LFP.
claims.quote: the chamber pressure increases from 102.4 to 535.82 kPa... the chamber pressure increases from 103.5 to 637.0 kPa... with chamber pressure increasing from 102.3 to 168.3 kPa

claims.claim: Steady-state gas production during thermal runaway at 100% SOC is 127.93 L for LFP, 347.48 L for NCM, and 362.98 L for SIB, corresponding to per-ampere-hour values of 0.64, 1.74, and 1.81 L/Ah respectively.
claims.quote: the steady-state gas production volumes of LFP, NCM, and SIB during TR are 127.93, 347.48, and 362.98 L, respectively, with gas production per ampere-hour values of 0.64, 1.74, and 1.81 L/Ah

claims.claim: LFP generates significantly higher H2 content (48.90%) during thermal runaway compared to NCM (22.66%) and SIB (21.23%), because the stronger reducibility of Li+ compared to Na+ promotes more H2 generation via reduction of H+ in the binder.
claims.quote: the H2 content in gases generated by LFP TR is as high as 48.90%, far exceeding that of NCM (22.66%) and SIB (21.23%). This is due to the stronger reducibility of Li+ compared to Na+, which promotes more H2 generation via reduction of H+ in the binder

claims.claim: LFP exhibits a lower LEL (6.17%) and higher UEL (54.57%) compared to NCM (LEL=7.30%, UEL=49.88%) and SIB (LEL=8.71%, UEL=49.97%), indicating LFP poses a greater explosion hazard than NCM and SIB.
claims.quote: compared with NCM (LEL = 7.30%, UEL = 49.88%) and SIB (LEL = 8.71%, UEL = 49.97%), LFP exhibits a lower LEL (6.17%) and a higher UEL (54.57%)... Therefore, LFP poses a greater explosion hazard than NCM and SIB

claims.claim: The SEI layer formed in sodium-conducting electrolytes of SIB contains a higher proportion of organic compounds than in LIB, making it more sensitive to temperature and causing SIB to be more prone to thermal runaway in low-temperature environments or over short time scales.
claims.quote: the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer formed in sodium-conducting electrolytes contains a higher proportion of organic compounds, making it extremely sensitive to temperature changes... the solubility of the SEI film in SIB is higher than in LIB, meaning SIB are more prone to thermal runaway (TR) in low-temperature environments or over short time scales

claims.claim: The olivine structure of LFP (LiFePO4) features strong covalent bonding, with positive electrode decomposition temperatures exceeding 200°C, and its decomposition involves only lithium ion deintercalation and lattice reconstruction without oxygen release, fundamentally suppressing early TR initiation.
claims.quote: the olivine structure of LFP (LiFePO4) features strong covalent bonding, with positive electrode decomposition temperatures exceeding 200°C. Its decomposition process involves only lithium ion deintercalation and lattice reconstruction without oxygen release, fundamentally suppressing early TR initiation

claims.claim: In NCM, high temperatures cause positive electrode material decomposition and oxygen release which reacts vigorously with organic electrolyte, producing large quantities of CO, CO2, and hydrocarbons, and this oxidative pathway is the primary cause of higher gas volume and heat release in NCM.
claims.quote: The released oxygen reacts vigorously with the organic electrolyte, triggering combustion—like oxidation reactions that produce large quantities of CO, CO2, and hydrocarbons. This oxidative pathway is the primary cause of the higher gas volume and heat release observed in NCM

claims.claim: The comprehensive safety performance scores using the AHP framework are 71.27 for LFP, 76.79 for NCM, and 80.23 for SIB, with SIB exhibiting higher overall safety performance primarily due to higher LEL and UEL weightings in the evaluation.
claims.quote: from the perspective of comprehensive scores, SIB exhibits higher safety performance than NCM and LFP. This is primarily because the evaluation places greater emphasis on the two indicators of LEL and UEL

claims.claim: CO content in NCM (24.21%) and SIB (27.28%) thermal runaway gas is significantly higher than LFP (11.95%), and CO2 content in NCM (32.64%) and SIB (35.64%) is significantly higher than LFP (24.71%), primarily because oxygen in NCM and SIB cathodes is more readily released than in the LFP cathode.
claims.quote: the CO (24.21% and 27.28%) and CO2 (32.64% and 35.64%) produced by NCM and SIB during TR are comparable and significantly higher than those of LFP (11.95% for CO and 24.71% for CO2). This difference primarily arises because oxygen in the cathodes of NCM and SIB is more readily released than that in the LFP cathode

claims.claim: HF PAC-1, PAC-2, and PAC-3 concentrations for 1 hour exposure are 1, 24, and 44 ppm, respectively.
claims.quote: HF is a highly toxic gas. The concentrations to reach PAC-1, -2, and -3 are 1, 24, and 44 ppm, respectively.

claims.claim: Evaporation of 24.7 mL of DMC in a 62 m³ enclosed space can produce an atmosphere at PAC-2 concentration, causing irreversible or serious health effects.
claims.quote: the evaporation of 24.7 and 42.6 mL of the common electrolyte components DMC and DEC, respectively, can lead to an atmosphere in which irreversible or other serious health effects and impaired ability to take protective action (PAC-2 concentration) can occur.

claims.claim: A single commercial Li-ion cell used in a widely deployed medium-sized BEV contains 18.4 ± 0.6 mL of free electrolyte, meaning one leaking cell can be sufficient to create a toxic atmosphere in an enclosed space.
claims.quote: Experimental quantification of free electrolyte in commercial Li-ion cells revealed that a single cell used in a widely deployed medium-sized BEV contained 18.4 ± 0.6 mL of free electrolyte. Hence, already one leaking cell in a battery pack can be sufficient to create a toxic atmosphere in an enclosed space.

claims.claim: POF3, resulting from the reaction of LiPF6 with water, can react with organic carbonate electrolyte solvents to form phosphorofluoridates with neurotoxicity comparable to chemical warfare agents sarin and soman.
claims.quote: Phosphoryl fluoride (POF3) resulting from the reaction of LiPF6 with water (see Equation (1)) can react with the electrolyte solvent (organic carbonate) to form phosphorofluoridates. Those compounds are known for their neurotoxicity. Due to their structural similarity, their toxicological effect is comparable to that of chemical warfare agents such as sarin and soman.

claims.claim: GC performance equipped with a PID detector is affected by temperature and humidity changes, leading to a concentration bias of up to 47% relative to the reference concentration of benzene at extreme humidity (30 g cm⁻³ absolute humidity).
claims.quote: The performance of GCs that were equipped with a PID was reported to be affected by temperature and humidity changes of the environment leading to a concentration bias of up to 47% to the reference concentration of benzene in a location with extreme humidity (30 g cm−3 absolute humidity).

claims.claim: Halogen compounds are well-known interferents for catalytic bead sensors that cause poisoning of the precious metal surface, requiring several hours for the sensor to recover.
claims.quote: Halogen compounds are well-known interferents which lead to poisoning of the precious metal surface and it takes the sensor several hours to recover.

claims.claim: MOS sensors have a gas desorption time of up to 45 minutes and are not specific to individual organic compounds, making VOC detection difficult especially in the presence of NO, NO2, or CO.
claims.quote: Further disadvantages are that the gas desorption time can be up to 45 min and that this type of sensor is not specific to individual organic compounds. Especially in the presence of NO, NO2, or CO, the detection of VOCs is rather difficult.

claims.claim: HF has an ionization energy of 16.05 eV and is therefore not detectable with conventional PID lamps.
claims.quote: HF has an ionization energy of 16.05 eV and is therefore not detectable with conventional PID lamps.

claims.claim: A MEMS-MOS based sensor integrated in the battery module detected battery electrolyte leakage with a response time of 35 seconds from leakage start to detection.
claims.quote: A defect cell cover, a subsequent battery leakage, and evaporation of the electrolyte were successfully detected through the decrease in resistance of the sensor. The response time was 35 s from leakage start to detection.

claims.claim: No commercially available off-the-shelf solution was identified among the presented set of devices for the detection of electrolyte vapors during in-use scenarios (driving, parked, and charging).
claims.quote: it becomes clear that there is no commercially available off-the-shelf solution among the presented set of devices for the detection of electrolyte vapors (i.e., not gases coming from venting) during in-use (driving, parked, and charging).

claims.claim: NIOSH requires the lower limit of detection for HF measurement at 10 m open path to be 0.93 ppm and the maximum detectable concentration to be 15.8 ppm.
claims.quote: For HF, lower limit of detection and the maximum concentration at 10 m path length are required to be 0.93 and 15.8 ppm, respectively.

claims.claim: Detector tubes for the detection of HF are widely commercially available with measurement ranges including 0.25 to 100 ppmv per ASTM D4490, but detector tubes for the detection of organic carbonates are not on the market.
claims.quote: So far, detector tubes for the detection of organic carbonates are not on the market... For HF, a measurement range from 0.25 to 100 ppmv is stated.

claims.claim: The average cell blow-up temperature across all tested pouch cells (12–25 Ah, NMC622/graphite) was 96.5 °C, and the average venting temperature was 133.3 °C, with little deviation from these averages and no notable influence of cell capacity on either event.
claims.quote: The average temperatures, visualized with dashed lines, are 96.5 °C for the blow-up event and 133.3 °C for the venting event. For both events, there is little deviation from the average values and no notable inﬂuence of the cell capacity.

claims.claim: Cells with a capacity of at least 15 Ah transited to rapid thermal runaway (defined as self-heating rate ≥20 °C min⁻¹ over ≥2 s) at temperatures between 200 and 209 °C in experiments, while simulations slightly over-predicted onset temperatures at 209–214 °C.
claims.quote: Except for cell 12.2, all cells transit to rapid thermal runaway at temperatures between 200 and 209 °C in the experiments. The simulations slightly over-predict the onset temperature, ranging from 209 to 214 °C.

claims.claim: The simulation model predicted peak temperatures of all cells with a capacity of 15 Ah or more within a maximum deviation of 15 °C, but 12 Ah cells showed notable deviations of 42.9 and 96.6 °C.
claims.quote: Overall for all tested cells with a capacity of at least 15 Ah there is an excellent agreement in peak temperature between experiments and simulations with a maximum deviation of 15 °C. The 12 Ah type cells however show notable deviations of 42.9 and 96.6 °C.

claims.claim: For the 17 Ah cell, a lower electrolyte mass fraction caused earlier depletion of organic solvent during thermal runaway, which reduced the fraction of active material undergoing decomposition and led to a lower peak temperature compared to higher-capacity cells.
claims.quote: For cell 17.1 the deviation can be explained by the lower mass fraction of electrolyte (see Table 3). As the organic electrolyte is a main reactant in both anode and cathode decomposition reactions, its depletion leads into a signiﬁcant diminution of heat release. Analysis of composition data in the simulations shows that a smaller fraction of active material undergoes decomposition reaction before depletion of the solvent compared to the other cells.

claims.claim: Simulations of five cell types with energy densities ranging from 393 to 558 Wh L⁻¹ showed that higher-power (lower energy density) cells released more specific gas (up to 2.82 L Ah⁻¹) than high-energy cells (as low as 2.23 L Ah⁻¹), and exhibited a higher CO₂/CO ratio and higher hydrocarbon fraction due to lower peak temperatures shifting the water–gas shift equilibrium.
claims.quote: The higher-power cell type E has the highest speciﬁc gas release with 2.82 L Ah⁻¹ while energy cell type A releases the lowest amount of gas with 2.23 L Ah⁻¹... the CO2/CO ratio is shifted more toward CO2 compared to the high-energy cells. This is explained by the lower peak temperature which inﬂuences the equilibrium of the water–gas shift, according to reaction Equation (13).

claims.claim: LiF becomes the dominant species on the surface of anode active materials at temperatures above 120 °C, as detected by XRD measurements, and is regarded as the main inhibiting species for the lithiated graphite–electrolyte decomposition reaction.
claims.quote: Haik et al. detected with X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements that LiF becomes the dominant species on the surface of anode active materials at temperatures above 120 °C. Therefore, we regard LiF as the main inhibiting species for reaction Equation (1).

claims.claim: Solvent evaporation in an open system becomes significant at temperatures starting at around 130 °C, well below the boiling point of ethylene carbonate at 248 °C, and pouch cells must be treated as open systems in this temperature range due to opening from overpressure.
