claims.quote: The boiling point of ethylene carbonate is 248 °C, but Kriston et al. showed that solvent evaporation in an open system becomes signiﬁcant at much lower temperatures starting at around 130 °C. As pouch cells are inclined to open due to overpressure during the heating process, they have to be considered an open system in the relevant temperature range.

claims.claim: In a 10-foot ESS cabinet combustion experiment, the maximum smoke outflow velocity recorded by an anemometer placed above the enclosure opening was 33.93 m/s.
claims.quote: Measurements recorded by the anemometer placed above the enclosure opening indicated a maximum airflow velocity of 33.93 m/s

claims.claim: In a 10-foot ESS cabinet combustion experiment, the highest temperature measured reached 898 °C.
claims.quote: the highest temperature measured reached 898 °C

claims.claim: Hydrogen released during lithium-ion battery thermal runaway has a Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) of 0.019 MJ, making it easily ignitable.
claims.quote: Hydrogen is of particular concern due to its wide flammability range and extremely low Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE), cited as 0.019 MJ, rendering it easily ignitable.

claims.claim: In confined ESS environments, the introduction of air after accumulation of flammable gases from incomplete combustion—such as upon opening access doors—can cause the mixture to enter its flammable range, potentially resulting in deflagration or flashover if an ignition source is present.
claims.quote: Subsequent introduction of air, for instance, upon opening access doors, can lead to the mixture entering its flammable range, potentially resulting in deflagration or flashover phenomena if an ignition source exists.

claims.claim: Gas produced during thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries was estimated at 1.96 L/Ah, with a composition of H₂: 42.8%, CO: 37.1%, CO₂: 10.0%, and CH₄: 3.0%.
claims.quote: Based on literature, gas production was estimated at 1.96 L/Ah [18], resulting in a simulated total gas volume of 3,885,504 L. The gas composition was H₂: 42.8%, CO: 37.1%, CO₂: 10.0%, CH₄: 3.0%

claims.claim: Under summer atmospheric conditions (28.5°C, humidity 84.0%, wind speed 1.88 m/s, stability class A), the farthest Lower Flammability Limit (LFL, 2.7%) dispersion distance from an uncontrolled ESS cabinet fire reached 54.22 m at a height of 23.96 m.
claims.quote: Dispersion modeling revealed that the farthest Lower Flammability Limit (LFL, 2.7%) dispersion distance reached 54.22 m at a height of 23.96 m

claims.claim: Fireball thermal radiation from an ESS cabinet fire reached distances of 89.35 m, 50.33 m, and 26.85 m for intensity levels of 4, 12.5, and 37.5 kW/m², respectively.
claims.quote: Fireball radiation reached distances of 89.35 m, 50.33 m, and 26.85 m for intensity levels of 4, 12.5, and 37.5 kW/m², respectively

claims.claim: Jet fire thermal radiation from an ESS cabinet fire reached distances of 63.99 m, 52.32 m, and 45.31 m for intensity levels of 4, 12.5, and 37.5 kW/m², respectively.
claims.quote: Jet fire distances for corresponding intensities were 63.99 m, 52.32 m, and 45.31 m

claims.claim: Explosion overpressure from an ESS cabinet fire reached distances of 302.38 m, 58.77 m, and 44.06 m for overpressure intensities of 0.02068, 0.1379, and 0.2068 bar, respectively.
claims.quote: Overpressure impacts reached distances of 302.38 m, 58.77 m, and 44.06 m for intensities of 0.02068, 0.1379, and 0.2068 bar, respectively

claims.claim: Without protective measures, thermal radiation around the ESS cabinet remained below 10 kW/m² within 1.2 meters horizontally and up to 3.5 meters vertically, typically ranging between 5 and 8.1 kW/m².
claims.quote: without protective measures, thermal radiation remained below 10 kW/m² within 1.2 meters horizontally and up to 3.5 meters vertically around the cabinet, typically ranging between 5 and 8.1 kW/m²

claims.claim: In open-field conditions, the downwind thermal radiation impact from an ESS cabinet fire did not exceed 6 meters, but lack of proper venting could lead to physical explosion hazards due to gas expansion.
claims.quote: In open-field conditions, the downwind thermal radiation impact did not exceed 6 meters; however, the lack of proper venting could lead to physical explosion hazards due to gas expansion.

claims.claim: The summer atmospheric scenario produced the greatest dispersion distance due to weaker vertical mixing, which favors longer-range gas transport.
claims.quote: The summer scenario displayed the greatest dispersion due to favorable conditions for longer-range gas transport, including weaker vertical mixing.

claims.claim: FDS simulation results indicated that smoke and thermal radiation from an ESS cabinet fire did not impede evacuation paths when the distance from the cabinet to the building exceeded 10 meters, with the nearest cabinet located 12 meters from the building and 16 meters from the building exit.
claims.quote: Simulation results indicated that the smoke and thermal radiation from the cabinet did not impede evacuation paths due to the distance exceeding 10 meters

claims.claim: The Gateway Energy storage facility fire, initiated by thermal runaway, continued for 11 days and was not extinguished by traditional firefighting methods but was allowed to burn out after consuming all available fuel.
claims.quote: The fire continued for 11 days and required continuous efforts from over 40 firefighters and five fire engines. Ultimately, the blaze was not extinguished by traditional firefighting methods but was allowed to burn out after consuming all available fuel.

claims.claim: The peak HRR for a 1.4 MWh ESS cabinet in the worst-case full combustion scenario was estimated at 5 MW, derived by applying a 50% reduction to the calculated ternary lithium battery HRR of 9,743 kW to account for fire-retardant containment structures.
claims.quote: the HRR per storage cabinet was calculated as HRR = 2 × EB^0.6 kW = 9,743 kW. Due to the independent design of the battery cabinets and the implementation of fire-retardant containment structures designed to suppress fire spread, half of the calculated HRR value for ternary lithium batteries was adopted, yielding a peak HRR of 4,871 kW. Therefore, the peak HRR for this scenario was rounded to 5 MW.

claims.claim: The average thermal runaway gas mixture from Li-ion batteries, determined by averaging 40 compositions from the literature, contains seven components: C3H8 (0.007 mole fraction), C2H6 (0.019), C2H4 (0.027), CH4 (0.119), H2 (0.144), CO (0.168), and CO2 (0.516).
claims.quote: The final mixture is composed of C3H8, C2H6, C2H4, CH4, H2, CO, and CO2... C3H8: 0.007, C2H6: 0.019, C2H4: 0.027, CH4: 0.119, H2: 0.144, CO: 0.168, CO2: 0.516

claims.claim: CH4 concentration in Li-ion battery thermal runaway gases varies between 0.12 and 73 vol% across different studies, and H2 varies between 0 and 35.68 vol%.
claims.quote: CH4 varies between 7310 and 0.12 vol %,9 H2 between 09 and 35.68 vol %,11 etc.

claims.claim: The laminar flame speed of the average TRG mixture reaches a maximum of 23.25 cm/s at an equivalence ratio of approximately 1.1, at 1 atm and 298 K initial temperature.
claims.quote: the laminar flame speed of the TRG mixture presents the classical shape observed with hydrocarbons and passes by a maximum (23.25 cm/s) at an equivalence ratio of around 1.1

claims.claim: Increasing the equivalence ratio of the TRG mixture from 0.5 to 1.0 increases ignition delay time by a factor of approximately 2 at high temperature and 1.5 at low temperature, with similar factors observed when increasing from 1.0 to 2.0.
claims.quote: Increasing the equivalence ratio from 0.5 to 1.0 increases the ignition delay time between a factor of about 2 (high temperature) and 1.5 (low temperature). Similar factors are observed when increasing the equivalence ratio from 1.0 to 2.0.

claims.claim: For fuel-rich conditions (ϕ = 2.0), only the NUIGMech 1.1 model accurately predicts the ignition delay time of the TRG mixture within 20–25%, while other models (JetSurf II and GRI3.0) show over-reactivity with differences of about 55%.
claims.quote: For the fuel-rich data (Figure 5c), the prediction difference among the models is larger, and the NUIG 1.1 model is the only one that is relatively accurate in predicting the data (within 20−25%). The other models present an over-reactivity (too short of an ignition delay time), especially for the JetSurf II and GRI3.0 models with a difference of about 55% with the data.

claims.claim: In very dilute mixtures (99% Ar), differences in reaction rate coefficients between models have a greater effect on predicted reactivity than in fuel/air mixtures, because the large exothermicity of fuel/air conditions masks kinetic differences.
claims.quote: In fuel/air mixtures, at least in the high-temperature domain, the kinetics are driven by the large exothermicity induced by the large fuel/O2 concentration. In very dilute mixtures, this exothermicity is strongly mitigated and the reactivity is therefore more sensitive to differences between the reaction pathways, if any, and the reaction rate coefficients selected.

claims.claim: At temperatures as low as 800–850 K, the most recent kinetics models predict ignition delay times for the TRG mixture in the 3–5 second range, with no change in the observed trends.
claims.quote: calculations with the most recent models38,49 did not show any change in the trends seen in Figure 5 for temperatures as low as 800−850 K, giving ignition delay times in the 3−5 s time range.

claims.claim: The GRI3.0 model overpredicts the laminar flame speed of the TRG mixture by about 7.5% at the peak value across the entire equivalence ratio range investigated, while JetSurf II underestimates it above ϕ = 0.9 by up to about 5%.
claims.quote: The GRI3.0 model is the only one that overpredicts the data (over the entire range of equivalence ratios investigated) by about 7.5% at the peak value. The other model considered (JetSurf II) tends to underestimate the flame speed above ϕ = 0.9, by up to about 5%

claims.claim: Water formation in dilute TRG oxidation chiefly occurs via H2 + OH ⇆ H + H2O (R6), followed by CH4 + OH ⇆ CH3 + H2O (R3) and C2H4 + OH ⇆ C2H3 + H2O (R7), with hierarchical contribution following the mole fraction order of the fuel components, except H2 which contributes disproportionately due to H-abstraction producing H2.
claims.quote: the formation of water chiefly occurs via H2 + OH ⇆ H + H2O (R6) followed by CH4 + OH ⇆ CH3 + H2O (R3) and C2H4 + OH ⇆ C2H3 + H2O (R7)... their hierarchical contribution to the amount of water formed follows the same order as for the mole fraction of the fuel components. Note that the contribution from R6 (H2 + OH ⇆ H + H2O) is disproportionally large compared to the relative mole fraction of H2 with the other fuels. This large contribution from R6 is due to the H-abstraction reactions from H radicals, such as CH4 + H ⇆ CH3 + H2 (R4), which produce H2.

claims.claim: The NUIGMech 1.1 model achieves the lowest average error on ignition delay time predictions across all equivalence ratios (9.1% average), compared to GRI3.0 (10.5%), AramcoMech 1.3 (13.1%), JetSurf II (14.7%), and CRECK 2003 (30.1%).
claims.quote: GRI3.0: 10.5, JetSurf II: 14.7, AramcoMech 1.3: 13.1, CRECK 2003: 30.1, NUIGMech 1.1: 9.1

claims.claim: The laminar flame speed of the TRG mixture correlates linearly with the peak net heat production according to NUIGMech 1.1 model predictions, with fuel-rich data points systematically falling slightly below the linear fit and fuel-lean data slightly above it.
claims.quote: the laminar flame speed increases in a linear fashion with the peak, net heat production, and a linear fit can be applied... the fuel-rich data (the equivalence ratio corresponds to the number above each point) are mixed with fuel-lean data in Figure 12. However, it is interesting to see that the fuel-rich data are typically slightly under the line defined by the fit, whereas the fuel-lean mixtures tend to be slightly above the fit.

claims.claim: The average TRG mixture studied is not representative of any real TRG composition because TRG composition varies widely with many parameters including electrolyte composition, electrode nature, state of charge, failure environment, and battery aging.
claims.quote: it can be concluded that the average mixture defined herein is, in a way, not representative of any real TRG composition... since the TRG composition varies widely from one study to the next

claims.claim: H2 is released earlier than CO and CO2 in LFP batteries under overcharge conditions.
claims.quote: Wang et al. (2024a) discovered that H2 is released earlier than CO and CO2 in LFP batteries under overcharge conditions.

claims.claim: CO, CO2, and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) are emitted from LIBs prior to the onset of thermal runaway under both overcharge and over-discharge conditions.
claims.quote: Zhang et al. (2024b) demonstrated that CO, CO2, and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) were observed to be emitted from LIBs prior to the onset of thermal runaway under both overcharge and over-discharge conditions.

claims.claim: During early-stage thermal runaway events, gaseous signatures emerge earlier than voltage, current, or temperature anomalies, while providing more precise indications of incipient failure.
claims.quote: Notably, during early-stage thermal runaway events, gaseous signatures emerge earlier than voltage, current, or temperature anomalies, while providing more precise indications of incipient failure.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway events in LFP batteries, H2 dominated gas composition at 36.34%, followed by CO2 at 25.24%, total olefins at 18.87%, total alkanes at 9.94%, CO at 7.39%, with residual gases at 0.73%.
claims.quote: during thermal runaway events in LFP batteries, the characteristic gas composition was quantified as follows: H2 dominated at 36.34%, followed by CO2 at 25.24%, total olefins at 18.87%, total alkanes at 9.94%, CO at 7.39%, with residual gases accounting for 0.73% of the total emissions.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway events in NCM811 batteries, CO constituted the predominant component at 49.26%, followed by H2 at 25.06%, CO2 at 18.08%, CH4 at 5.22%, C2H4 at 1.62%, with residual gases at 0.76%.
claims.quote: During thermal runaway events in NCM811 batteries, the gaseous emission profile exhibited the following predominant composition: CO constituted the predominant component at 49.26%, followed by H2 at 25.06%, CO2 at 18.08%, CH4 at 5.22%, C2H4 at 1.62%, with residual gases accounting for 0.76% of the total emissions.

claims.claim: In the initial reaction phase of LIB thermal runaway, thermal decomposition of the SEI layer at elevated temperatures liberates minor quantities of C2H4, CO2, and O2.
claims.quote: This process is primarily attributed to the thermal decomposition of the SEI layer at elevated temperatures, liberating minor quantities of C2H4, CO2, and O2.

claims.claim: During the gas ejection phase, when thermal runaway progression reaches extreme temperatures above 200°C, electrolyte decomposition approaches completion through a self-accelerating oxidative decomposition mechanism, generating significant quantities of H2, CO2, and CO.
claims.quote: as the thermal runaway progression reaches extreme temperatures (>200°C), the electrolyte decomposition approaches completion through a self-accelerating oxidative decomposition mechanism. This process not only sustains olefin evolution but also generates significant quantities of H2, CO2 and CO.

claims.claim: Hydrogen evolution in LIB thermal runaway predominantly occurs during Stage III, where elevated temperatures trigger intense exothermic chain reactions that persist for 20–100 s.
claims.quote: Within the thermal runaway mechanism of LIBs, hydrogen evolution predominantly occurs during Stage III, where elevated temperatures trigger intense exothermic chain reactions that persist for 20–100 s.

claims.claim: A ZIF-8-loaded Ag/ZnO nanofiber-based H2 gas sensor detected hydrogen leakage 67.79 s prior to LIB bulge.
claims.quote: This sensor demonstrated real-time safety warning capabilities, detecting hydrogen leakage 67.79 s prior to LIBs bulge.

claims.claim: A Ce-doped MoS2 H2 gas sensor demonstrated capability to detect LIB thermal runaway events within 26 s of their initiation through real-time hydrogen monitoring.
claims.quote: Hou et al. (2025) successfully synthesized a Ce-doped MoS2 H2 gas sensor through hydrothermal synthesis method, demonstrating the capability to detect LIBs thermal runaway events within 26 s of their initiation through real-time hydrogen monitoring.

claims.claim: A Bi2O3 nanosheet-structured DMC gas sensor achieved a 50 ppb detection limit with greater than 15-minute preemptive warning capability for LFP battery thermal management.
claims.quote: Pan et al. (2024) established a novel Bi2O3 nanosheet-structured DMC gas sensor through hydrothermal synthesis, attaining exceptional sensitivity (50 ppb detection limit) with >15-min preemptive warning capability for LFP battery thermal management.

claims.claim: The incorporation of Au species into CeO2-based materials reduced the optimal operating temperature for CO detection from 600°C to 450°C.
claims.quote: Comparative experimental investigations revealed that the incorporation of Au species improved the CO gas-sensing performance of CeO2-based materials, resulting in enhanced stability of baseline resistance and response profiles during CO exposure, while reducing the optimal operating temperature for CO detection from 600°C to 450°C.

claims.claim: The absence of redox transformations during CO2 adsorption induces metastable surface binding configurations, which fundamentally limits electron transfer efficiency and consequently reduces detectable signal amplitudes in low-concentration regimes.
claims.quote: The absence of redox transformations during CO2 adsorption induces metastable surface binding configurations, which fundamentally limits electron transfer efficiency and consequently reduces detectable signal amplitudes in low-concentration regimes.

claims.claim: MOF-derived ZnO exhibited higher sensitivity toward H2 compared to CO and H2S, arising from the preferential reduction of ZnO to metallic Zn with higher charge density upon interaction with H2 at operating temperatures, whereas no analogous reactions occurred when exposed to CO or H2S.
claims.quote: Experimental results revealed that the MOF-ZnO exhibited higher sensitivity toward H2 compared to the other two gases. This enhanced response arises from the preferential reduction of ZnO to metallic Zn with higher charge density upon interaction with H2 at operating temperatures, whereas no analogous reactions occurred when exposed to CO or H2S.

claims.claim: Volatile electrolyte components (DMC, EC, DEC) primarily vaporize during the initial stage of thermal runaway with limited gas emission, requiring sensors to respond to ultra-low concentrations at ppb-level.
claims.quote: since vaporization primarily occurs during the initial stage of thermal runaway with limited gas emission, sensors are required to respond to ultra-low concentrations (ppb-level) of electrolytic vapors.

claims.claim: A p-n SnO-SnO2 heterojunction composite demonstrated a response of 1.07 toward 1,000 ppm CO2 with a sub-1-second response time at room temperature.
claims.quote: This architecture demonstrated a 1.07 response toward 1,000 ppm CO2 with a sub-1-s response time.

claims.claim: An Ag@Ag2O-SnO2 sensor demonstrated a response value of 106 toward 100 ppm DMC at an operating temperature of 250°C, with a lower detection limit of 11.76 ppb.
claims.quote: At an operating temperature of 250°C, the sensor demonstrated a response value of 106 toward 100 ppm DMC, accompanied by response/recovery times of 28/55 s and a lower detection limit of 11.76 ppb.

claims.claim: CO exhibits stronger reducibility than H2, yet semiconductor materials exhibit superior gas-sensing response toward H2 because redox reactions with hydrogen generally exhibit higher reaction intensity compared to those with other gases.
claims.quote: It is well-established that CO exhibits stronger reducibility than H2, yet the superior gas-sensing response of materials toward H2 stems from their intrinsic capacity to undergo redox reactions with hydrogen, which generally exhibit higher reaction intensity compared to those with other gases.

claims.claim: In the PHEV A (SUV-type) full-scale fire experiment, visible flames appeared at the chassis approximately 63 minutes after the external short-circuit triggered thermal runaway, and the chassis flames evolved into a passenger compartment fire in 9 minutes 11 seconds.
claims.quote: Under these experimental conditions, chassis flames of SUV-type PHEV moved to the front passenger compartment in 9 min 11 s

claims.claim: In the PHEV B (sedan-type) full-scale fire experiment, chassis flames developed for 9 minutes 56 seconds before advancing to the rear passenger compartment, and the fire was less intense than the SUV-type in both flame form and flame temperature.
claims.quote: The combustion phase lasted 9 min 56 s for PHEV B and 9 min 11 s for PHEV A. Although the burning duration of PHEV B was longer than that of PHEV A, the fire of PHEV B was less intense, both in terms of flame form and flame temperature.

claims.claim: The maximum external flame temperature recorded during the PHEV fire experiments was 843.6°C, occurring in the chassis flame zone (Zone γ), and the maximum compartment flame temperature was 696.8°C.
claims.quote: The PHEVs that entered the combustion phase for around 9 min 30 s had a maximum external flame temperature of 843.6°C and a maximum compartment flame temperature of 696.8°C.

claims.claim: White smoke released by the thermally-runaway battery pack before visible flames appeared lasted approximately 60 minutes, and when this combustible gas mixture accumulated at the chassis and encountered a spark, it caused a gas-phase explosion.
claims.quote: The PHEVs released a large amount of white smoke before flames appeared, which lasted for approximately 60 min. The white smoke originated from the battery pack that was experiencing TR. Combustible gases were the main component of the smoke. When the gas encountered a spark or flame, it exploded.

claims.claim: At a horizontal distance of 1 m from the burning PHEV A, the maximum radiative heat flux measured was 1.151 kW/m², which is below the skin exposure tolerance limit of 2.5 kW/m².
claims.quote: As the fire experiments were conducted in an open space, PHEV fires produced a maximum heat flux of 1.151 kW/m² to objects at a horizontal distance of 1 m from it... the radiant heat emitted by the PHEV fires did not exceed the tolerance limit for exposure of skin [2.5 kW/m²]

claims.claim: The low car body of the sedan-type PHEV (PHEV B) limited flame development in the vertical direction, and the restricted fire was also less able to spread horizontally, whereas the SUV-type trunk provided more combustibles for upward fire propagation.
claims.quote: The low car body of PHEV B limited the development of flames in the vertical direction. The restricted fire was also less able to spread horizontally. For PHEV A, its SUV-style trunk provided more combustibles for the fire that tended to move upward.

claims.claim: The armor plate of the battery pack or vehicle frame provided fire resistance that prevented chassis flames from propagating directly upward through the chassis floor into the vehicle interior; instead, fire entered the car by igniting door sealing rubber or through cracks between car doors and the frame.
claims.quote: It indicates that the armor plate of the battery pack or the vehicle frame had a certain degree of fire resistance, which could prevent flames or heat from igniting the vehicle's interior floor. Chassis flames did not propagate directly upward through the chassis but entered the car by igniting the door sealing rubber or through the crack between car doors and its frame.

claims.claim: EOL lithium-ion batteries retain residual states of charge of up to 100% in some cases, along with flammable and toxic electrolytes and their vapors.
claims.quote: Waste from spent or EOL-LIBs remains highly active due to residual states of charge (SOC) of up to 100% in some cases, along with flammable and toxic electrolytes and their vapors.

claims.claim: Ethylene carbonate (EC) has a flash point of 143°C, boiling point of 238°C, and lower/upper flammability limits of 3.6/16.1 vol.% in air.
claims.quote: Ethylene carbonate (EC) C3H4O3 143/238 3.6/16.1

claims.claim: Dimethyl carbonate (DMC) has a flash point of 18°C, boiling point of 90°C, and lower/upper flammability limits of 4.2/12.9 vol.% in air.
claims.quote: Dimethyl Carbonate (DMC) C3H6O3 18/90 4.2/12.9

claims.claim: A higher boiling point of an electrolyte solvent correlates with reduced flammability and enhanced thermal stability.
claims.quote: Generally, a higher boiling point (BP) correlates with reduced flammability and enhanced thermal stability; therefore, solvents with higher BPs are preferable for improving battery safety.

claims.claim: Inhalable particles of nickel, cobalt, and manganese compounds smaller than 10 µm have potential carcinogenic effects.
claims.quote: Of particular concern is the potential for inhalable particles of nickel, cobalt, and manganese compounds (<10 µm) to have carcinogenic effects.

claims.claim: A battery is no longer suitable for EV applications once its state of health (SOH) drops below 80%.
claims.quote: Typically, a battery is no longer suitable for EV applications once its SOH drops below 80%.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in LIBs begins with SEI layer breakdown at temperatures between 90–120°C, releasing gases such as C2H4, CO2, and O2.
claims.quote: As temperature between 90~120 ◦C, the thermal runaway reaction process begins with the breakdown of the thin SEI layer. The exothermic reaction release gases such as C2H4, CO2 and O2, fueling the subsequent stages of the runaway event.

claims.claim: At approximately 100°C, heat-induced reactions between organic solvents and the anode with intercalated lithium produce flammable gases including C2H4, C3H6, and C2H6.
claims.quote: At temperature around 100 ◦C, heat-induced reactions between organic solvents and the anode with intercalated lithium produce flammable gases such as C2H4, C3H6 and C2H6.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, EOL-LIBs exhibit a specific gas production of 2.10–2.17 L/Ah, mass loss of 75–78%, thermal runaway onset temperature of approximately 148–150°C, and maximum temperature of approximately 612–614°C.
At approximately 130% overcharge SOC, EOL-LIBs exhibit a thermal runaway onset temperature of approximately 80–104°C, which is lower than at 100% SOC.
There is a positive correlation between increasing SOC and the severity of the thermal runaway hazard in EOL-LIBs.
claims.quote: The data clearly demonstrate a positive correlation between increasing SOC and the severity of the thermal runaway hazard.

claims.claim: Smoke from LIB thermal runaway is classified as a hybrid mixture—a suspension of combustible particles in a flammable gases/air medium—and can cause an explosion even when the concentration of each individual component remains below its respective explosibility threshold (LEL for gases, MEC for dusts).
claims.quote: A critical safety implication of this hybrid mixture is that its combination can lead to an explosion even when the concentration of each individual component—gas or dust—remains below its respective explosibility threshold, specifically the Lower Explosion Limit (LEL) for gases and the Minimum Explosion Concentration (MEC) for dusts.

claims.claim: Thermal pretreatment of LIBs must maintain temperature below the melting point of aluminum (660°C) to avoid triggering the thermite reaction, which could destroy cathode materials and release significant heat.
claims.quote: A crucial safety constraint for any thermal pretreatment is maintaining the temperature below the melting point of aluminum (660 ◦C). Exceeding this temperature risks triggering side reactions, such as the thermite reaction, which could destroy the cathode materials and release significant heat.

claims.claim: Thermal decomposition of LiPF6 produces highly reactive phosphorus pentafluoride (PF5), which further reacts with moisture to form POF3 and HF.
claims.quote: Toxic fluoride gases are generated primarily from the decomposition of the LiPF6 electrolyte (see Equation (1)). This decomposition yields PF5 gas, which is highly reactive and triggers a chain reaction when exposed to moisture or high temperatures. The subsequent formation of hydrogen fluoride (HF) and the hydrolysis of PF5 into the highly toxic phosphoryl fluoride (POF3) represent significant hazards.

claims.claim: Degradation of PVDF binders above 400°C yields fluorohydrocarbons and PFOA during LIB recycling.
claims.quote: The degradation of PVDF binders above 400 ◦C yields fluorohydrocarbons and PFOA.

claims.claim: Pyrolysis of NCM materials above 300°C releases toxic nickel/cobalt oxide dust.
claims.quote: Pyrolysis of NCM materials above 300 ◦C releases toxic nickel/cobalt oxide dust.

claims.claim: HF exposure can cause respiratory necrosis at concentrations over 3 ppm.
claims.quote: Exposure of LiPF6 to water/moisture forms highly corrosive HF, which can cause respiratory necrosis at concentrations over 3 ppm.

claims.claim: Black mass can contain fine aluminum powder in concentrations of up to 6% by mass, significantly elevating the risk of dust explosions.
claims.quote: In addition, fine aluminum powder may be present in concentrations of up to 6% by mass. Both components significantly elevate the risk of dust explosions due to the fine-grained and complex nature of the black mass.

claims.claim: An ignition energy of 20 kJ produced an overpressure exceeding 0.6 MPa at a black mass concentration of 300 g/m3, while ignition energies of 10 kJ or 2 kJ were insufficient to trigger an explosion in tested black mass samples.
claims.quote: one study showed that an ignition energy of 20 kJ produced an overpressure exceeding 0.6 MPa at a concentration of 300 g/m3. Conversely, ignition energies 10 kJ or 2 kJ were insufficient to trigger an explosion in the tested black mass samples.

claims.claim: Small black mass samples of 8 mL can reach temperatures over 600°C due to self-heating, as confirmed by Grewer oven tests.
claims.quote: Experimental results from Grewer oven tests confirm the severity of this hazard, demonstrating that even small samples (8 mL) can reach temperatures over 600 ◦C due to self-heating.

claims.claim: Recycling-related LIB fires increased by 187% from 2020 to 2024, with 63% of incidents attributed to inadequate deactivation and 28% to black mass handling errors.
claims.quote: According to UL Solutions incident data, recycling-related LIB fires increased by 187% from 2020 to 2024, with 63% of incidents attributed to inadequate deactivation and 28% to black mass handling errors.

claims.claim: EOL-LIBs retain 20–80% of their power, maintaining significant electrochemical activity and thermal safety risks during storage, transportation, dismantling, and post-processing.
claims.quote: These batteries retain 20–80% of their power, maintaining significant electrochemical activity and thermal safety risks during storage, transportation, dismantling, and post-processing.

claims.claim: Fully discharging cells to 0 V can induce oxidation of the copper anode current collector, causing copper ions to dissolve into the electrolyte and deposit on the cathode, compromising the purity of recovered cathode materials.
claims.quote: fully discharging cells to 0 V can induce the oxidation of the copper anode current collector; the resulting copper ions may dissolve into the electrolyte and subsequently deposit on the cathode. This contamination compromises the purity of recovered cathode materials, thereby limiting their potential for reuse in the production of new batteries.

claims.claim: Hydrometallurgical processing leaching efficiency and kinetics are improved when thermal treatment is conducted between 500°C and 600°C.
claims.quote: The removal of organics improves leaching efficiency and kinetics, especially when thermal treatment is conducted between 500 ◦C and 600 ◦C, thereby avoiding uneconomically high operating costs.

claims.claim: In the Umicore shaft furnace pyrometallurgical process, the pre-heating zone operates below 300°C to evaporate electrolytes safely and mitigate explosion risk, while the smelting and reduction zone operates at 1200–1450°C to yield metal alloys.
claims.quote: Pre-heating zone (<300 ◦C): This upper zone evaporates electrolytes safely, mitigating the risk of explosions... Smelting and reduction zone (1200–1450 ◦C): In the bottom zone, smelting and reduction reactions occur, yielding a metal alloy rich in copper, cobalt, nickel, and iron.

claims.claim: The energy release from LIB incidents can reach 22,000 MJ/ton for standard household battery packs.
claims.quote: The substantial energy release, which can reach 22,000 MJ/ton for standard household packs, poses a significant threat.

claims.claim: Aging-induced degradation in EOL-LIBs reduces the battery's thermal stability and lowers the onset temperature of exothermic reactions, increasing the risk of thermal runaway compared to new batteries.
claims.quote: These factors collectively reduce the battery's thermal stability and lower the onset temperature of exothermic reactions, thereby increasing the risk of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Aluminum in black mass reacts with water to produce hydrogen gas, and can also react with HF to produce hydrogen gas and aluminum fluoride.
claims.quote:
Al + 3H2O → 1.5 H2 + Al(OH)3
2Al + 2HF → H2 + 2AlF

claims.claim: At high SOC with sufficient oxygen present, the white vapor vented during LIB thermal runaway often ignites immediately, resulting in high-intensity jet fires.
claims.quote: At a high SOC and sufficient oxygen present, this vapor often ignites immediately, resulting in high-intensity jet fires.

claims.claim: In environments with low SOC or inadequate oxygen during LIB thermal runaway, accumulated vapor can lead to flash fire or vapor cloud explosion if the gas mixture reaches its explosive limit in highly confined areas.
claims.quote: In environments with a low SOC or inadequate oxygen (e.g., when vapor displaces air or fire suppressant are deployed), immediate ignition may not occur. Instead, the accumulation of the vapor can lead to: flash fire (a sudden ignition, especially in a confined space) or vapor cloud explosion (a more catastrophic scenario that occurs if the accumulated gas mixture reaches its explosive limit in highly confined areas).

claims.claim: Single cells vent gases faster and more violently than those failed in an array, causing damaged cells to look very different.
claims.quote: Single cells vent gases faster and more violently than those failed in an array. This causes damaged cells to look very different.

claims.claim: In 5 Ah pouch cell single-cell experiments, the rapid temperature increase associated with thermal runaway (dT/dt exceeding 100°C/sec) occurred when the cell/heater interface temperature was between 185°C and 196°C.
claims.quote: In all three tests, the time of this rapid temperature increase occurred when the cell / heater interface temperature was between 185 ◦C and 196 ◦C.

claims.claim: For 5 Ah pouch cell arrays, the average total cell-to-cell thermal runaway propagation time was 15.4 seconds, comprising a preheat time of 4.3 seconds and a vent time of 11 seconds.
claims.quote: Each set of statistics comes from a sample of 13 cells... Array Preheat (sec) 5 Ah 4.3 ... Vent (sec) 11 ... Total (sec) 15.4

claims.claim: For 10 Ah pouch cell arrays, the average total cell-to-cell thermal runaway propagation time was 20.6 seconds, comprising a preheat time of 7.4 seconds and a vent time of 13.2 seconds, with the venting time being 40% longer than that of 5 Ah cells.
claims.quote: Based on the pressure data, the propagation time was approximately 22 seconds for the first 8 cells on average. Of this, pressure increased for 14 seconds and decreased for 8 seconds... This indicates that the venting time was 40 % longer than that of the 5 Ah cells while the delay between venting and thermal runaway propagation was 60 % longer.

claims.claim: Cells failing due to thermal runaway propagation from adjacent cells have less internal damage and release gas over a greater surface area compared to slowly heated cells, which show localized regions of heavy damage where jets of hot material exit the cell.
claims.quote: Heater failed cells have localized regions of heavy damage, while cells failed due to propagation have a more uniform damage throughout... Examination shows that the propagated cells have less damage inside and gas is released out of a greater surface area.

claims.claim: A single 94 Ah NMC prismatic cell released 221.6 L of dry gas at standard pressure and temperature during thermal runaway, equivalent to 0.64 liters of gas per Watt-hour of energy stored.
claims.quote: The cell released 221.6 L of dry gas at standard pressure and temperature. Normalized by the nominal energy capacity of the cell, the cell produced 0.64 liters of gas per Watt-hour of energy stored.

claims.claim: In a closet explosion experiment with a single 94 Ah cell, a partial volume deflagration occurred and broke the closet door when the volume of gas released was less than 2% of the closet volume, well below the 9% required to reach LFL in a well-mixed room.
claims.quote: Based on the release rate time history, this means that the explosion happened after the battery had released less than 50 L of the 221 L expected to be released. This volume of gas is less than 2% of the room volume. This value is well below the 9% required to reach LFL in a well mixed room. However, this deflagration was able to occur because it there was a localized region with a gas mixture within the flammability limits.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC battery vent gas from 21 published compositions, the mean lower flammability limit is 10%, mean upper flammability limit is 47%, and mean maximum laminar burning velocity is 0.48 m/s.
claims.quote: Lower Flammability Limit... Mean 10%... Upper Flammability Limit... Mean 47%... Burning Velocity... Mean 0.48 m/s

claims.claim: The maximum explosion pressure from vented deflagration models is most sensitive to laminar burning velocity and turbulent flame acceleration parameters (β and R0), not to the SDOF structural model error.
claims.quote: As expected, the vented gas deflagration is most sensitive to the laminar burning velocity (Su). The deflagration is also very sensitive to the parameters describing turbulent burning acceleration, β and R0.

claims.claim: LFP cylindrical cells in thermal runaway produced only 0.12 liters of gas per Watt-hour of nominal energy storage capacity, significantly lower than the 0.35 L/Wh for LCO 10 Ah pouch cells and 0.64 L/Wh for the 94 Ah NMC prismatic cell.
claims.quote: The LFP cells produced only produced 0.12 liters of gas at STP for every Watt-hour of nominal energy storage capacity. This is significantly lower than the 0.35 L/Wh for the 10 Ah pouch cell arrays and the 0.64 L/Wh produced by the 94 Ah prismatic NMC cell.

claims.claim: It may be incorrect to apply single cell experiment gas species data to predict the response of arrays of cells because the thermal runaway process and gas species produced are likely different for cells failing in propagation versus slowly heated single cells.
claims.quote: Since the timing is different and the damage pattern is different, it is likely that the species of the gases produced is also different. This is important to note for safety designs. It may not be conservative or appropriate to design systems to mitigate explosions using species data produced by single cell tests as the volume and species produced by an array of cells is likely different.

claims.claim: In the Surprise, Arizona ESS explosion, the opening of the ESS building door by firefighters introduced air into an oxygen-limited flammable gas mixture, causing a deflagration that threw one firefighter 73 feet and another 30 feet.
claims.quote: The door was opened to the ESS at 20:00:54. A deflagration occurred which firefighters described as a loud noise and a jet of flame extending 75 ft outward and 20 ft vertically from the open door. During this event, the two firefighters by the door were thrown from their positions. One firefighter went through a chainlink fence and ended up in a bush 73 ft from the door.

claims.claim: The NCM811 prismatic cell (62 Ah) discharged 4.83 mol of combustible gas during thermal runaway, with a gas generation rate of 0.0779 mol/Ah.
claims.quote: By calculating the difference in gas volume before and after the experiment, the amount of gas is determined to be 4.83 mol. The gas generation of this battery is 0.0779 mol/Ah.

claims.claim: The main gas components vented from the NCM811 battery during thermal runaway are CO2 (28.6%), CO (27.9%), H2 (20.5%), C2H4 (12.5%), and CH4 (5.5%), with approximately 68.1% of the venting gas being flammable.
claims.quote: the main components of the venting gas of NCM811 battery in the order of high to low are: CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4, O2. About 68.1 % of the venting gas is flammable, which has potential combustion risk.

claims.claim: The area 1 m above the safety valve exhibits the highest combustion risk and greatest explosive power during NCM811 battery thermal runaway venting.
claims.quote: The highest combustion risk is located 1 m above the safety valve, which is also the area of greatest gas explosion power.

claims.claim: Near the safety valve outlet, the combustible gas concentration exceeds the upper explosive limit (UEL), resulting in a low combustion risk index (Erisk = 0) at that location.
claims.quote: At the battery safety valve, the flammable gas concentration exceeds the UEL with a risk index of 0. Differential diffusion of gas concentrations on either side of the safety valve creates an ignition risk zone.

claims.claim: The safety valve of the NCM811 battery opens at a threshold pressure of 326 kPa, and the rapid internal gas pressure rise from 220 kPa to 340 kPa just before rupture is attributed to large amounts of gas generated during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The pressure threshold of the safety valve is 326 kPa... just before the safety valve bursts, there is a rapid increase in internal gas pressure from 220 kPa to 340 kPa. Subsequently, the safety valve ruptures, leading to a TR venting. Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that the rupture of the NCM battery safety valve is mainly caused by the large amount of gas generated during the moment of TR in the battery.

claims.claim: Gas combustion or explosion occurred during 19.6% of the total venting time in the NCM811 battery thermal runaway experiment, and combustion mainly occurred within 3 s after the safety valve opened.
claims.quote: The total time is 15 s. The time for combustion or explosion is 2.94 s. The proportion of time for explosion occurrence is 19.6 %. Furthermore, it can be observed that combustion mainly occurs within 3 s after the safety valve is opened.

claims.claim: As nickel content increases in NCM battery cathodes, the T3 (maximum thermal runaway temperature) increases, with the T3 of NCM811 being 133°C higher than that of NCM523.
claims.quote: the T3 temperature also rises with an increase in nickel content in the cathode. The primary reason for this is that as the nickel content increases, the energy density of the battery also increases, leading to more heat released during thermal runaway, which results in a higher T3 temperature. The T3 temperature of NCM811 is 133°C higher than that of NCM523.

claims.claim: Higher nickel content in NCM batteries leads to increased gas generation rate: NCM811 generates gas at 0.0779 mol/Ah, NCM622 at 0.0706 mol/Ah, and NCM523 at 0.0658 mol/Ah.
claims.quote: the gas generation rates for NCM811, NCM622, and NCM523 are 0.0779 Ah/mol, 0.0706 Ah/mol, and 0.0658 Ah/mol, respectively. The gas generation rate of NCM622 is 7.29 % higher than that of NCM523. Compared to NCM622, the gas generation rate of NCM811 is increased by 10.34 %.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway mass loss of the NCM811 battery (465.8 g total) is distributed as 13.2% gas, 36.3% particulate matter, and 50.5% debris.
claims.quote: TR venting resulted in a mass loss of 465.8 g... the proportion of the gas, particulate matter and debris mass is 13.2 %, 36.3 % and 50.5 %, respectively.

claims.claim: As jet velocity of the battery decreases, the risk of combustible gas explosion and combustion at the safety valve opening increases, with the highest risk period occurring during the initial and final stages of the jet.
claims.quote: As the jet velocity of the battery decreases the risk of combustible gas explosion and combustion at the safety valve opening increases. The period with the highest risk of jet-induced explosion and combustion occurs during the initial and final stages of the jet.

claims.claim: In lithium-ion batteries, the SEI membrane decomposes in the temperature range of 70°C to 90°C, releasing gases such as CO2 and C2H4.
claims.quote: Initially, in the temperature ranging from 70°C to 90°C, the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) membrane near the anode side of the lithium battery decomposes, releasing gases such as CO2, C2H4, etc.

claims.claim: When battery temperature reaches approximately 170°C, the cathode and electrolyte undergo disproportionation and decomposition reactions, releasing O2 and a large amount of heat.
claims.quote: When the battery temperature reaches about 170°C, the cathode and the electrolyte undergo disproportionation and decomposition reactions, continuing to release O2 and a large amount of heat.

claims.claim: The cathode-electrolyte reactions and separator destruction stages are considered the most intense reactions during lithium-ion battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: These two stages are considered to be the most intense reactions during the thermal runaway process of the battery.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, the final stage involves reactions between electrodes and binder, releasing a large amount of H2.
claims.quote: Finally, reactions occur between the electrodes and the binder, releasing a large amount of H2.

claims.claim: For thermal runaways induced by thermal abuse, a 3.2 Ah NCM lithium battery produces approximately three times the volume of gases as a 3.8 Ah lithium iron phosphate battery.
claims.quote: For thermal runaways induced by thermal abuse, a nickel cobalt manganese lithium battery with a capacity of 3.2 Ah produces approximately three times the volume of gases as a lithium iron phosphate battery with a capacity of 3.8 Ah.

claims.claim: Within the capacity range of up to 4 Ah for lithium battery thermal runaway, the total volume of gases produced generally ranges from 1 to 10 L.
claims.quote: Overall, within the capacity range of up to 4 Ah for lithium battery thermal runaway, the total volume of gases produced generally ranges from 1 to 10 L From 1 to 10 L.

claims.claim: Without controlling for other conditions, there is no evident positive correlation between battery capacity and the volume of gases produced during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: According to Figure 3, without controlling for other conditions, there is no evident positive correlation between the capacity of a battery and the volume of gases it produces during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Lithium cobalt oxide batteries with a capacity of 1 Ah and lithium iron phosphate batteries with a capacity of 1.1 Ah have a 100-fold difference in the volume of gases produced during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Lithium cobalt oxide batteries with a capacity of 1 Ah and lithium iron phosphate batteries with a capacity of 1.1 Ah have a 100-fold difference in the volume of gases produced during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: CO2 and CO are the most commonly mentioned gases in battery thermal runaway literature, with mention rates of 90.5% and 85.7% respectively.
claims.quote: In the literature on battery thermal runaway, CO2 and CO are the most commonly mentioned gases, with mention rates of 90.5% and 85.7%, respectively.

claims.claim: Gas sensor-based detection reacts more quickly than surface temperature sensing for early warning of lithium-ion battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Compared to surface temperature sensing, gas sensors react more quickly.

claims.claim: Temperature signal monitoring for battery thermal runaway may have time delays and measurement errors due to thermal conduction inside and outside the battery.
claims.quote: However, due to thermal conduction inside and outside the battery, temperature monitoring may have time delays and measurement errors.

claims.claim: FTIR analysis revealed that a large amount of carbonate esters were ejected before a lithium-ion battery entered thermal runaway, and these gases could ignite upon cooling, transmitting heat and causing adjacent batteries to also enter thermal runaway.
claims.quote: They revealed that a large amount of carbonate esters were ejected before the battery entered thermal runaway, and these gases could ignite upon cooling, transmitting heat and causing adjacent batteries to also enter a state of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: A non-dispersive infrared gas sensor successfully detected CO2 concentrations over 30,000 ppm during overcharge experiments that led to battery venting.
claims.quote: Cai and others conducted overcharge experiments that led to battery venting, successfully detecting CO2 concentrations over 30,000 ppm using a non-dispersive infrared gas sensor kit.

claims.claim: MOS gas sensors could detect increases in VOC concentration even before the battery fails completely, providing earlier warnings for timely intervention.
claims.quote: The study results showed that these gas sensors could detect increases in VOC concentration even before the battery fails completely, providing earlier warnings for timely intervention.

claims.claim: Gases generated after the first charge in lithium-ion batteries may gradually be reabsorbed or consumed, complicating in situ monitoring of gas evolution.
claims.quote: In situ monitoring of gas evolution is complicated by secondary reactions: gases generated after the first charge may gradually be reabsorbed or consumed.

claims.claim: New battery chemistries such as sodium-ion or all-solid-state systems may release different gases (e.g., SO2) that are not always captured by instruments focused on CO2 or H2.
claims.quote: Additional complexities arise with new battery chemistries—such as sodium-ion or all-solid-state systems—which may release different gases (e.g., SO2) that are not always captured by instruments focused on CO2 or H2.

claims.claim: Fiber-optic systems maintain functionality from cryogenic temperatures to 800°C due to their inorganic, dielectric construction, unlike electrochemical sensors which suffer from electrolyte degradation at high temperatures.
claims.quote: Unlike electrochemical sensors, which suffer from electrolyte degradation at high temperatures and humidity fluctuations, fiber-optic systems maintain functionality from cryogenic temperatures to 800°C due to their inorganic, dielectric construction.

claims.claim: A polymer-coated Fiber Bragg Grating CO2 sensor exhibited a detection limit for CO2 of approximately 0.78%, with minimum response and recovery times of 3.27 and 3.98 minutes respectively.
claims.quote: This FBG sensor exhibits good sensitivity, with a detection limit for CO2 of approximately 0.78%. Additionally, yielding minimum response and recovery times of 3.27 and 3.98 min.

claims.claim: In a polymer-coated FBG sensor, the maximum shift of the Bragg wavelength is positively correlated with coating thickness and negatively correlated with temperature.
claims.quote: They found that the maximum shift of the Bragg wavelength is positively correlated with the coating thickness and negatively correlated with temperature.

claims.claim: An MOF thin film deposited on a 5 cm single-mode optical fiber achieved an ultra-low CO2 detection limit of less than 20 ppm at a wavelength of 1.57 μm.
claims.quote: The study in Figure 10 achieved an ultra-low detection limit of less than 20 ppm for CO2, at a wavelength of 1.57 μm by depositing a Cu-benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (BTC) MOF thin film on a single-mode optical fiber with a length of only 5 cm.

claims.claim: The absorption peak intensity of hydrogen in the near-infrared region is weak, making it unsuitable for concentration measurement using direct absorption methods.
claims.quote: The absorption peak intensity of hydrogen in the near-infrared region is weak, making it unsuitable for concentration measurement using direct absorption methods.

claims.claim: A Fabry-Perot interferometer using palladium alloy as sensing material achieved a hydrogen detection limit of 0.25% at room temperature.
claims.quote: This approach enables reproducible and reversible measurement of hydrogen concentrations, achieving a detection limit of 0.25% at room temperature.

claims.claim: A hollow-core photonic band-gap fiber methane sensor using approximately 2 mW laser and 0.45 m HC-PBF achieved a detection limit of approximately 4.35 ppm with 0.5 s averaging time and a minimum detection limit of approximately 360 ppb with 75 s averaging time.
claims.quote: the optimized sensor, employing an approximately 2 mW laser and a 0.45 m HC-PBF, achieved a detection limit of approximately 4.35 ppm with a 0.5 s averaging time, and a minimum detection limit of approximately 360 ppb with a 75 s averaging time.

claims.claim: Electrochemical sensors suffer from short shelf lives of 6–12 months and progressive electrolyte depletion, necessitating frequent recalibration.
claims.quote: Electrochemical sensors suffer from short shelf lives (6–12 months) and progressive electrolyte depletion, necessitating frequent recalibration.

claims.claim: A single-channel fiber-optic CO2 sensor may cost 3–5 times more than an equivalent NDIR unit.
claims.quote: A single-channel fiber-optic CO2 sensor may cost 3–5× more than an equivalent NDIR unit.

claims.claim: NDIR sensors are highly specific for gases with strong IR absorption (e.g., CO2, CH4) but fail entirely for diatomic molecules like O2 and N2.
claims.quote: NDIR sensors are highly specific for gases with strong IR absorption (e.g., CO2, CH4) fail entirely for diatomic molecules like O2 and N2.

claims.claim: Semiconductor gas sensors with rapid response times of seconds to minutes and low detection thresholds of ppb to ppm outperform electrolyte-based indicators in early-stage thermal runaway detection capability.
claims.quote: Teng et al. highlight specific semiconductor sensors with rapid response times (seconds to minutes) and low detection thresholds (ppb to ppm), which outperform electrolyte-based indicators in early-stage detection capability.

claims.claim: Existing standards like UL 2580 and IEC 62619 primarily address traditional detection methods, lacking specific guidelines for novel optical sensors for lithium-ion battery thermal runaway monitoring.
claims.quote: existing standards like UL 2580 and IEC 62619 primarily address traditional detection methods, lacking specific guidelines for novel optical sensors.

claims.claim: The release of gases during LIB thermal runaway occurs earlier than the observation of obvious smoke and fire, enabling early-stage detection before visible failure.
claims.quote: Because the release of gases is earlier [16], [17] than the observation of obvious smoke and fire, the detection of thermal runaway gases in LIB can prevent the propagation of thermal runaway in the early stage of failure, providing sufficient reaction time for equipment shutdown and personnel evacuation.

claims.claim: Among LIB thermal runaway gas products, H2 and CH4 are classified as typical explosive gases, while CO is classified as the representative toxic gas.
claims.quote: Among them, H2 and CH4 are the typical explosive gases, while CO is the representative of toxic gases.

claims.claim: Gas chromatography combined with mass spectrometry (GC-MS) requires continuous sampling from the cell, which may affect the composition of gases, and is mostly used for analysis of final gas products rather than in situ analysis during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: continuous sampling from the cell is needed [5], which may affect the composition of gases. Moreover, its devices are bulky, especially time-consuming, which is mostly used for the analysis of the final gas products but not suitable for in situ analysis of battery condition during the thermal runaway process.

claims.claim: FTIR spectroscopy is difficult to analyze trace homonuclear diatomic gases such as oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen, which are among the thermal runaway gas products.
claims.quote: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) [23] is another commonly used analytical method, but it is difficult to analyze trace homonuclear diatomic gases such as oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen.

claims.claim: The HC-ARF-based Raman sensing device with micromirror achieves LODs of 0.8, 3.0, 1.4, 1.5, 9.2, 4.2, and 4.1 ppm·bar for CH4, C2H6, C2H4, C2H2, CO, CO2, and H2 respectively, at 200 mW excitation power and 60-s integration time.
claims.quote: With 200-mW excitation power and 60-s integration time, a low limit of detection (LOD) is achieved: 0.8, 3.0, 1.4, 1.5, 9.2, 4.2, and 4.1 ppm·bar for CH4, C2H6, C2H4, C2H2, CO, CO2, and H2, respectively

claims.claim: Attaching a micromirror to the rear of the HC-ARF provides a 3.15-fold Raman signal enhancement and a 2.66-fold SNR enhancement compared to a conventional bare fiber system.
claims.quote: Compared with a conventional bare fiber system, an enhancement factor of 3.15 and 2.66 is obtained for Raman signal and SNR, respectively

claims.claim: CO2 detection at near-LOD concentration (4.2 ppm) is not achievable at 1-bar pressure due to a silica fiber background Raman signal at 1387 cm−1 coupling with the CO2 characteristic peak at 1388 cm−1, but detection becomes possible when total pressure is increased to 2 bar.
claims.quote: The reason why CO2 is not detected is that the silicon material has a background Raman signal at 1387 cm−1, and this will couple with the characteristic peak of CO2 (1388 cm−1), which has a greater impact on low-concentration CO2 sensing. The Raman peak of CO2 is shown when the total pressure increases to 2 bar

claims.claim: H2 detection can serve as an indirect method to detect early trace lithium dendrite growth, which is the main cause of separator piercing and cell shorts.
claims.quote: the detection of H2 can also be used as an indirect method to detect early trace lithium dendrite growth, which is the main cause of separator piercing and cell shorts [16].

claims.claim: For NMC cells at 100% SOC, peak particle number levels were approximately 2.5 × 10+09 particles/cc and black carbon levels were approximately 60 mg/m3.
claims.quote: For NMCs at 100% SOC, peak PN levels were ∼2.5 × 10+09 particles/cc (part/cc), and black carbon levels were ∼60 mg/m3.

claims.claim: For LFP cells at 100% SOC, peak particle number levels were approximately 9.0 × 10+08 particles/cc and black carbon levels were 2.5 mg/m3.
claims.quote: For LFPs at 100% SOC, peak PN levels were ∼9.0 × 10+08 part/cc, and black carbon levels were 2.5 mg/m3.

claims.claim: NMC cells exhibited much higher particulate emissions than LFP cells at each SOC tested.
claims.quote: In general, much higher particulate emissions were observed for NMCs compared to LFPs at each SOC.

claims.claim: LFP cells exhibited a high level of variability in particulate emissions at a specific SOC, while NMC cells exhibited relatively less variability.
claims.quote: A high level of variability was observed for a speciﬁc SOC for LFP cells, while NMCs exhibited relatively less variability.

claims.claim: Peak particulate emission levels from thermal runaway were higher at higher states of charge for both cell chemistries.
claims.quote: Failure of a single cell led to a signiﬁcant release of particulate emissions, with peak emission levels being higher at the higher SOCs.

claims.claim: Neither LFP nor NMC cells at 10% SOC yielded any black carbon emissions, as the cells did not experience violent venting or thermal runaway.
claims.quote: 10% SOC tests did not yield any black carbon emissions for both cell chemistries as the cells did not experience violent venting or thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Peak particle number concentrations during NMC tests were of the order of 2 × 10+9 part/cc, which is 6 orders of magnitude higher than ambient laboratory PN levels of 2000–4000 part/cc.
claims.quote: Peak levels observed during the NMC tests were of the order of 2 × 10+9 part/cc, which is 6 orders of magnitude higher than ambient levels.

claims.claim: Particulate emissions from fine particle measurements during thermal runaway of LFP and NMC modules were 5–6 orders of magnitude higher than those observed in the exhaust of a contemporary heavy-duty diesel engine.
claims.quote: their ﬁndings revealed emission rates that were 5–6 orders of magnitude higher than those observed in the exhaust of a contemporary heavy-duty diesel engine.

claims.claim: High-speed, high-temperature particles emitted during thermal runaway can potentially ignite flammable gases also released during the event, further exacerbating the thermal runaway process.
claims.quote: these particles can include high-speed, high-temperature particles that can potentially ignite the ﬂammable gases that are also released, further exacerbating the thermal runaway process

claims.claim: For both cell chemistries, a decrease in SOC did not dramatically change the particle size signature but instead resulted in changes in peak emission levels.
claims.quote: for both cell chemistries, a decrease in SOC did not dramatically change the size signature, but instead, resulted in changes in peak levels.

claims.claim: Particulates emitted from cell failure were well within the human respirable size range, with a large fraction in the ultrafine size range below 100 nm.
claims.quote: One signiﬁcant conclusion that can be drawn is the fact that the particulates emitted from cell failure were well within the human respirable size range. Further, high levels of emissions were also observed in the ultraﬁne particulate size range. These are deﬁned as sub-100-nm particles and are known to be more toxic to human health as they tend to penetrate deeper into the human respiratory system and eventually diffuse into the bloodstream and reach various organs.

claims.claim: For NMC cells at 100% SOC, average peak temperatures exceeded the thermocouple measurement limit as the thermocouples were destroyed in the fire, while fire was only observed during 100% SOC NMC tests.
claims.quote: Although peak temperatures observed for the NMC cells were much higher, ﬁre was only observed during the 100% SOC tests. At 100% SOC, the thermocouples were destroyed in the ﬁre for the NMC cells, therefore showing temperatures peaking out at the thermocouple limit.

claims.claim: The abuse protocol applied external heating at a rate of 10 °C/min until 220 °C, after which temperature was held at 220 °C until thermal runaway occurred.
claims.quote: External heating was used as the abuse mechanism, where the test article was heated at a rate of 10 °C/min until the maximum rated temperature of the heater pads was reached (220 °C). Once the peak temperature was achieved, the temperature was held at 220 °C until thermal runaway occurred.

claims.claim: In multi-cell modules and packs, particulate emissions from thermal runaway could scale dramatically if propagation of thermal runaway and failure occurs across multiple cells.
claims.quote: It is to be noted that these high PN emissions were from the thermal runaway of a single cell. In real-world applications involving modules and packs composed of multiple cells, these emissions could scale dramatically if propagation of thermal runaway and failure occurs.

claims.claim: The study's results are specific to the cell design, size, and chemistry studied and are not intended as a direct comparison between the two chemistries.
claims.quote: Cells from two different cathode chemistries were studied for pure scientiﬁc data gathering and not for comparison between the two chemistries. The results obtained are speciﬁc to the cell design, size, and chemistry studied.

claims.claim: In a N2 atmosphere, TR onset for 21700 LIBs occurred at 328 seconds, compared to 267 seconds in air and 267.3 seconds in hypoxic (11% O2) conditions, all at 100% SoC.
claims.quote: In Case 2, the TR onset was significantly delayed, occurring at 328 seconds, compared to 267 seconds in Case 1 and 267.3 seconds in Case 3.

claims.claim: The maximum battery surface temperature in the N2 atmosphere (Case 2) reached 381.4°C, compared to 284.2°C in both air (Case 1) and hypoxic (Case 3) atmospheres, for 21700 LIBs at 100% SoC.
claims.quote: the battery surface temperature in the N₂ atmosphere reached a higher maximum (381.4 °C) compared to 284.2 °C in both the air and hypoxic atmospheres.

claims.claim: The maximum pressure during TR in air and hypoxic (11% O2) environments was 2.85 bar, whereas the N2 atmosphere produced a lower peak pressure of 1.55 bar, for 21700 LIBs at 100% SoC.
claims.quote: The maximum pressure reached during TR in both the air and hypoxic environments was 2.85 bar, whereas the N₂ atmosphere exhibited a lower peak pressure of 1.55 bar.

claims.claim: Total gas volume produced during TR of 21700 LIBs at 100% SoC was 1.48 L in air, 3.77 L in N2, and 1.98 L in hypoxic (11% O2) atmosphere.
claims.quote: Table 2 shows the gas volumes generated for each atmosphere: 1.48 L for Case 1, 3.77 L for Case 2, and 1.98 L for Case 3.

claims.claim: Hydrogen gas (H2) was absent in the air atmosphere during TR of 21700 LIBs because available oxygen fully consumed hydrogen during combustion, whereas hydrogen was detected at 0.66% in N2 and 0.28% in hypoxic (11% O2) conditions.
claims.quote: This explains the absence of hydrogen (H₂) in Case 1, as the available oxygen fully consumes the hydrogen during combustion... hydrogen (0.66%)... hydrogen (0.28%).

claims.claim: The LEL and UEL of gases emitted during TR of 21700 LIBs at 100% SoC were: 2.77% and 14.81% in air; 9.76% and 33.69% in N2; and 2.95% and 15.77% in hypoxic (11% O2) atmosphere.
claims.quote: Case 1 shows an LEL of 2.77% and a UEL of 14.81%... Case 2 presents significantly higher LEL and UEL values, with an LEL of 9.76% and a UEL of 33.69%... Case 3 has an LEL of 2.95% and a UEL of 15.77%.

claims.claim: Total heat release (THR) during TR of 21700 LIBs at 100% SoC was lowest in air (2.19 kJ), highest in N2 (3.42 kJ), and intermediate in hypoxic 11% O2 conditions (2.86 kJ).
claims.quote: The THR values for the three cases are summarized in Table 4... 2.19 [kJ]... 3.42 [kJ]... 2.86 [kJ].

claims.claim: In a N2 atmosphere, suppression of combustion allows continuous thermal decomposition, which results in higher heat release than in air despite the absence of oxygen-supported combustion.
claims.quote: In Case 2, the THR was 3.42 kJ, higher than in the air atmosphere. This can be attributed to the lack of oxygen, which suppresses combustion but allows for continuous thermal decomposition.

claims.claim: Post-TR battery mass was lowest in air (13.53 g), intermediate in hypoxic atmosphere (13.75 g), and highest in N2 (14.39 g), indicating that oxygen-containing environments cause greater material loss due to more complete combustion.
claims.quote: the post-test battery weight being lowest in the air atmosphere (13.53 g), slightly higher in the hypoxic atmosphere (13.75 g), and highest in the N₂ atmosphere (14.39 g). The greater material loss in the oxygen-containing environments reflects the more complete combustion that occurs.

claims.claim: N2-enriched environments used in aircraft cargo holds, while limiting combustion, could still result in significant heat release from thermal decomposition, posing TR risks during LIB transport.
claims.quote: these findings are particularly relevant for aviation safety, as they suggest that N₂-enriched environments, such as those used in aircraft cargo holds, while limiting combustion, could still result in significant heat release from thermal decomposition.

claims.claim: CO2 and CO were generated the most during thermal runaway in all lithium-ion batteries tested, regardless of cathode material or cell format.
claims.quote: In the experiment, CO2 and CO were generated the most during thermal runaway in all lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway, CO2 increased and CO decreased in prismatic and pouch-type batteries, whereas both CO2 and CO increased in cylindrical-type batteries.
claims.quote: Thereafter, CO2 increased, and CO decreased in the prismatic and pouch types, and both CO2 and CO increased in the cylindrical type.

claims.claim: HF and H2 concentrations generated during lithium-ion battery thermal runaway are inversely proportional to each other, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of -0.886.
claims.quote: HF와 H2 농도는 반비례 관계로 나타났으며... Pearson의 상관계수는 –0.886이 도출되어 강력한 음(-)의 상관이 있음을 통계적으로도 추론할 수 있었다.

claims.claim: Among cathode materials tested, HF generation during thermal runaway follows the order LFP > NCA > NCM, while H2 generation follows the reverse order NCM > NCA > LFP.
claims.quote: 양극재를 기준으로 HF가 많이 발생되는 순서는 LFP > NCA > NCM이며, H2는 이와 반대의 순서로 많이 발생 되었다.

claims.claim: At temperatures above 260°C, PVDF reacting with lithium-intercalated carbon anodes generates both HF and H2 via two competing reaction pathways, such that greater HF generation corresponds to lesser H2 generation.
claims.quote: 온도 260 ℃ 이상 등 특정 조건에서, 탄소 음극에 리튬이온이 삽입된 상태로 PVDF (-CH2-CF2-)가 반응하게 되면 HF뿐만 아니라, H2가 다음과 같이 발생 된다... HF 가스 발생이 많으면 수소 발생량이 적음을 위 화학식으로 유추해 볼 수 있다.

claims.claim: Pouch-type lithium-ion batteries undergo thermal runaway earliest regardless of cathode material type, while prismatic and cylindrical types undergo thermal runaway later, attributed to smaller heat-receiving contact area with the heating plate.
claims.quote: 양극재 종류와 관계없이 전반적으로 파우치형에서 열폭주가 가장 일찍 발생하고, 각형(prismatic)과 원통형(cylindrical)에서 열폭주가 늦게 발생되는 것을 확인할 수 있었다. 단, 파우치형은 한쪽 면 전체가 열판과 접촉하지만, 각형 및 원통형은 아랫면만 열판에 직접 닿는 형태로 수열 면적이 파우치형보다 적은 영향이 있을 것으로 보인다.

claims.claim: LFP pouch-type batteries exhibited thermal runaway 7 to 147 seconds later than NCM pouch-type batteries, providing experimental evidence of greater thermal stability of olivine-structure LFP cathode material.
claims.quote: LFP 계열 배터리의 열폭주는 NCM 계열 배터리보다 적게는 7 s에서 많게는 147 s 가량 늦게 발생하였다. 이러한 실험 결과로 올리빈계 양극재인 LFP가 NCM보다 안정성을 나타내고 P=O 공유결합의 결합력 등 효과를 실험적으로 증명해볼 수 있었다.

claims.claim: For the NCM prismatic battery, the vent gas was released approximately 2,400 seconds after experiment start, followed by thermal runaway approximately 7 seconds later, with the vent temperature reaching approximately 540°C and hydrogen concentration reaching approximately 3% at the point of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: 실험 시작 후 약 2,400 s가 경과한 시점에 열폭주 이전에 배출변에서 벤트 가스를 분출하였다. 가스가 분출되고 약 7 s 후 열폭주가 발생되었다. 열폭주 발생 시 급격한 화염 발생과 함께 배출변의 온도는 급격하게 상승하여 약 540 ℃까지 도달하였고, 이 지점에서 수소 농도 또한 급격하게 증가하여 약 3%에 도달하였다.

claims.claim: Battery capacity (Ah) was found to have no correlation with vent occurrence time or thermal runaway occurrence time.
claims.quote: 실험을 통하여 용량 (Ah)과 벤트 및 열폭주 발생 시간과는 상관관계가 없음을 확인할 수 있었다.

claims.claim: Overcharging beyond SOC 140% can cause thermal runaway, while SOC exceeding 130% results in significant capacity loss that can directly affect battery life.
claims.quote: State of charge (SOC) 130% 초과 시 용량 손실이 크고 이는 배터리 수명에 직결될 수 있으며, SOC 140%가 초과될 경우에는 열폭주가 발생될 수 있다.
